## PREFACE

In this Report for the academic year 1957-8, Dean Woodside has stressed the measures being taken to prepare for the sudden rise in student enrolment. At no time in their histories have universities been more conscious of the future. It is no longer sufficient to find a solution for immediate problems. It is not, for instance, simply a question of finding sufficient staff to meet the needs of the next academic year; it is rather a question of building a staff that will be able to cope with the conditions that will appear in five or six years' time. New buildings cannot be looked upon simply as extensions of the present plant; they take their place in a new pattern of development. In everything that we do, the long-term objective dwarfs the immediate need.

The kind of extensive and detailed long-range planning that is going on at the University of Toronto—and, indeed, at all Canadian universities—makes heavy demands upon the administration, not only on the full-time administrators but even more on the many members of staff who participate in discussions and planning. But if the new demands of administration have made inroads into time which is needed for teaching and research, they have none the less brought to the campus a sense of co-operative venture that is exhilarating. Now that, after long debate, Toronto's role in expansion has been clearly defined, staff and administration are united in one purpose: to see that expansion becomes primarily a means of strengthening academic standards.

On returning to my Alma Mater, I am deeply conscious of a sense of adventurous reappraisal. The change from the youngest and the smallest of Ontario universities to the oldest and the largest has not involved a difficult transition. It is as if I had moved from work on a new section of the main structure to work on the main structure itself. The nurturing of the new, and the development and strengthening of the old, belong to the same process, and one must not be neglected at the expense of the other. And yet it is not unfair to say that in the preparation for the new era everything must ultimately depend upon the strength of the old, established universities and on their power to preserve the highest standards. Never before in its history has Toronto occupied a more exacting and strategic position among Canadian universities.

Ever since I took up my duties here, I have been aware of the warm support of my colleagues, and in this first official publication I take the opportunity to record my gratitude for the reassuring way in which I have been received back into the Toronto academic community. In these opening months I am particularly conscious of my debt to my former colleague and predecessor, Dr. Sidney Smith—*il miglior fabbro*—and to Dean Woodside, under whose guidance during the past year the University has moved forward with tested self-confidence.

C. T. BISSELL

---

# REPORT OF THE ACTING PRESIDENT

*To the Governors and the Senate of the University of Toronto:*

On September 12, 1957, Dr. Sidney Smith resigned as President of the University. While his former colleagues consoled themselves with the thought that he was being elevated to a position of the greatest trust and responsibility in which his service to Canada would be, perhaps, more direct, there was still lively consciousness of the magnitude of the University's loss and grateful recognition of all that he had contributed over a period of twelve years by mind, personality and sheer toil to the welfare of a complex institution and of the society to which it belongs. Although this is not the place to catalogue even the major expressions of loss and gratitude, two events may be cited as symbols. The Association of the Teaching Staff arranged for an assembly of the staff of the University on the afternoon of January 11, 1958, at which an attempt was made to attribute to the Honourable Sidney Smith and Mrs. Smith what was their due, and for a reception on the evening of the same day where the numbers taxed the resources in space of the Royal Ontario Museum. On May 30, 1958, the Chancellor conferred on the Honourable Sidney Smith alone, the degree of Doctor of Laws, *honoris causa*.

For the second time in its long history, the University entered an interregnum on September 13, 1957. It is undoubtedly the duty of an Acting President to avoid innovation or change in policy, to maintain the momentum of the previous administration, to preserve the values of the past and to leave the future as clear as possible for the new administration. This might suggest that if an Acting President were truly effective, he would have no Annual Report to write. However a living university moves and changes and grows. Movement and growth depend on no individual; indeed it is beyond the power of any single person and beyond the influence of a mere interregnum to stop or to curb them. The present Report must be taken as a brief account of some major phases of the University's life during the academic year 1957-8.

## II

The greatest of all the problems facing the universities of our country—and others—at the present time is, as everybody knows, posed by the increase in numbers of those who are seeking admission as undergraduates. The significant increases which are now being experienced will shortly swell into a veritable flood. The problem is complex but there is no profit in rehearsing what every intelligent layman knows. Whatever view may be taken about the maximum size of a university or of a division of a university, whatever opinion may be held about qualification for admission to a university, there is no doubt that large additional facilities in the University of Toronto will be required, and that the staff must be greatly increased. It is hoped that a society which is now aware of the problems of its universities and which seems to understand that anything worth having must somehow be paid for will be willing through its governments, its corporations, its other organizations, and its private citizens to provide the necessary financial

---

means, and to recognize that while the problems cannot be solved without money, problems still remain after the money is at hand.

The enrolment at the University during the academic year now under review was 13,574 as opposed to 12,871 in the preceding year. The increase, of somewhat more than 5 per cent, is significant without being frightening and it corresponds fairly well with the prophecies of the statisticians. But while the flood has not yet descended upon the University, it is visible in the offing and the months of grace are short. Even now both planning for the future and administration in the present are made extraordinarily difficult by what seem to be incalculable shifts in the interests of students. Who could have guessed that enrolment in the Faculty of Arts would rise more than 10 per cent with a much greater increase in the first year, while enrolment in the Faculty of Applied Science and Engineering would rise only a little more than 3 per cent with a decrease in the first year? Within the complex Faculty of Arts who can foresee sudden fluctuations such as the doubling of numbers in one honour course? It would be foolish to suggest that there is no value in forecasts of total numbers. But the needs in academic staff, in equipment and other facilities, and in supplies, depend specifically on the divisions and the courses in which students enrol, and curves or other forms of extrapolation are not always reliable here. There is need for constant study of old and new evidence for enrolment, otherwise the University of the future will not be equipped to meet the needs of the future.

In his Annual Report for the year ended June, 1957, Dr. Smith referred to the establishment of the Advisory Planning Committee by the Board of Governors and gave more than an inkling of what that Committee's report would recommend. There is perhaps some value in pointing out the speed—but not the haste—with which planning for the future of the University has been conducted. The Committee of the Senate, generally known as the "Plateau Committee," was appointed on December 16, 1955, and made its report on June 5, 1956. In October, 1956, the Board of Governors appointed the Advisory Planning Committee. It is perhaps true to say that public opinion was not ready to receive any specific and realistic discussion of plans for University expansion involving considerable sums of money until about the time when the "Plateau Committee" of the Senate was established. At that date time was already short. It is worthy of note that within 16 months of the report of the "Plateau Committee" and within 12 months of the appointment by the Board of Governors of the Advisory Planning Committee, the large and detailed report of the latter committee was formally presented to the Board of Governors and adopted by the Board in principle. The University (and our society) is largely in the debt both of the Plateau Committee, which did the pioneering, and of the Advisory Planning Committee, which so effectively and quickly studied and organized a great volume of information and judgment and produced a clear-cut and imaginative programme for the University's expansion. Tribute must also be paid to the members of departments and other divisions of the University who conscientiously undertook the important but unattractive task of producing the detailed information on which the Advisory Planning Committee's report ultimately rests.

Since the Report of the Advisory Planning Committee has been made public there is no need to review its recommendations here. The best compliment which

---

can be paid to it is that in October, 1957, after it was released to the public, two or three senior administrative officers of the University were over a period of weeks subjected to a bombardment of questions by representatives of newspapers and other information agencies, members of the academic staff, graduates and undergraduates, and while the answers to some of the questions were not regarded as wholly satisfactory, no question was asked which had not been considered by the Committee. It must not be forgotten that the work of the Advisory Planning Committee would have been fanciful had not the Board of Governors with the support of the Provincial Government dramatically expropriated a large area of land west of St. George Street.

The plan for the future, recommended by the Advisory Planning Committee, is not static. While the Board of Governors adopted the plan in principle, it must approve each part of the plan in particular. Cognizance may and must thus be taken of changes of any sort which may occur as time advances. A Presidential Advisory Committee formally approved by the Board of Governors has been meeting regularly with the double purpose of considering, and making recommendations about, proposed revisions of the plans in the Advisory Planning Committee's report, and of recommending expedients for the solution of the University's immediate problems of space. The second of the Committee's two functions daily assumes greater significance. Numbers of staff and students are rising. Existing buildings must be demolished before larger and more efficient buildings may be constructed. It is certain that for a period of two or three years at least, ingenuity will be tested, and comfort and even efficiency will be restricted before the new University begins to arise.

During the academic year 1957-8 a significant beginning was made with the building programme. The new and handsome structure planned to house the offices and the bookstore of the University Press has been completed. The addition to the Archives Building which contains a Canadiana Gallery of the Royal Ontario Museum was opened in March by the Lieutenant-Governor of the Province in the presence of the donor, Dr. Sigmund Samuel. Large progress was made in the construction of new laboratory and storage buildings at the Dufferin Division of the Connaught Medical Research Laboratories. Contracts for the Women's Athletic Building (the first new building to be constructed on the "West Campus"), the new building for the Faculty of Dentistry and a new laboratory for the Institute of Aerophysics were let. Architects for a large Arts building on the "West Campus," and for a new Engineering Building, were appointed. Consideration was given to the construction (on the "West Campus") of a new building to house the shops, stores and offices of the Superintendent's Department; lengthy and continued consideration has been given to the possibility of a new building for the Royal Conservatory of Music. Committees of staff members were appointed to do the preliminary (and tentative) planning for a new Chemistry building, a new Physics building, a new Zoology building, new residences for University College and new residences for the other divisions of the University. While the Board of Governors must take specific action about each of these buildings before an architect may be appointed and tenders may be called for, the preliminary planning is going ahead. In the course of consideration of plans for the new Engineering building it was discovered that money could be saved,

---

better facilities for Engineering could be provided and Baldwin House could be at least temporarily preserved for use if the existing Forestry Building were moved some distance north. Decision to undertake this move was made before the end of the year. In general it may be stated that planning is more than well advanced, and that the new University is taking shape as rapidly as available funds will permit. At the same time every effort is being made to utilize to the full new properties acquired by expropriation on the "West Campus."

It will be urged that whereas buildings and equipment have great importance, the academic—and non-academic—staff are of much greater significance. With this no true academic would disagree. It must not be forgotten that at the time when the Board of Governors was expropriating the "West Campus" and making plans for new buildings, it was also being served by a special committee on "staff compensation." It would be wrong, if not sinful, to overlook the fact that the welfare and the excellence of the academic staff were being considered *pari passu* with the plans for new space and new buildings. In the spring of 1957 a new salary scale was adopted by the Board of Governors, the new minima for the various academic ranks to be reached over a period of three years. The first stage was adopted for the year 1957–8 and the second stage has been adopted in the budget for the year 1958–9. In these two years 80 per cent of the rise to the new salary floors has been accomplished. It is expected that the remainder of the programme will be achieved in the session 1959–60, and it seems reasonable that on these new salary terms the University will be able to compete without grave disadvantage with almost all other universities. Other aspects of "staff compensation" have not been neglected. Careful consideration is being given to improvements in such important items as pensions, group life insurance and assistance with housing problems.

In 1957–8, and again in 1958–9, a sum was provided in the budget for the acquisition of "additional academic staff." This was an example of imaginative policy on the part of the Board of Governors. Although it has become customary in casual parlance to refer to this policy as one of "stockpiling" for the future, it would not be wrong to say that in general it has made possible the remedying of existing deficiencies rather than provision for the future. In some divisions of the University classes have for years been far too large for genuine education, and the wishes of departments (based on legitimate needs) have been frustrated by the exigencies of the annual budget.

While generous provision has been made for the existing academic staff and for additional academic staff, some comment should be made about matters which might easily be regarded as peripheral but which are in a sense central. The changes in salary scale are generous and the University community is truly grateful for them. The principle of one thing at a time is not open to objection. But for a scholar or a scientist supplies, apparatus and equipment (in which the resources of the Library may be included) are almost as important—for teaching and research—as salaries. Appropriations for these essentials have suffered as salaries have improved. Rates of pay for demonstrators, readers and assistants are below those in the larger American universities and in some Canadian universities. Furthermore, time is important. It has often been represented to me with some vigour that the University is making uneconomic use of its academic re-

---

sources by obliging members of the academic staff to undertake work which could be better done by secretaries and technicians. There is no doubt in my mind that the demand for secretarial and technical services is expanding rapidly. And it is difficult to refute the argument that over the past few years the expansion of these services has not kept pace with the expansion of the student body, the expansion of academic staff, and the expansion of demands made on the academic staff.

The rate of remuneration of non-academic staff has been under constant surveillance by the Personnel Officer of the University. The criterion is "community averages" in the different categories. During the year 1957-8 increases in salary rates for non-academic staff have been approved on the basis of "community averages."

### III

During the past year certain members of the academic staff have retired. Dr. A. C. Lewis, Dean of the Ontario College of Education, has retired after a life of effective service to education in Ontario as teacher, principal, dean, and contributor to the counsels of the University. Professor I. R. Pounder has retired as Professor and Chairman of the Department of Mathematics. He has been a great teacher in his discipline, a conscientious administrator and a statesman in the councils of the University. He is continuing for the coming year as Special Lecturer in Mathematics. Professor W. G. McIntosh has retired as Professor of Mechanical Engineering. He, too, will in the coming year be a Special Lecturer in his department, where he will continue to apply his wisdom to the planning for the expansion of the Faculty of Applied Science and Engineering and to maintain his interest in the establishment of new graduates. Generations of students will regret the retirement of F. R. Crocombe as Associate Professor in the Department of Political Economy. His devotion to his students has been returned by their devotion to him. Dr. G. V. Morton, Assistant Professor in the Faculty of Dentistry, has retired after 37 years of quiet, conscientious and single-purposed service to his Faculty and his students.

Eight deaths are recorded with sorrow and regret. Dr. Chester Martin, Professor Emeritus of History and former Head of the Department, who had retired in 1952, died in April, 1958. It is true to say that he was one of the pioneers in Canadian historiography, and with the late Professor George Wrong a founder of the University's school of history. Professor James H. White had retired from the staff of the Faculty of Forestry in 1946. He was the first graduate in that Faculty, and had made an outstanding contribution not only to the University but also to forestry in its wider aspects. Dr. G. C. Cameron, formerly Professor of Pathology and Bacteriology in the Faculty of Dentistry, died a little more than a year after his retirement in June, 1956. He was a medical doctor who devoted himself for a long time to the teaching of students of dentistry. Dr. C. W. J. Armstrong, Research Associate in the Connaught Medical Research Laboratories, suffered an untimely death early in the academic year. His skill at planning and carrying out meticulous and important work has put in his debt not only the Laboratories which he served but also countless people who did not know him. Dr. H. W. D. McCart was Assistant Professor in the Department of Otolaryngology and a member of the staff of the Toronto General Hospital. He was

---

both an excellent practitioner and an excellent teacher of his specialty. Miss F. M. Quinlan, Assistant Professor in the Department of Physics, was an able teacher who enjoyed unusual success in the populous elementary laboratories in her discipline. Her death will be especially lamented by generations of women students who readily turned to her for advice and help. Dr. N. M. Rockman, Assistant Professor in Dental Radiology, died all too young. As a result of almost overly-conscientious labours, he left, as one of his legacies to his University and his profession, well-developed plans for the area which his Department will occupy in the new Dentistry building. The services of T. A. Reed to the University are symbolized by the fact that the trophy awarded for the championship in the complex and extensive intramural athletic programme, which evokes universal admiration, is known and will be known as the T. A. Reed Trophy.

At a later place in this report there is a record of the new members who joined the staff of the University during the year, of promotions among the staff and of the honours which have been conferred on members of the staff. The section on Research gives a brief description of researches which are at present being carried on, and the section on Publications presents, in so far as a list of publications may do so, evidence of researches successfully completed.

#### IV

It would be easy—and probably fatal—for a University to yield to pressures which may be brought to bear from many sources and to establish a variety of courses for which there appears to be demand of some sort. It is a pleasure to report the establishment of certain new courses at the University because the nature of these courses, and the need for them, have been carefully if not rigorously examined at the levels of the Department, the Council and the Senate.

In the Faculty of Arts a three-year General Course in Science has been introduced after nearly three years of planning and critical consideration. Its main purpose is “to provide a broad but integrated education in natural science” without excluding a measure of work in the humanities and social sciences which, in the circumstances, is not illiberal. This course will, it is believed, meet a real need of the mid-twentieth century. Although the disappearance of the old honour course in English and History is still regretted by some, modifications in the existing honour course in English Language and Literature, and the establishment of a new honour course in Modern History (English Option), have gone far to compensate for the loss. It may be noted that courses in modern Arabic and undergraduate courses in Persian have been offered (and taken) for the first time during the year under review. A gap, the seriousness of which requires no comment in 1958, has thus been at least partially filled.

A Department of Medical Biophysics has been created by the Board of Governors in the Faculty of Medicine, and has already been recognized as a department in the School of Graduate Studies. This has in large measure been made possible by an agreement between the University and the Ontario Cancer Institute whereby the close co-operation of the two institutions in the area of interest which they have in common is encouraged. A new diploma, in Speech Pathology and Audiology, to be awarded on the successful completion of two

---

years of prescribed study normally at the postgraduate level, has been established. The courses of study leading to the diploma in Industrial Health (formerly known as the diploma in Industrial Hygiene) have been carefully and thoroughly revised. A new one-year postgraduate course in electro-encephalography has been authorized.

The urgent need for specialists in the important field of medical microbiology has been met potentially by provision for courses of postgraduate study in the School of Hygiene leading to a new diploma in Bacteriology.

V

Any attempt to list changes, improvements and additions to the equipment of the University for research and teaching would probably be undesirable here and in any case would involve a mere catalogue of many items. However three major and unusual accessions may be mentioned.

In June, the Prime Minister of Ontario formally declared in use the Sub Critical Atomic Reactor which had been built during the year in the laboratory of the Department of Chemical Engineering. It is "the first atomic energy facility to be installed in a Canadian university, and will provide the necessary equipment for instructing graduate engineers in this growing and important field."

At the David Dunlap Observatory the Department of Electrical Engineering has collaborated with the Department of Astronomy in the design and construction of two additional antennas for the observation of radio stars. More than a mere beginning has thus been made in the new and increasingly important area of research known as "radio astronomy" and it is hoped that the facilities available to the University's scientists will increase at a rate commensurate with the development of the new field's significance.

FERUT, the computing machine around which the Computation Centre has been built since 1952, was replaced after some years of valuable service by an International Business Machines Type 650 Electronic Data Processing Machine. The new machine provides somewhat greater facilities at somewhat less cost. If it is impossible to offer gratitude to a machine, even a complex electronic device, thanks may be expressed to those who were responsible for the installation of FERUT, a machine on which the University has done its own pioneering.

VI

Lectures other than those demanded by the normal procedures of teaching have been given both within the University and beyond its borders by many members of the University staff. Scholars and scientists from elsewhere have continued to visit the University as a province of the great commonwealth of learning and to make as individuals or as groups their own special contribution to the academic life of the University and of that commonwealth. A list appears elsewhere in this Report. It may perhaps not be invidious to make special reference to four events. The International Union of Geodesy and Geophysics met at the University late in the summer—and early in the International Geophysical Year. Scientists in large numbers from all parts of the world participated in the

---

meetings and the Prime Minister of Canada was present at the opening of the session. In October the University celebrated the fiftieth anniversary of the establishment of the Faculty of Forestry. More than half of all the graduates of the Faculty assembled for the occasion. It was marked by a dinner for the graduates, a special Convocation at which honorary degrees were conferred, and an "open house" within the Faculty of Forestry itself. The Department of French of University College conducted in April a successful Bilingual Seminar on French Canada. The discussions, participated in by specialists from both Ontario and Quebec, were held in public.

The Falconer Lectures on the topic "The Commonwealth Statesman in International Politics" were given, in January, by the Right Honourable the Viscount Kilmuir of Creich, Lord High Chancellor of Great Britain.

## VII

During the year the University has received gifts and grants totalling \$2,826,853.75. The significance of this is open to no doubt and the listing of the individual benefactions at a later place in this Report must be regarded as a record rather than as an adequate expression of the gratitude which the University feels. The material resources represented by the benefactions are of great importance; indeed it would be difficult if not impossible for the University to maintain its present programme without them. Nevertheless the effect of these benefactions both on the University and on researchers and teachers and students is larger than that which could be expected from the purchasing power of dollars and cents, important though that purchasing power certainly is. Gifts are an expression of faith in the University, of a high regard for the benefits which it is able to confer on individuals and through them on society. It would be quite impossible to measure the ultimate effect for good of encouragement to a student or of stimulus to a researcher. Yet everyone who has had an opportunity to observe knows that the profit to society may in the long run be incalculably large.

## VIII

In other parts of this Report, Deans, Directors and all others responsible for the welfare of the many divisions of the University examine the achievements of the year, and analyse the problems both of the present and of the future. Any failure to comment on any of those achievements and those problems here must not be taken as evidence that they lack importance. It is due rather to the belief that the achievements and the problems are most effectively presented by those to whom they are of most immediate concern. If any emphasis appears to have been laid on the material equipment of the University, that is only because the material is easier to assess in detail than the intellectual and the spiritual. A university is great only if its teaching and research staff is great and if its teachers and researchers are ably supported by all those upon whom the teachers and researchers inevitably depend.

Not often in the history of this University has the Annual Report been written by one who after enjoying thirty years of close connection with one large division

---

of the University (and thus acquiring an elementary understanding of the whole University) has suddenly been given an opportunity to observe every phase of its complex life at close quarters and from the inside, and who is inhibited neither by modesty about its achievements—for which he can claim no credit at all—nor by worry that a present optimism may in future—a future in which he will have no ultimate responsibility—appear to have been *hybris*. The vivid impression made upon me by an unusual experience of close to ten months is of a university full of vitality and vigour, conscious of its problems and shortcomings and assiduous in seeking the means to meet them, and eager to serve the community in the only way in which a university can truly serve—by academic teaching and research. The Board of Governors, the Senate and the administration in general recognize that the academic welfare of the University is the end to which all means must contribute. The supporting arms of the service are aware that it is their duty to support. The academic staff, on which everything depends, is excellent. I have no doubt that under a brilliant new President, the University as it grows larger will add immeasurably to its achievements and its glories.

The past year has bequeathed to me many happy memories. Of these the happiest are of the kindness, generosity and support accorded to me by the Chairman and the members of the Board of Governors, the members of the Senate, every member of the University staff—academic, administrative and non-academic—and those members of the student body with whom my office brought me in contact. I would be remiss if I did not refer specifically to the services of Dr. Frank Wetmore who with one day's notice cheerfully assumed the duties of the Dean of the Faculty of Arts, and carried them out carefully and with distinction. The most golden memory of all is of the loving-kindness (only the Scriptural word will serve) shown me by my academic colleagues and those members of the administrative staff with whom I have been in constant association. It would be idle to attempt to put my gratitude in words.

MOFFATT WOODSIDE  
*Acting President*

---

PAINTER*Office of the President**Acting President*—Dean M. ST. A. WOODSIDEHONORARY DEGREESThe following persons received the degree of Doctor of Laws, *honoris causa*:

At the Convocation of October 25, 1957:

JOSEPH AVILA BÉDARD  
 WALLACE ACHESON DELAHEY  
 JOHN MILES GIBSON  
 ROBERT WILLIAM LYONS  
 HARVEY REGINALD MACMILLAN  
 EDMUND JOHN ZAVITZ

At the Convocation of April 10, 1958:

MAURICE ROY

At the Convocation of May 30, 1958:

SIDNEY EARLE SMITH

At the Convocation of November 22, 1957, the degree of Doctor of Letters, *honoris causa*, was conferred on

ROBERT MORRISON MACIVER

ADDRESSES AND PAPERS GIVEN BY MEMBERS OF THE STAFF OUTSIDE THE UNIVERSITY

Professor J. H. ACLAND, an address to the joint conference of the American Institute of Architects and Association of Collegiate Schools of Architecture; on "The History of Urban Form" to the C.M.H.C. Conference on Urban Renewal.

Dr. D. ANDERSON, a paper to the Annual Meeting of the International Association for Dental Research.

Professor F. H. ANDERSON, on "Aesthetic Motivation in Aristotle's Philosophy" to the Pan-American Philosophical Association; on "Specialization as a Problematic Factor in the Organization of Honours Courses" to a universities conference at Boulder, Colorado.

Professor D. G. ANDREWS, on "Nuclear Reactors and Chemical Engineers" to the Chemical Institute of Canada.

Dr. A. ANTONI, a lecture to the Lambton County Dental Association.

Professor ERICH BAER, on "Synthesis of Unsaturated a,b-Diglycerides; D- and L-a,b-Diolein" to the Toronto Biochemical and Biophysical Society at Queen's University; on "Synthesis of Unsaturated a-Phosphatidic and a-Bisphosphatidic Acids" to the Canadian Federation of Biological Societies, Queen's University, and the Royal Military College.

Professor R. M. BAXTER, on "Vitamins and the Pharmacist" at two pharmaceutical seminars in Walkerton and Simcoe.

Professor GEORGE H. BEATON, a paper to the Canadian Physiological Society.

---

Professor W. A. BECKETT, on "The 1958 Economic Outlook" to the Toronto Chapter of the American Marketing Association; on "The Application of Computers to Canadian Business Forecasting" to the First Canadian Computer Conference.

Dr. C. H. BEDFORD, on "Dmitry Merezhkovsky, the Intelligentsia and the Revolution of 1905" and "The Dramatic Trilogy of Sukhovo-Kobylin" to the Canadian Association of Slavists.

Professor K. S. BERNHARDT, on "A Security Theory of Personality and Samples of Relevant Research" to the International Congress of Psychology in Brussels.

Dr. CHARLES H. BEST, on "Insulin" at St. George's Hospital Medical School, London; on "Comments on the Present Status of the Oral Hypoglycaemic Agents" to the British Diabetic Association, Oxford; on "Recent Work on Insulin and Glucagon" at Boston City Hospital; on "A Canadian Trail of Medical Research" and three other lectures at Washington University School of Medicine, St. Louis; on "Prophecies about Diabetes" to the Post-graduate Course of the American Diabetes Association, Atlanta; on "The Development, Physiological Actions and Clinical Use of Purified Heparin" at the New York Academy of Medicine; on "The Contributions of the Biological Sciences to Public Health in the Future" and other lectures, at Central University, Caracas; on "Oral Hypoglycaemic Agents" at the 5th International Congress of Internal Medicine; on "The Future in the Field of Diabetes" at the National Conference on Teaching and Research in Diabetes, and to the Royal Society of Medicine, London.

Professor P. P. BIRINGER, and Professor G. R. SLEMON, on "Three-Phase Induction Motor Control Using Static Frequency Doublers" to the American Institute of Electrical Engineers.

Professor V. W. BLADEN, an address to the President's Conference, Trans Canada Airlines; an address to a conference (Perspectives for Management) at Queen's University.

Dr. W. E. BLATZ, the Presidential Address to the Canadian Psychological Association.

Professor H. BOESCHENSTEIN, on "The Contemporary German Lyric" to the Humanities Association in Ottawa.

Professor P. H. BRIEGER, on "The Madonna with the Child and St. John in the Greene Collection and its Place in Renaissance Painting" at the College of Christ the King, University of Western Ontario; on "The Westminster Abbey of Henry III" at the National Gallery of Canada; on "The Theatre and the Arts in the Baroque Period" at the University of New Brunswick and Dalhousie University; on "Leonardo's Last Supper" at Acadia and McGill Universities, and the University of Montreal; on "Nicholas Poussin and the French Classical Tradition" at Mount Allison University; on "Early Illustrations of Dante's Divine Comedy" to the Dante Society.

Dr. R. C. BURGESS, a paper to the International Association for Dental Research.

Professor J. C. CAIRNS, on "The Taxis of the Marne and the Fall of France" to the Society for French Historical Studies.

Mr. W. H. CARD, on "Transistor-Oscillator Induction-Motor Drive" to the American Institute of Electrical Engineers.

Professor J. M. S. CARELESS, on "Conserving the Historical Resources of the Upper Great Lakes Basin" to the Northern Great Lakes Area Council; addresses at the unveiling of plaques erected by the Ontario Archaeological and Historical Sites Board; on "Canada in the 1850's" to the Hamilton Association in their Centennial Lecture Series; on "Byways of History in Old Ontario" to the Royal Canadian Institute; a series of lectures on "Aspects of Canadian History and Politics" to undergraduates and graduate students at the Universities of Sydney, New South Wales, Queensland, New England, Melbourne, Adelaide, Perth, and the Australian National University and Canberra College.

Miss HELEN CARPENTER, an address to the International Congress of Nurses in Rome.

Professor G. A. P. CARROTHERS, on "Population Projection by means of Income Potential Models" to the Economic and Allied Social Science Associations; on "Theories of Land Use and Economic Location" to the Advanced Staff Course of the Central Mortgage and Housing Corporation in Ottawa.

Professor W. E. CARSWELL, on "Colour Composition" to the Humber Valley Photographic Association.

Professor C. F. COMFORT, on "The Dutch Influence on English Painting" in the Art Gallery of Toronto series; on "The Academic Trend" at the opening of the Royal Academy of Arts Exhibition.

Professor J. B. CONACHER, on "Peel and the Peelites 1846-50" to the Anglo-American Conference of Historians; on "British Political Parties 1832-1867" to the Southampton Branch of the Historical Association.

Professor H. S. M. COXETER, a course of lectures on "Geometry" at the summer session of the University of Michigan; two addresses on "Subgroups of the Braid Group" to the Banff meeting of the Canadian Mathematical Congress; 42 lectures on nine topics such as "The Golden Section and Fibonacci Numbers" and "The Six-Color Theorem" at 18 universities and colleges in the western United States and Alaska.

Dr. G. B. CRAIG, a paper to the symposium on Defence Problems in Structures and Materials Research in Ottawa.

Professor D. G. CREIGHTON, on "The Making of a Biography" to the London Branch of the Humanities Association of Canada.

Professor D. F. DADSON, an address at the annual meeting of the Rural and Urban Trustees

---

Association; the commencement address at Forest Hill Collegiate Institute; on "Modern Education" to the Engineering Institute of Canada, Niagara Peninsula Branch; on "Segregation and Streaming in Secondary Schools" to District 8, Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation; an address at the official opening of Thistletown Collegiate Institute, Etobicoke.

Professor M. M. DAVIS, on "Highway Design and Frost-Susceptible Soils" to the Canadian Good Roads Association.

Professor B. C. DILTZ, lectures at the College of Education, University of British Columbia; on "Placid Ponds and Whirlpools" to the Association of Women Teachers of Ontario; on "The Play's the Thing" to the Shakespeare Society; on "Views versus Visions in Literature" to District 8 of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation.

Professor W. A. C. H. DOBSON, on "Polarity in Linguistic Analysis" to a joint session of the American Oriental Society and the Society for Asian Studies.

Professor W. DRAY, on "Historical Causation and Freedom of the Will" to the Metaphysical Society of America.

Dr. R. W. DUNNING, a joint paper with Professor T. F. McILWRAITH to the Eleventh Annual Iroquois Conference at the Allegheny State Park; on "Ecologic and Demographic Considerations in Ojibwa Residence Patterns" to the American Anthropological Association; a paper to the Political Science Association.

Professor W. T. EASTERBROOK, on "Comparative Economic History—Prospects and Problems" to the Economic History Association at Williams College, Williamston, Mass.; on "The North American Pattern" to the Queen's University Summer Seminar in Economics; on "Political Economy in a Staples Producing Area" to the Commonwealth Studies Center, Duke University, Durham, North Carolina; on "Sputnik and After" to the Royal Society of Canada, in Edmonton.

Dr. J. H. EBBS, on "Physical Fitness" to the Citizens Council in Ottawa.

Dr. GORDON EDMUND, a paper to the Society of Vertebrate Palaeontology.

Professor G. A. EDWARDS, on "Compensation Programs for Salesmen" to a Sales Management Conference at Queen's University; on "The Changing Concept of Marketing" to the Toronto Young Men's Advertising and Sales Club; on "Salesmen's Compensation" to the Hamilton National Sales Executives.

Professor I. T. ESCHMANN, a series of lectures on "Mediaeval Political Theories" at the University of Montreal.

Professor B. ETKIN, on "From Icarus to Earth Satellites—The Romance of Flight" to the Royal Canadian Institute and the Toronto Branch of the Engineering Institute of Canada; on "Aeolian Tones" to the Acoustical Society of America.

Professor E. L. FACKENHEIM, on "The Ontological Distinction between Nature and History" to the Metaphysical Society of America; addresses to the Philosophical Society of New York University, the Pittsburgh Midday Forum and Cornell University students.

Professor JOHN FARINA, on "Play is my Business" to the Rotary Club, at Winnipeg, and to the Albert College Alumni, at Belleville; on "The Values of Leadership" to the Metropolitan Toronto Boys' Association; on "Criteria for an Agency Sports Programme" to the Young Men's Hebrew Association, at Montreal; on "Problems of Leadership" to the Ontario Recreation Association, at North Bay.

Dr. R. M. FARQUHAR, on "The International Geophysical Year" to local Rotary Clubs; on "Research in Lead Isotopes" to the Volcanology section of the International Union of Geodesy and Geophysics.

Dr. J. L. FARRAR, on "Growth Patterns in Norway Spruce" to the Royal Society of Canada.

Dr. K. C. FISHER, on "An Approach to the Organ and Cellular Physiology of Adaptation" to the Society of General Physiologists' Symposium on Physiological Adaptations.

Professor GEORGE E. FLOWER, on "Pass or Fail" to a conference on promotion policies of the Toronto Public School Principals' Association; on "Unitary and Multiple Executive Organization" to the Ontario Association of School Business Officials.

Professor H. M. FOWLER, on "Teacher-constructed Tests" to the Ottawa Public School Principals' Association; on "What Tests Can Do for the Teacher" to the Elementary School Inspectors and Teachers' College Staff annual conference.

Professor NORTROP FRYE, on "Culture and the National Will" at Carleton College.

Professor H. J. FULLER, on "A New Basis of Prescription Fees" to the St. Catharines and District Pharmacists' Association; on "Developing the New Philosophy of Professional Fees" to the Fourth Pan-American Congress, Pharmaceutical Economics Section, and to the National Conference of Pharmaceutical Secretaries; on "A Summary of an Extensive Canadian Survey on Prescription Costs" to the Annual Executive Meeting of the Canadian Pharmaceutical Association; on "Prescription Costing and Pricing" to the Ontario Retail Pharmacists' Association, and to Pharmaceutical groups at Ottawa and Sarnia; on "The Economics of Pharmacy" to the Spring Clinic, University of Buffalo School of Pharmacy.

Professor R. J. GETTY, on "Vergil in English Poetry" and on "A. E. Housman" at the University of North Carolina; on "Pagan Influence on the Form and Spirit of Greek and Latin Poetry" to the American Philological Association; on "Cicero as a Scholar" to the Classical Association of the Middle West and South.

Professor I. I. GLASS, on "Blast From a Pressurized Sphere" at Imperial College (London),

---

Atomic Energy Research Establishment (Harwell), University of Manchester, and the University of Liverpool; on "Interaction of a Moving Shock Wave with a Wire Screen" at Imperial College, London, and the College of Aeronautics, Cranfield; on "Boundary Layers behind a Moving Normal Shock Wave" at Imperial College, London; on "Research in Progress at the Institute of Aerophysics" to the Armament Development and Research Establishment, Fort Halstead, England; on "A Spherical Shock Tube" to the Technological Institute at Northwestern University, Evanston, Illinois.

Dr. D. H. GORMAN, on "Let Us Not Forget the Mineral" to the Joint Session of Prospectors and Developers Association, the Geological Association of Canada, and the Mineralogical Association of Canada.

Professor T. A. GOUDGE, on "Causal Explanations in Natural History" to the American Philosophical Association, Eastern Division.

Dr. ELIZABETH GOVAN, on "Old Age Security" to the Canadian Welfare Council's Workshop on Social Security; on "Residence and Public Assistance" to the Annual Conference of the Ontario Welfare Council.

Professor J. W. GRAHAM, on "Palaces of Minoan Crete" at local societies of the Archaeological Institute of America in Indianapolis, Lexington, Oxford (Ohio), Cincinnati, Springfield, and Pittsburgh; on "Turkey by Car" at Springfield; on "Archaeology Today and Tomorrow" at the University of Pittsburgh, and to the Aesculapian Society in Toronto.

Professor T. C. GRAHAM, on "Organization and Administration" to the National Office Managers Association.

Dr. F. S. GRANT, on "The International Geophysical Year" to scientific Societies and clubs in Ontario.

Professor A. C. H. HALLETT, on "The Viscosity of Liquid Helium at Frequencies of 11.8 and 35.5 kc/s," "Drag Coefficient of a Sphere in Liquid Helium II," "The 'Critical Velocity' Found with the Rotating Cylinder Viscometer in Liquid Helium II," and "The Velocity of Sound in He<sup>3</sup>-He<sup>4</sup> Mixtures" to the Fifth International Conference on Low Temperature Physics and Chemistry, Madison, Wisconsin; on "The Flow Properties of Liquid Helium" to the Canadian Association of Physicists Congress at Ottawa; on "The Flow Properties of Liquid Helium II" to the Second Symposium on Melting, Diffusion, and Related Topics, Ottawa; on "Liquid Helium" to the South Western Ontario Local Section of the Canadian Association of Physicists, London.

Dr. G. C. HARE, a lecture to the Rideau District Dental Society.

Professor D. M. HAYNE, addresses at the Commencement Exercises of Parkdale Collegiate Institute and Schumacher High School; on "Shop Talk in Education" to the Board of Education and Home and School Association of East York.

Professor J. F. HEARD, on "Satellite Round-up" to the Royal Astronomical Society of Canada, Toronto Centre.

Professor F. M. HEICHELHEIM, on "Moses Mendelsohn and the Essentials of Reform" to the Reform Congregation Temple Emmanue-El; on "World-historic Problems 669-585" to the Orient Club; on "Passover Questions" to Congregation Habonim.

Professor KARL F. HELLEINER, lectures in the summer session of the University of British Columbia; on "An Unknown Chapter in the Demographic History of Pre-Industrial Europe: The Sixteenth Century" to the staff seminar in the Social Sciences at the University of British Columbia.

Professor CHARLES E. HENDRY, on "Team Work—Today and Tomorrow" to the Ontario Psychiatric Association; on "Health and Welfare in an Expanding Economy" to the Ontario Public Health Conference; on "The Home and School—Their Role in Society and Education" to the Canadian Conference on Education; on "Social Change and the New Understanding of Human Values" to the Canadian Conference on Social Work; on "Some Implications of Leadership Research for Staff Training and Development" to the Ontario Association of Training Directors; on "Some New Understandings and their Implication for Leadership Development" to the Department of Christian Education, Canadian Council of Churches; on "Changing Patterns in the Canadian Family" to the Quebec Association of Home and School and Parent-Teacher Federation; on "Values in Community Relations" to the Police Community Relations Institute, Michigan State University; on "An Applied Social Science View of Leadership" to the 1958 Canadian Education Association Short Course; on "The Role of Home and School in Education Today" to the Canadian Home and School Association and Parent-Teacher Federation.

Professor C. HERSHFIELD, on "The Design and Construction of the Roof Structure for the Stratford Shakespearean Festival's New Theatre" to the Engineering Institute of Canada.

Professor DAVID HOENIGER, on "Problems in Shakespeare Editing" to the Association of Canadian University Teachers of English.

Professor HELEN S. HOGG, on "Stars that Travel Together" to the Royal Canadian Institute and the Royal Astronomical Society of Canada; on "Twentieth-Century Astronomy" to the University Women's Club of London; on "Star Clusters" to the London Centre of the Royal Astronomical Society of Canada.

Professor WM. C. HOOD, an address at a conference on Financial Institutions at Queen's University.

Dr. B. HORD, a lecture to the Bay of Quinte Dental Society.

KEIRSTEAD, an address to the Annual Congress of the National Welfare Council of Canada; a paper to the 75th Anniversary Symposium of the Royal Society of Canada; on "The Theory of Capital" before the Seminar on Economic Development (Centre of International Studies), and the Graduate Economics Seminar of the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, and before the Seminar on Fiscal Policy of Harvard University; a talk to a staff group at Yale University; on "Some Regional Aspects of the Canadian Economy" to the Staff Course Seminar of Central Mortgage and Housing Corporation; on "Canada and the North Atlantic Treaty Organization" to the Senior Officers' Seminar at the R.C.A.F. Staff College.

KENT, on "Modular Coordination in Building" to the Nova Scotia Association of Architects, to the Toronto Chapter of the Ontario Association of Architects, and to the Architectural Institute of British Columbia; lectures to Civil Engineering students at the Nova Scotia Technical College, and Architectural students at the University of British Columbia and the University of Manitoba.

Professor DONALD KERR, a paper to the Canadian Association of Geographers.

Professor M. W. LISTER, on "Complex Ions" in a lecture tour of the Western Provinces.

Professor K. C. LIVINGSTON, two addresses on "Wage and Salary Administration" to the National Office Managers Association.

Dr. R. S. LOCKE, a lecture to the Niagara Peninsular Dental Society.

Professor W. M. D. LONG, five lectures to the Calgary and Edmonton Teachers' Conventions; addresses to the Canadian Clubs at North Bay, Pembroke, Shawinigan Falls and Quebec City.

Professor D. V. LOVE, on "Observations on Forest Management in Europe" to the Southern Ontario Section, Canadian Institute of Forestry.

Professor C. C. LUCAS, on "A Biochemist Views Silicosis" to the McIntyre Research Foundation; on "The Problem of Cholesterol in the Diet" to the Ontario Dietetic Association; on "Reflections upon Some Lipotropic Facts and Fantasies" to the Symposium on Human Nutrition held in New York by the National Vitamin Foundation; on "Lipotropic Factors"

---

in the Transport of Cholesterol in Experimental Animals" to the Lipotropic Foundation Symposium, Philadelphia.

Professor L. E. LYNCH, on "The 'is' and 'ought to be' of Television" to the National Catholic Social Life Conference in Halifax.

Mr. JOSEPH McCULLEY, on "Some Problems in Corrections" to the John Howard Society of London and Middlesex; on "The 'Y' and the Future" to the Y.M.-Y.W.C.A., Kingston; on "Today's Challenge to Education" to the Kitchener-Waterloo Canadian Club; an address to the graduating class, Ontario College of Art, Toronto; lectures in the Penitentiary Officers Training Courses, Penitentiary Staff College, Kingston.

Dean IAN MACDONALD, on "Automation and its Effects on People" to the Public Affairs Group of the St. Catharines Y.M.C.A.-Y.W.C.A.; on "A New Economy of Welfare" to the Triennial Meeting of the Association of Y.M.C.A. Secretaries of North America; on "A Plea and Program for Social Consumption" and on "What do Canadians Value in their Standard of Living?" to the annual Couchiching Conference of the Canadian Institute of Public Affairs; on "Foreign Investment in Canada" to the Engineering Institute of Canada; an address to the John Fisher School Graduation Dinner.

Professor D. J. MCDougall, on "Cardinal Manning and the Social Problem" to the Canadian Catholic Historical Association; on "Constitutional Development in Canada and Ireland" to the Mid-Western Conference on British History.

Dr. S. A. MACGREGOR, an address to the Red Cross Annual Dinner Meeting.

Professor E. W. McHENRY, an address to the Virginia State Nutrition Conference; a paper to the Western Regional Group, National Research Council; on "Problems of Communication" to the Canadian Federation of Biological Societies; an address to the Scientific Club of Winnipeg; on "Nutrition Adaptation" to Medical undergraduates in the University of Manitoba.

Professor T. F. McILWRAITH, addresses at the unveiling of plaques in honour of the Honourable G. Howard Ferguson, the marking of the Backhouse Mill, and of Paul Kane, as representative of the Archaeological and Historic Sites Board of Ontario; a joint paper with Dr. R. W. Dunning to the Eleventh Annual Iroquois Conference at the Allegheny State Park; an address at the unveiling of a plaque to Sir William Osler at Bondhead; on "The Public, Science and the Role of Anthropology" to the American Anthropological Association; on "The Indians of Canada" to the Royal Canadian Institute; a paper to the Royal Society in Edmonton. Addresses at the unveiling of historical plaques at: the Mohawk Institute, Brantford; the earliest Baptist Church in Ontario, at Wicklow; at Hamilton, commemorating McNab and Dundurn Castle; at Middleport, commemorating Pauline Johnston; at Kingston, commemorating militia units in the 1837 hostilities; at Temagami, commemorating the H.B.C. Post at Bear Island; at Leaside, commemorating the first Airmail Service.

Dr. W. G. McINTOSH, two papers on "Periodontology" to the Southern Academy of Dentistry in New Orleans.

Dean R. R. McLAUGHLIN, an address to the Official Boards and Staff of Assumption University of Windsor and Essex College; an address to the students at the Timmins High and Vocational School Commencement; on "Canada's Graduate Student Problem in Science and Engineering" to the Toronto Section of the Chemical Institute of Canada.

Mr. W. B. MacMURRAY, on "Citizenship" to the North Toronto Kiwanis Club.

Dr. E. M. MADLENER, a paper to the International Association for Dental Research.

Professor D. MARÍN, on "Trends in Contemporary Spanish Poetry" to the Humanities Association of Canada; lectures at the International Academy of Spanish.

Dr. W. J. MAYER-OAKES, on "Excavations in Saskatchewan" to the Ontario Archaeological Society; on "Radiocarbon Dates from the Upper Ohio Valley" to the Eastern States Archaeological Federation; a lecture series on "Upper Ohio Valley Pre-History" in the Pittsburgh Public Schools as a Frick Commission Special Lecturer; on "Ancient Mexican Civilizations" to the Archaeological Institute of America, Toronto Society.

PATTERSON, on "The Research Program at the Institute of Aerophysics" to the Armament Research and Development Centre at Fort Halstead, England; on "Molecular Approach to Problems of High-Altitude, High-Speed Flight" to the Advisory Group for Aeronautical Research and Development, NATO; on "Problems of Hypersonic Flight" to the Belleville Branch of the Engineering Institute of Canada; on "Aerophysical Problems of Flight at Extreme Altitudes and Speeds" to the Canadian Aeronautical Institute and the Institute of Aeronautical Sciences (W. Dr. K. J. PAYNTER, a lecture to the Kingston and District Dental Society.

Professor G. B. PHELAN, a series of lectures on "The Place of Humanistic Studies in the Contemporary University" at the University of Dallas, Texas.

Professor C. E. PHILLIPS, addresses at the High School Commencements at Agincourt, Shelbourne, and at Bathurst Heights Collegiate Institute and Vocational School; lectures on "The History and Philosophy of Education" at the University of British Columbia; on "What Should We Aim to Achieve in the Professional Training of Teachers?" to the Canadian Conference of Deans and Professors of Education.

Professor CLIFFORD C. PITT, twenty lectures on "Pedagogy and Psychology" to field officers and home teachers of the Canadian National Institute for the Blind; a series of lectures on "Educational Psychology" to the Hebrew Midrash.

Professor M. R. POWICKE, a paper to the Anglo-American Conference of Historians; papers to the Institute of Historical Research in London.

PRIESTLEY, on "The Victorian Age in England" to the Royal Society; on "Dickens and Education" to the Dickens Fellowship; a paper to the Canadian Association of University Teachers of English and the Humanities Association of Canada; a paper for Section II of the Royal Society of Canada.

RAPSON, on "Materials of Construction for Chlorine Dioxide Manufacture and Bleaching" to the Technical Section, Canadian Pulp and Paper Association at the Western Conference and at the Summer Meeting; on "A New Process for Chlorine Dioxide Manufacture" to the Eleventh Alkaline Pulping Conference of the Technical Association of the Pulp and Paper Industry; on "Highly Purified Cellulose from Wood" and on "Carbonyl Groups and Colour Reversion" to the Technical Association of the Pulp and Paper Industry; on "Recent Developments in the Pulp and Paper Industry" to the Toronto Section of the Chemical Institute of Canada; on "New Developments in the Manufacture of Chlorine Dioxide" to the joint American Institute of Chemical Engineers—Chemical Institute of Canada Chemical Engineering Conference; on "Three-Stage Bleaching of Kraft Pulps to High Brightness" to the South-Eastern Technical Association of the Pulp and Paper Industry and to the Technical Section of the Canadian Pulp and Paper Association.

RIBNER, on "The Strength Distribution of Noise Sources along a Jet" to the Acoustical Society of America; on "Some Linear Problems in Turbulence" to the Department of Chemistry, Stanford University; on "Some Aspects of Flow Noise" to the Department of Mechanical Engineering, University of California.

WILSON, 23 lectures at universities in the United States, as national lecturer for the Sigma Xi Society; papers to the International Association of Volcanology and the International Association of Hydrology; lectures at the University of Colorado Summer School; an address to the Annual Meeting of the Royal Canadian Geographical Society and Convocation at Carleton University; on "The International Geophysical Year" under various auspices.

Professor MILTON WILSON, on "Recent Canadian Poetry" to the Association of Canadian University Teachers of English.

Dr. W. C. WINEGARD, a paper to the Symposium on "Defence Problems in Structures and Materials Research."

Professor F. E. WINTER, on "Byzantine Mosaics in Ravenna and in Sicily" to the Toronto Society of the Archaeological Institute of America; on "The Hellenistic Fortifications of Side in Pamphylia" to the Archaeological Institute of America; on "Byzantine Monuments in Istanbul," on "Greek Artillery and Other Engines" and on "Greek Cities in South Italy and Sicily" at local societies of the Archaeological Institute of America in Urbana, Oxford (Miss.), St. Louis, Lawrence; on "Greek and Roman Cities in Asia Minor" at the University of Mississippi; on "Roman Architecture in the Provinces" and on "Byzantine Mosaic Decoration" at the University of Kansas; on "Greek Artillery and Siegecraft" for the St. Louis Art Museum Radio Series.

LECTURES AT THE UNIVERSITY

The Sir Robert Falconer Lectures were delivered by The Right Honourable The VISCOUNT KILMUIR OF CREICH, G.C.V.O., Lord High Chancellor of Great Britain, on "The Highest Common Factor in International Affairs" and on "The Equipment of the Democratic Statesman in 1958."

The Donald C. Balfour Lecture in Surgery was delivered by Professor JOHN BRUCE, Department of Surgery, University of Edinburgh, on "Strategy and Tactics in the Surgical Management of Peptic Ulcer."

The First Glidden Lecture in Chemistry was delivered by Dr. J. H. SCHULMAN, Stanley Thompson Professor of Chemical Metallurgy, Columbia University, on "Static and Dynamic Attachment of Bubbles to Solid Surfaces."

The Armstrong Lecture was delivered by Professor PERRY MILLER, of Harvard University, on "The Puritan Element in American Writing."

Professor H. ASHLEY, Department of Aeronautical Engineering, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, on "Recent Theoretical Research on Unsteady Supersonic Flow over Wings and Wing-Body Combinations."

Dr. D. L. BLACKSTONE, Professor of Geology, University of Wyoming, on "Tectonic Framework of the Rocky Mountains."

Dr. IRA BROWN, Geological Survey of Canada, on "The Proterozoic of Northwest Territories."

Dr. ALFRED COBBAN, Professor of French History, University College, London, on "What is History and What is not History."

Dr. P. COURVOISIER, of Reactors Limited (Switzerland), on "Problems of Radiation Measurements."

Dr. IRA DYER, of Bolt, Beranek and Newman Inc., Cambridge, Mass., on "The Estimation of Noise-Induced Missile Vibration."

Dr. J. W. EARLEY, Gulf Research and Development, Pittsburgh, Pa., on "Search for Oil through Chemistry."

Mr. L. E. FRAENKEL, Department of Aeronautical Engineering, Imperial College, London, on "The Newtonian Theories of Hypersonic Flow."

Dr. J. N. L. GAUVIN, Laval University, on "Research at Laval."

Professor Sir HAMILTON A. R. GIBB, Director, Center for Middle East Studies, Harvard University (former Laudian Professor of Arabic, University of Oxford), on "Islam in the Modern World."

Sir NOEL HALL, Principal, Administrative Staff College in England, on "Experiments in Britain in Developing Managers since the End of World War II."

Mr. M. J. HEARLEY, senior member of Her Majesty's Inspectors of Schools, on "The Education of the People of England and Wales," on "The Role of Her Majesty's Inspectors," and on "An English Concept of Headmaster."

Professor A. M. KUETHE, Department of Aeronautical Engineering, University of Michigan, on "The Transition to Turbulent Flows in Tubes and in Boundary Layers."

Dr. RACHMIEL LEVINE, Chairman, Department of Medicine, Michael Reese Hospital, Chicago, on "Concerning the Etiology of Diabetes Mellitus in Man."

Dr. A. I. LEVORSEN, of Tulsa, Oklahoma, on "Some Applications of Regional Paleogeology."

Dr. JOHN F. MCCREARY, Professor of Paediatrics, University of British Columbia, and Chief of Paediatrics, Vancouver General Hospital, on "Child Health in India."

Sir WALTER MERCER, Past President, Royal College of Surgeons, Edinburgh, on "Painful Conditions of the Shoulder."

Professor C. I. H. NICHOLL, Mechanical Engineering Department, Université Laval, on "The Influence of Density Gradients in Turbulent Shear Flow."

Dr. ARNOLD R. RICH, Professor and Director of the Department of Pathology, Johns Hopkins University, on "Studies in Hypersensitivity."

Dr. S. K. RUNCORN, King's College, Newcastle-on-Tyne, on "Paleomagnetism."

Dr. F. S. SHERMAN, of Office of Naval Research, Washington, on "Structure of Shock Waves."

Dr. L. L. SLOSS, of Northwestern University, on "Stratigraphic Analysis and the Search for Oil."

Dr. A. W. TRUEMAN, Director, Canada Council, an address to the Royal Conservatory of Music Convocation.

Mr. A. J. WILLIAMS, Saskatchewan Department of Mines, on "Recent Advances and Opportunities in Saskatchewan Geology."

## REPORTS

### *Report of the Acting Dean of the Faculty of Arts*

In the session 1957-8 enrolment in the first year of the Faculty of Arts rose substantially above what might have been predicted from the forecast of growth in the enrolment of the University as a whole. The increase over admissions of the previous session was 24 per cent and even more striking was the demand for honour courses (up 38 per cent), in contrast with that for the General Course (up 8 per cent). The marked rise in Arts cannot be attributed to limitation in the professional courses, since applications for these courses in 1957-8 did not generally exceed the quotas; within the next year or two, however, this factor will probably come into play. At the moment there is apparent no circumstance which would reduce enrolment in the Faculty. The following table shows the growth of registration during the past four years; bracketed numbers indicate the ratios with respect to the registrations in the session 1954-5.

<table><thead><tr><th></th><th><i>All years</i></th><th></th><th colspan="2"><i>First year</i></th></tr><tr><th></th><th></th><th></th><th><i>% General</i></th><th><i>% Honour</i></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>1954-5</td><td>3,202 (1.000)</td><td>1,176 (1.000)</td><td>49</td><td>51</td></tr><tr><td>1955-6</td><td>3,348 (1.045)</td><td>1,185 (1.008)</td><td>44</td><td>56</td></tr><tr><td>1956-7</td><td>3,578 (1.117)</td><td>1,198 (1.019)</td><td>47</td><td>53</td></tr><tr><td>1957-8</td><td>4,064 (1.270)</td><td>1,485 (1.266)</td><td>41</td><td>59</td></tr></tbody></table>

Lack of space continues to be one of the most pressing problems of the Faculty; several departments are housed in already cramped and inadequate quarters. For some time the Department of Psychology has occupied buildings on the west side of St. George Street. During this session the Department of Italian, Spanish and Portuguese and the Faculty Offices moved to Huron Street and arrangements are under way for the Department of Mathematics to use Baldwin House. The Departments of Zoology, Political Economy and Anthropology need more space now.

During the autumn of 1957 four Presidential Committees were appointed and charged with the planning of three new science buildings to accommodate the Departments of Chemistry, Physics, and Zoology, and a new Arts Building to accommodate eleven non-College departments of languages, humanities, and social sciences. According to the present position and projection, the new Arts Building should be ready for use during the session 1960-1.

Two major additions to the courses offered have been made by the Council of the Faculty and adopted by the Senate. In response to the re-establishment of the Type A Specialist's Certificate in English and History by the Ontario Department of Education, the Departments of English and History jointly proposed modification of options in the honour course in English Language and Literature and introduction of a new honour course, Modern History (English option). Both of these courses will provide the subjects necessary for attainment of full academic credit toward the certificate.

Seven years ago the three-year General Course in liberal arts was introduced. In the autumn of 1958 there will be launched a new three-year General Course in Science, the outcome of nearly three years of planning begun in 1955 and mentioned by Dean Woodside in his Report for the session 1955-6. The new course has been designed to provide a broad but integrated education in natural science. Of the thirteen courses offered in mathematics and the natural sciences, no less than eight have been prescribed without option; in this respect the new course differs from its counterpart in liberal arts. The election of the other five courses will permit some measure of specialization in mathematics or the sciences, but much less than in the honour courses. In each of the three years a minimum of four hours per week will be devoted to studies in the humanities and social sciences.

The introduction of new courses and new options within existing courses has

---

made it virtually impossible to find within the regular time-table of thirty-seven hours per week a single hour which does not involve a clash of some sort; even the period from one to two o'clock, once reserved for the mid-day meal, has commonly been pressed into service. There is real need for examination of the structure and basis of the time-table. It might also be profitable to examine as a whole the pattern of options which has grown by periodic accumulation.

At the end of this session Professor I. R. Pounder retired as Chairman of the Department of Mathematics after several years of able and conscientious leadership. In the present scarcity of lecturers in mathematics, he has kindly consented to remain as a member of the teaching staff. Professor F. R. Crocombe retired from the Department of Political Economy, carrying with him the respect and affection of a generation of students for whom his enthusiasm and ready wit provided a happy stimulus. Regretfully reported is the death of Professor Florence M. Quinlan, who devoted the greater part of her life to teaching in the Department of Physics.

For the friendly and helpful co-operation of the members of the Faculty and the University in carrying out the affairs of the Faculty during Dean Woodside's absence, the appreciation and thanks of an inexperienced Acting Dean are gratefully recorded.

F. WETMORE

### *Report of the Principal of University College*

The anticipated annual increase in the number of students registered in University College continues. This year the figure is 1,417, and next session we may expect an enrolment of 1,500. The ratio of men to women remains at nearly two to one and, though this observation has no bearing on what precedes, the ratio of honour course students to general course students is, similarly, about two to one. This session our students come from 131 centres in Ontario, from eight other Canadian provinces, from the United Kingdom, the United States, and from fifteen other countries; 67 per cent are from Metropolitan Toronto, 25 per cent from Ontario outside Metropolitan Toronto, 1 per cent from other Canadian provinces, and 7 per cent from other countries.

Up to the present there has been no appreciable increase in the teaching staff, the number being 84 as compared with 83 last year, but henceforth each teaching department will have to be augmented steadily if our standards of instruction are to be maintained.

It has been assumed that the general expansion would necessitate the removal of University departments from University College, and this exodus has already begun with the departure of most of the members of the Department of Italian, Spanish, and Portuguese. It is regrettable that this department must be separated from other departments with closely allied interests. The move will afford better office accommodation to all concerned, but will give little or no relief in the matter of classrooms. Careful study must be given to this situation immediately, especially for University departments no longer housed in University College. Some additional classrooms must be found for them outside the College during the period before the opening of the new Arts Building.

An analysis of examination results reveals little variation from former years. A very large number of University scholarships were awarded to students registered in University College, including the Moss Scholarship and five Woodrow Wilson Fellowships in the graduating year. At a time when our educational system is being closely scrutinized, it is gratifying to note that the increase in the use of library books in the College Reading Room exceeds the increase in registration by almost 2 per cent.

A significant change has taken place in the matter of student government in the College. After much consideration and debate during the last two sessions, the undergraduates have effected an amalgamation of the University College Literary and Athletic Society and the Women's Undergraduate Association, the joint body

---

henceforth to bear the name of the men's historic organization. The vigour, enthusiasm, and goodwill that characterized the deliberations of the members of the two former organizations, and more especially of their two presidents, augur well for the success of this union.

It is with great regret that I have to report the resignation of Professor R. J. Getty, the Head of the Department of Classics. After eleven years of distinguished service as teacher, academic administrator, and productive scholar, he leaves University College to accept appointment as the George L. Paddison Professor of Classics in the University of North Carolina. Our best wishes for success and happiness go with Professor and Mrs. Getty, and we thank them very warmly for the very great contribution they have made to our community.

With the appointment of Mr. G. M. Wickens, formerly lecturer at Cambridge University, as Associate Professor in the Department of Near Eastern Studies, instruction is now being given in Modern Arabic and Persian. Courses in other Near Eastern languages and literatures will be offered in the near future.

Professor Frédéric Deloffre, D. ès L., professeur titulaire à la Faculté des Lettres de Lyon, was this year's Visiting Professor in the Department of French during the spring term. His graduate course on "La comédie de mœurs en France de Molière à Marivaux," in which field he is an outstanding authority, and his literary courses to advanced undergraduates were in the high tradition established ten years ago by the late Professor René Bray, our first Visiting Professor, and maintained by all his successors.

Mention should be made of the Bilingual Seminar on French Canada Today held by the Department of French of University College in the Howard Ferguson Hall on the occasion of the annual meetings of the Alliance Canadienne and the Ontario Modern Language Teachers' Association on April 9 and 10. This public discussion in French and English of such important topics as culture, economics, labour unions, democracy, and education in Quebec, introduced by specialists from both provinces and freely participated in by many members of the audience, was highly successful in promoting mutual understanding and goodwill. The several hundred Canadians who came from many parts of Quebec and Ontario are under deep obligation to Mr. C. L. Burton, C.B.E., LL.D., whose donation of the royalties from his autobiography *A Sense of Urgency* made this seminar possible.

Research projects and publications, now listed under separate headings in this Report, indicate the great importance which all departments in the College attach to this phase of their activities. No increase in teaching hours, a natural enough contingency of rapid expansion, should be allowed to interfere with this primary obligation of scholarship. Other items of College interest, such as promotions, resignations, sessional appointments, leaves of absence, and public lectures, are now reported elsewhere in this publication.

The report of the Committee on College Residences, submitted recently to the Board of Governors, calls attention to the immediate need for increased residential accommodation for both men and women students. The Provincial College must be at least as well prepared to offer these facilities as are the Federated Colleges. The important role of residences in the intellectual life of the University is fully demonstrated in the reports of the two deans.

Dean Innis submits the following statement on Whitney Hall:

"Whitney Hall is rapidly becoming once more a solidly University College residence. Of the 165 residents there this year, 151 were University College students; this is 91 per cent as against a proportion of 80 per cent last year. The distribution between courses was ninety-five students, or 63 per cent, enrolled in honour courses and fifty-six in the General Course.

"Of the students in honour courses, three led their courses and twenty-three attained first-class standing, 24 per cent of the total number. Residents won twenty-three scholarships and awards, six of them going to one third-year student. There were four failures in the residence, a rate of less than 3 per cent.

---

"This year there were fourteen overseas students in Whitney Hall as against eight the year before. As to students in professional courses it is not only very unlikely that any can be accommodated in Whitney Hall next year, but quite possible that increase in numbers may make it necessary to place some University College students in the house at 146 St. George Street.

"Among the dons of Whitney Hall Miss Elisabeth Orsten replaced Miss Marion Hogarth, and Miss Ann Bolgan and Miss Elma Kopetsky continued in residence. The don acts as an adviser and court of appeal while the business and discipline of each house are looked after by the elected head girl and house committee. Dons and head girls together constitute the Residence Council presided over by the Dean, which is concerned with the order and welfare of the entire residence.

"During the year staff members are entertained as guests at dinner in the houses. Talks by the guests or discussions with them over coffee in the Common Room are a pleasant kind of informal contact between staff and students.

"The Dean of Women serves on numbers of committees both in the College and in organizations affiliated with the University, and fulfils many speaking and social engagements in connection with the College. A great deal of time during this year has been invested in planning, in general, for future men's and women's residences for University College and, in particular, for the projected new wing of Whitney Hall. In another year or two the space provided by this new house will be desperately needed and 146 St. George Street can then be used for students in professional courses until the new University residences are ready.

"The library, provided and supported by the University College Alumnae, is in constant use and is a much appreciated facility. This winter Miss Scott, a graduate of the College and a former teacher who had once stayed during a summer course in Whitney Hall, bequeathed to the residence a sum of money and a number of books from her collection.

"The Women's Union has this year served as a place for lunch and a social centre for larger numbers of non-resident women students and staff members than ever before. The theatre and common rooms were, as usual, constantly booked throughout the year for the plays, discussions, meetings and dances of about thirty campus organizations."

The following report on the Sir Daniel Wilson Residence is submitted by Dean Macdonald:

"To Sir Daniel Wilson, Malcolm Wallace has attributed the following statement of attitude: 'The chief function of student residences is to make a notable contribution to the intellectual atmosphere of the university.' The success of a college residence must be judged primarily by academic achievement in the widest sense.
