It should provide a ferment of intellectual activity for the students of the College as a whole; as an instrument of education, it must certainly rank second only to the classroom.

"Although success in such wide terms is difficult to measure, it is gratifying that the unchanged level of residence fees during the past four years has made it possible for the best students, upon whom the quality of the Residence depends, to afford the privileged life which the Residence provides. It is difficult to imagine higher fees without a substantial increase in our Residence Bursary Fund, which is now virtually exhausted.

"In 1957-8, the number of University College men in residence was 167 compared with 120 in 1954-5, the first year of the Sir Daniel Wilson Residence. Although this represented 18 per cent of 892 male members of the College, it was still significantly lower than the percentage for the other Arts Colleges. On the other hand, the gathering of thirty-five of our members from the four corners of the world has made the Residence a truly universal community.

"It is heartening to note that in statistical terms the performance of residence members was again outstanding. Whereas the average of all students in the Faculty of Arts gaining first class honours was 10 per cent, the thirty-eight University College students who won honours in the Sir Daniel Wilson Residence made up 23 per cent

---

of our members. The preference given to students in honour courses for admission (141 of the 167 men) is justified by the striking total of 27 per cent who gained first class honours compared with 17 per cent for the Faculty as a whole. The number of admission scholars increased from nine last year to twelve, and the number of college and university scholarships and prizes won at the annual examinations increased to forty-two.

"A surprising feature of the academic results was the failure rate of 10 per cent compared with the 12 per cent average for the whole Faculty. That this should occur where the students have daily access to a don as an "academic tutor" and where every encouragement to study is available might suggest that the problem of academic wastage is one to be attacked more at the stage of admission than during the early years of the university course. It is, of course, dangerous to generalize from such slim evidence.

"The Residence continues to attract a staff of dons of the highest quality, with Dr. R. H. Walters, Mr. J. T. McLeod, Mr. E. A. Walker, and Mr. W. B. Macomber as the new appointments from twenty-seven candidates. Happily, they will be remaining with us for the next academic year, thereby softening the blow of losing at once Mr. R. S. Woof (Senior Don) from McCaul House and Dr. W. W. Piepenburg from Hutton House.

"The patient assistance of the dons, and in particular of Mr. Woof, was largely responsible for the introduction of new regulations and a new disciplinary procedure. Regulations acceptable to the Dean and students were devised by the Dean and Residence Council with their enforcement entrusted to a Residence Caput composed of the six house presidents and following a set procedure. The purpose is twofold: to encourage the students to assist in maintaining a sound community life, and, more important, to free the don for his most effective role—the guidance and tutoring of his students. The Caput has demonstrated an increasing sense of responsibility and the perennial problem of damages to the building has diminished steadily.

"In addition to the usual residence social life, prominent people were invited to meet our students at dinner and over coffee in the Senior Common Room, notably Sir Geoffrey Vickers, Mr. J. B. McGeachy, Reverend Samuel Shoemaker, and Mr. Joseph McCulley. Stocked with 1,000 volumes and a wide assortment of periodicals, the J. S. McLean Library has added a new dimension to residence life this year. For their assistance in providing the Residence with a guest room and numerous improvements to the building, I am indebted to the Assistant Comptroller and various members of the Superintendent's Office.

"Meanwhile, the Howard Ferguson Wing continues to serve the students, the College, and the University for a wide variety of functions: dinners, plays, rehearsals, musical events, meetings, and parties. Moreover, it was the opinion of the College Committee on Residence Expansion, which was chaired last year by the Dean of Men, that the Howard Ferguson Wing would be called upon for double duty if the College acquired the residences necessary for a doubled enrolment by 1968-9.

"Human resources, however, may not be as elastic as physical facilities and as our enrolment grows I would anticipate the Office of Dean of Men devoting more time to the College and non-residential men of the College. This in turn will imply greater responsibility delegated to the senior don in charge of each residence building. Indeed, quite apart from increased enrolment, this second year of office has served only to increase my awareness of the variety of student needs and problems, and my inadequacy, even with the virtually ideal facilities such as the Sir Daniel Wilson Residence provides, in meeting them."

Grateful acknowledgment is made by University College to the Acting President, Dean Woodside, for the efficient and generous support given by him to the College throughout the present session. I wish also to record my gratitude to all my colleagues for their continued loyal co-operation. To the Board of Governors and to the Alumni and Alumnae we express our warm appreciation for their constant devotion to the interests of the College. We thank the following donors for their special gifts:

---

The estate of the late Mrs. Ella Jane Ferguson for the large residuary legacy, as well as table and chairs from her dining-room, and paintings by Moreland and Weston.

The estate of the late W. C. Michell, and the estate of the late Miss C. S. Michell, for a legacy sufficient to establish numerous scholarships in University College.

The estate of the late Miss Ethel O. Scott, approximately \$5,000 to \$6,000 for the building fund for Women's Residences in University College.

The James Harris Foundation, \$1,500 to maintain their scholarships at their present value of \$350 and to provide for future increase in value.

The University College Alumnae Association, \$1,330 for scholarships, bursaries, and student activities.

The McLean Foundation, \$1,250 as special assistance to a deserving student, and \$350 toward the expenses of the Alexander Lectures.

The University College Alumni Association, \$775 for scholarships, prizes, and student activities.

Simpson-Sears, Limited, \$1,080 to cover the six Simpson and Simpson-Sears Scholarships.

Mrs. Frederic Davidson, \$1,000 to increase the value of the Frederic Davidson Fund.

Mr. Arthur Cohen, \$900 to increase the two Cohen Scholarships from \$150 to \$300 each until the session 1960-1.

Mr. Percy Hermant, \$700 to cover the Hermant Scholarships.

Mr. Arthur Caplan, \$350 to cover the Arthur Caplan Matriculation Scholarship.

Mr. Edmund Peachey, \$300 for a Bursary for a student living in the Sir Daniel Wilson Residence.

The Panhellenic (Undergraduate) Association, \$300 for bursaries for three women students.

B'nai B'rith Toronto Lodge no. 836, \$250 to cover the two annual scholarships of \$125 each.

The Canada Life Underwriters' Association, \$200 for a student living in the Sir Daniel Wilson Residence.

The Kappa Kappa Gamma Association, \$200 for a bursary for a woman student.

The Toronto Branch of the Association of Administrative Assistants or Private Secretaries, \$100 for the W. J. Dunlop Bursary and \$100 for the W. G. Frisby Bursary.

The Canon Hedley Chapter, I.O.D.E., \$170 for a special bursary award.

The Delta Delta Delta Fraternity, two cheques of \$125 each, for women students of the College.

Mr. T. J. Meek, \$100 to establish the T. J. Meek Prize in Hebrew Syntax.

Mrs. Fanny Schwartz Solway, \$100 to establish the Joseph Schwartz Scholarship.

Members of the Class of 2T4, donations to increase the Class of 2T4 Bursary Fund.

The Delta Phi Epsilon Fraternity, \$100 for their bursary award.

An anonymous donor, \$100 for the Archives of the College.

Mrs. J. Home Cameron, \$50 for the J. Home Cameron Prize.

The Public Speaking Association, \$50 for their bursary award.

Mr. R. F. Rodgers, \$25 to be applied on the W. R. Taylor Fund.

The University College Alumnae Association, a copy of the film "Pursuit of Wisdom."

Dr. W. S. Wallace, the gift of a reprint by Hugh Hannah, on *Sir Daniel Wilson: The Man and His Work*.

F. C. A. JEANNERET

## Report of the Dean of the Faculty of Medicine

One hundred and nineteen students were graduated in Medicine at the special convocation for Law and Medicine on June 13. This number is somewhat smaller than usual—not because of any plan to reduce enrolment, but rather because the admissions committee of the Faculty, when they were selecting entrants to the first professional year, overestimated the number in this class who would complete the premedical examinations. We try to arrange the classes so that there will be approximately one hundred and fifty students in each of the professional years. Twenty doctors qualified at the same time for the Diploma in Psychiatry, fourteen for the Diploma in Public Health, six for the Diploma in Medical Radiology, and six for the Degree of Bachelor of Science in Medicine. At an earlier graduation ceremony, fifty-nine girls received the Diploma in Physical and Occupational Therapy, and two students received the Diploma in Art as Applied to Medicine.

Students qualified for honours and scholarships in the undergraduate course in

---

about the same numbers as in other years. It is noteworthy that one of the two Rhodes Scholars selected to represent Ontario at Oxford University was a member of the graduating class in Medicine. The award of one gold and two silver Cody Medals was reinstated this year.

Although the Faculty of Medicine is not as immediately concerned with expansion plans as are some of the other faculties and schools, there are some immediate needs which it is hoped it will be possible to meet early in the expansion programme. The research areas in the Banting Institute are seriously crowded, and it is hoped that the east wing of the building may be completed in the upper levels as was planned when the building was put up in 1926. There is also serious need for air conditioning and modernization of the animal quarters on the sixth floor, as well as for the provision of boarding kennels on the outskirts of the city for animals under long term observation.

The increased load of teaching of physiology to students in faculties other than Medicine has already resulted in the maximum utilization of all the teaching and student laboratory space in the Best Institute, and the research quarters here are becoming crowded. It is hoped that the extra three storeys originally planned to be erected over the lecture room may be completed without too much delay.

The buildings which presently house the Division of Physical and Occupational Therapy will be demolished to make way for more modern structures in the new plan, and it is expected that the Division will move to the old stone building on Queen's Park, now occupied by the Department of Zoology.

New plans made early last year resulted in a reduction of lectures in the third medical year, and an increased amount of time for practical work. Throughout the year there have been continuing discussions about the present system of teaching. Dr. Wightman is chairman of a small committee which will bring in recommendations as to ways and means for a more searching and prolonged investigation of teaching methods and curriculum content, in the light of present-day knowledge and future needs in Canada. It will probably be necessary to seek financial support from some of the foundations which are interested in medical education, and it is hoped that we may find a young man who is keenly interested in teaching and teaching methods, and who might devote one to two years in the capacity of executive secretary to the task of helping the committee in their work.

By a further agreement between the Women's College Hospital and the University, a closed teaching unit of forty beds in the division of Medicine has been established in the hospital, and students will attend for undergraduate teaching for the first time in the autumn of 1958, under the direction of Dr. Jean Davey, the physician-in-chief, who has been appointed to the Department of Medicine in the University as Assistant Professor. She will be assisted in the teaching programme by Dr. Joan Vale and Dr. Marguerite Hill. Teaching in the division of Obstetrics and Gynaecology has been carried on successfully for the past two sessions.

Dr. Botterell's committee brought in a report early in 1958 which dealt with the possible effects of universal hospital insurance on the teaching of clinical medicine. The report was adopted by the Council, and, after copies had been sent to the other medical schools in the province, was forwarded to Mr. Swanson, the chairman of the Ontario Hospital Services Commission. The chairman, in acknowledging the receipt of the report, expressed appreciation for the interest of the medical schools and assured us that everything possible should be done to safeguard the facilities for the teaching of clinical medicine. The Association of Canadian Medical Colleges had, in the autumn of 1957, presented a brief to the Minister of National Health and Welfare, which dealt with the same matter, and urged that nothing in the enabling legislation should in any way result in the limitation of teaching of undergraduate medicine and the training of specialists.

The Association of Canadian Medical Colleges also presented a brief at the same time to the Minister of Trade and Commerce, the Minister of Veterans Affairs, and the Minister of National Health and Welfare, urging an increased budget for medical research through the National Research Council. We had a very satisfactory hearing,

---

and it was gratifying to note that the medical committee of the National Research Council was informed in February that the allocation of funds for medical research would be increased by \$500,000.

A Department of Medical Biophysics was established in the Faculty, effective July 1, 1958. The new department will be centred in the research areas in the Ontario Cancer Institute on Sherbourne Street. Dr. Arthur Ham, for many years a member of the Department of Anatomy, will be the head of the Department, and he will be closely associated with Dr. Harold Johns, a member of the Department of Physics in this University for the past year, previously in charge of radiation physics in the University of Saskatchewan. The new institute is particularly well equipped with facilities for investigation in this area of ever increasing importance—the interrelation of physical forces and living matter. The new department has been recognized as a graduate department in the School of Graduate Studies, and it is expected that students proceeding to the degrees of M.A. and Ph.D. may be enrolled in the autumn of 1958. The areas of particular interest and investigation include cellular biology, electron microscopy, immuno-chemistry, virology, radiobiology, radiotherapy, and radiation physics.

The work of the Division of Postgraduate Education continues to expand and plays a most important role in the activities of the school.

A new course in electro-encephalography has been authorized. It will begin this autumn, and the students in the first six months will be engaged in lectures, laboratories, and seminars dealing with the basic sciences and practical demonstrations. This will be followed by six months' attachment to an active hospital department of electro-encephalography.

The Faculty is offering this autumn for the first time a two year diploma course in Speech Pathology and Audiology. There seems to be a need for men and women with training in this field. Those at present engaged as therapists have had their training in the United States or Great Britain. The University of Montreal has recently established a course to meet the needs of French Canada. The Government of Canada has granted limited financial support for the course in its early stages.

The name of the Diploma in Industrial Hygiene has been changed to Industrial Health. A widely representative committee under the chairmanship of Professor Rhodes, the Director of the School of Hygiene, has reviewed the curriculum and the arrangements for presenting material in this field. The committee recommended wide revisions in line with present-day Canadian needs. The committee also recommended that the course be offered on a part-time basis over two academic sessions for those who find it impossible to devote full time during one session. These recommendations were approved and will be effective with session 1958-9.

### *Buildings*

The research departments of the new institute on Sherbourne Street have been operating throughout the year, and the out-patient clinics and the in-patient area gradually took over the work of the old institute on University Avenue during the early spring of this year. This experiment in co-operation between the teaching hospitals—this modern institute with facilities for basic research and the latest devices for treating patients with ionizing radiation—has already attracted the interest of clinicians and investigators from many other centres. The plans for its formation, financing, administration, and staffing have engaged the attention of many people in the provincial government, in the University, in the teaching hospitals, and in the Ontario Cancer Research and Treatment Foundation over several years. It gives promise of being a great centre for investigation and treatment of cancer and for the training of scientists, and an important addition to this teaching community.

At the time of writing, the only units available for use in the new addition to the General Hospital are the lecture rooms and the students' common room. The new lecture room will probably be taken into general use in the coming session. It is hoped that the old lecture room may still be available for lectures also. With increasing numbers of postgraduate courses and special lectures, there is need for two such large

---

rooms. The new common room has been in use for several months, and the students are fortunate in the arrangements whereby coffee and other refreshments are served graciously and at extraordinarily reasonable prices by women volunteer members of the hospital committee.

The Western Hospital is proceeding with additions and alterations which will greatly improve and increase its facilities as a centre of treatment, teaching, and investigation.

I have tried in successive annual reports to stress the need for a new psychiatric institute. This need has been investigated by the Board of Governors of the University and presented to the Government in a comprehensive report. It is obvious to anyone who is familiar with the present situation. The rather antiquated eighty-bed receiving hospital was built in 1926 to serve the needs of a city one-third its present size, to provide for undergraduate teaching (the lecture theatre will now not accommodate a full class in Medicine), to be a centre for training psychiatrists for the province, and a nursery of research and investigation. Dean Clarke's vision of those days was realized. The hospital was a joint venture of the Government of Ontario, the city of Toronto, and the University. It has done excellent work, but a new building is badly needed—a building which will give increased facilities for treatment (both out-patient and day care as well as in-patient); facilities for basic research; and facilities for teaching and training not only in psychological medicine, but in all the ancillary branches which are so important to modern psychiatry.

In a field where costs of medical care are greater than for any other illness, a field which in the past has lagged far behind other branches of Medicine in research and enquiry, but in which there are successively brighter lights burning in a previously darkened horizon, a field in which there is at all levels of society increasing need to understand the very beginnings of break-down, and indeed all the illnesses that are included in the broad area of psychiatry, it is disappointing to report that our central physical facilities, both for treatment and for teaching and research, are primitive and outmoded. The delay in plans to correct this situation is difficult to understand. The maintenance of a staff with the training, imagination, and foresight necessary to deal with the urgent problems of psychiatry may present serious problems in the face of recurrent disappointments and temporizations.

### *Staff*

Dr. H. Cullumbine has resigned from the chair of Pharmacology. During his regrettably short stay here he has established a firm reputation as a first class teacher, a sound investigator, and has attracted to his laboratories increasing numbers of young graduate students in Pharmacology. For personal reasons he has decided to accept a post in Philadelphia. Dr. Edward Sellers has been appointed to the chair; he is a graduate of the University of Manitoba, who after service in the navy joined the university staff here in the Department of Pharmacology. He subsequently transferred to the Department of Medical Research, and for the past two years has been Director of the Defence Medical Research Laboratories, the principal medical research organization of the Defence Research Board. Dr. Sellers brings to the department the advantages of his previous experience in teaching and research here, his own valuable contributions in the research field, as well as considerable experience in administration and familiarity with the physiological and pharmacological problems that are peculiar to Canada.

Dr. F. I. Lewis retires from active association with undergraduate teaching on the wards of the General Hospital, where he has been the surgeon in charge of the Ward A service for the past ten years. Dr. Lewis has been a very valuable teacher and director of surgery. As one sees from year to year a succession of clinicians retire from active teaching while still mentally and physically vigorous, there arises the question of whether the problem of retirement age should not again be reviewed. There are, of course, arguments in favour of early retirement, but it is notable that in business and other professions there is an increasing tendency to retain the services

---

of older men, provided they are physically and emotionally fit. Dr. Lewis, of course, will continue in his private practice, and will, we know, maintain an active interest in the welfare of the Department in which he has given such valuable service.

Dr. Desmond Burke has resigned from the Department of Radiology on giving up his post as radiologist of Sunnybrook Hospital. Dr. W. G. B. Casselman has resigned from the Department of Anatomy to take a research post in New York. Dr. D. L. J. Bilbey of King's College Hospital, London, England, is expected to join the Department of Anatomy in September.

Dr. Howard McCart of the Department of Oto-Laryngology died after a rather long illness. He had been a member of the Department since 1926, interrupting his hospital and teaching work to serve in the Second World War as the specialist in oto-laryngology with #15 General Hospital. Dr. Lionel Armstrong, who had just retired as the senior obstetrician and gynaecologist to the Western Hospital, died in February after a brief illness. Both these men will be remembered gratefully by their colleagues, and by generations of students and internes, as outstanding practitioners of their respective specialties, and as sound teachers and preceptors of students and resident staff.

### *Visitors and Special Lectures*

The Balfour Lecture was given in the autumn by Professor John Bruce, the newly appointed Regius Professor of Clinical Surgery in the University of Edinburgh. The Phi Delta Epsilon Lecture was given by Dr. Rachmiel Levine of Chicago. The school was privileged to have a visit from Professor Sir Walter Mercer, the retiring President of the Royal College of Surgeons of Edinburgh. He lectured to the students and resident staffs and visited the teaching hospitals. The exchange visitor from the Middlesex Hospital was Dr. Evan Bedford. He spent a month in the autumn term visiting the various teaching hospitals and taking part in teaching. His main interest is cardiology.

Dr. R. M. Janes, who retired as Professor of Surgery in July, 1957, spent four months as Sims Travelling Professor of the Royal College of Surgeons of England, visiting the countries of the Commonwealth in the continent of Africa.

Professor J. D. Hamilton, the head of the Department of Pathology, spent three weeks in France at the invitation of the Government of France, lecturing in the French language at the Sorbonne and other medical schools.

Dr. Nelles Silverthorne spent two weeks in Ecuador lecturing and teaching in paediatrics.

### *Financial Report*

The Medical Alumni increased their contributions this year to a total of \$12,000 in scholarships and bursaries and loan funds. Dr. Wallace Duncan of Cleveland increased the amount of the Mary and Wallace Duncan Fund by a further \$5,000.

Postgraduate and special training has again been strongly supported through grants from the McLaughlin Foundation. Several scholars from our school are enjoying these fellowships. There are two fellows under the Markle Foundation, both doing excellent work in teaching and investigation. Two men have received support for postgraduate training under the McLean Foundation. The research work of the school has been strongly supported in several areas by the Bickell Foundation.

The school continues to receive support from the government granting bodies, the National Cancer Institute, the Ontario Cancer Research and Treatment Foundation, the Canadian Arthritis and Rheumatism Society, and several private bequests and gifts. The Atkinson Charitable Foundation grant, which has for the past five years made it possible to establish and operate a special unit in the Banting for the direction of the clinical use of isotopes, has been terminated. The Faculty of Medicine is extremely grateful for the very liberal support of the Foundation, which has enabled the school to give a lead in this new field. The hospitals and the University can now make arrangements for the continuance of the work.

J. A. MacFARLANE

---

## *Report of the Dean of the Faculty of Law*

The session just completed was significant for the Faculty of Law since it marked the inauguration of two new policies in the somewhat chequered career of law in this University. Of paramount importance was the implementation of the new Ontario plan for legal education under which it became possible for all universities in Ontario to open teaching faculties of law and to have their graduates accepted for admission to practice on a basis of equality with those of the reorganized law school conducted by the Law Society of Upper Canada. As a result of this final removal of the difficulties with which graduates of this Faculty have been faced since 1949, it is gratifying to report that for the session 1957-8 applications for admission to the first year were received from some 192 persons. If one bears in mind that normally the Committee on Admissions of this Faculty requires a "B" standing in previous Arts work as a condition of admission, this rise in the application rate is even more remarkable. The increase in applications was a source of great satisfaction, coming as it did after several precarious years during which, owing to factors beyond our control, numbers had shrunk to the vanishing point. It is unfortunate that the enthusiasm engendered by the apparent success of this University's policy was tempered by some of the problems raised by the very success of that policy.

In the first place, limitations of both staff and physical accommodation required a drastic reduction in the number of applicants that could be accepted for admission. The Committee on Admissions accepted one hundred of the applicants seeking admission and of those hundred, eighty-seven persons actually registered for the work of the first year. The number of students writing the first-year examinations last May was eighty. Despite the fact that the Committee on Admissions had an opportunity of selecting students with high academic attainments in their previous university work, some 20 per cent of this group unfortunately failed their examinations. This raises again a problem with which this and other faculties of law have been contending for some years. It is obvious that a satisfactory, indeed a good record in Arts work is no guarantee of successful attainment in a programme of legal education. While many factors may contribute to this somewhat melancholy conclusion—a conclusion which is well-nigh universal on this continent—the change (if it be one) from an uncritical absorption of information to a position where individual judgments and discrimination concerning information must be made seems too difficult for a great number of university-trained applicants. During the past year we arranged to give all students of our first year the law admission test prepared and conducted by the Educational Testing Service of Princeton, New Jersey, in order to see whether it might not be possible to obtain additional information in an effort to reduce the failure rate in the first year. It is too early as yet to indicate whether there is sufficient correlation between examination results and the American law-school admission test to warrant making that test part of our admission requirements. We shall, however, continue it on an experimental basis during the next session in an attempt to cope with a problem that is particularly pressing in view of our limitations of space.

With the admission of some eighty-odd students in the first year, the limited accommodation of the reading room of our library necessitated a move to enlarge the space in which students could pursue the intensive work required in the use of library facilities. The only possible way of doing this was to take over a room which had been used as a lecture room during the previous session and to throw it open as an adjunct to the reading room. In so doing, the school lost one of its three classrooms and was throughout the year confined to the use of two classrooms. This required considerable "staggering" of classes with the result that the two rooms were in almost constant use from nine in the morning until five in the afternoon. As the policy of the Faculty is to have classes conducted in the morning, with afternoons free for library work—the counterpart of laboratory work in other professional schools—it is apparent that in the first year of the "successful" termination of the struggle concerning legal education, we were faced with obstacles which, of necessity, made the work of the school difficult. The problem is, obviously, bound to become more acute in the future.

---

If this Faculty is to maintain its position in preparing students for admission to the bar in what has now become a highly competitive situation, the problem of physical accommodation must be faced without delay. This question was raised in my report of last year, and during the current session I again raised it on more than one occasion. In the light of the fact that our move to Glendon Hall (made at a time when removal of difficulties preventing the growth of this and other law faculties seemed almost an impossibility) was based even then on the understanding that it would be for a minimum of three and a maximum of five years, it is clear that the events of last year, opening legal education to Canadian universities on a freely competitive basis, has made even that limited period totally inapplicable to the changed circumstances. In view of these facts it was, to say the least, disappointing to find the report of the Advisory Planning Committee of last September relegating developments of space for the Faculty of Law to the category of "unscheduled expansion." It is difficult to appreciate the reasons for this decision and as I have on other occasions reported concerning the effect of the present situation on staff and student body alike, I can do no more in this respect than indicate the gravity of the situation and the folly of attempting to continue a teaching programme in law at the present site under existing conditions.

It is certain that the session 1958-9 will raise formidable problems. Outside of the classroom situation which is acute, perhaps the more serious problem will be the lack of accommodation in our library. The classroom problem could be solved by the erection of temporary classrooms. The library problem is insoluble without extensive capital construction. To consider such expansion at the present site would be, in our opinion, unwise and unwarranted.

During the last year participation of our student body in general university activities was almost non-existent, owing entirely to the distance from the campus and the feeling of isolation which that engenders. The fact that our students pay the increased athletic fee, to say nothing of the Hart House fee, and have little or no opportunity of enjoying the privileges of either, is not only irritating to students, but is as well a denial of the principle of full participation in the academic and extramural life of a university which is the prerogative of any university student regardless of the faculty in which he may be pursuing the subject-matter of his own discipline.

Overcrowding of classrooms and library and isolation from other university activities could in a well-established faculty be viewed with equanimity and many of the problems could be solved by that panacea of administrators, a limitation of enrolment dictated by limitations of physical accommodation. The effect of such a policy on a teaching staff just beginning to see the promised land after a struggle for nine years in the most adverse conditions, to say nothing of its effect on prospective applicants who have an increasingly wider selection of institutions offering academic instruction in law, should be obvious. I would be performing less than my duty if I did not point out that unless steps are taken at the earliest date to solve this problem, a situation may arise in which there will be no problem in which the University has any interest to preserve.

At a time when the sheer weight of numbers and the popular interest in current scientific advances may exert pressures not always coincidental with the maintenance of sound academic standards, it would seem essential to reconsider the role and place of law in a university programme. By a resolution of the Board of Governors, adopted in May, 1953, the intention was reaffirmed of developing "a centre for legal education and research second to none in Canada." Under the complete change of circumstances affecting legal education which has taken place during the last five years, this policy requires re-examination and affirmative action. At a time when other universities are bending their efforts to establish law faculties and to extend their appeal to prospective students, this University cannot afford to stand by idly and see its position of pre-eminence in this field undermined.

During the past session, the law library began its first year as an autonomous and self-governing library. While this has raised totally novel problems, there is no doubt, so far as the Faculty is concerned, that the move was a tremendous advance, and

---

despite the difficulties of installing and operating an entirely new system, the library staff has done a magnificent job for which all of us are grateful and thankful. Unfortunately, the problem of the disposition of past acquisitions in law which, in view of the long history of law in this University, are considerable, has still not been solved. There are many hundreds, indeed thousands, of law books in the main library which the law faculty feels should be properly housed in a law library and whose present position makes the utilization of university resources less fruitful than should be the case. It is possible that but for the break in administrative policy during the past session, steps could have been taken to resolve some of these questions. It was scarcely the type of problem to press during the last year and the matter still remains for solution in the not too distant future. Once again, it would seem likely that problems of this nature would be solved more easily were the law library of the University in closer proximity to other parts of the University where its facilities could be used by students and teaching colleagues in other departments of the University.

It is gratifying to report that the Carnegie Corporation, on the termination of its five-year \$50,000 programme of assistance in the field of Comparative Law, has seen fit to make to this Faculty an additional \$30,000 capital grant for a continuation of that work. As there should be added to this \$30,000 several thousands of dollars which are now being returned from the sale of books in the University of Toronto Comparative Law Series, there is no doubt that we can look forward to another four or five years of adequate financial support in this field. With a view to strengthening our basic research concerning the mutual problems of French- and Anglo-Canadian aspects of law, we are making use of this fund during the next session in supporting a member of our staff in a year's study of French legal institutions preparatory to further intensive work in those institutions as related to this country. The next session should also see an additional volume added to our Comparative Law Series, dealing with the relation between French civil law and English common law in Canada.

It is with pleasure that I record once more my sincere appreciation of the teaching and administrative staff for their strenuous and loyal co-operation under circumstances which, to a less devoted group, might have proved overwhelming.

CECIL A. WRIGHT

### *Report of the Dean of the Faculty of Applied Science and Engineering*

The most significant event in this Faculty during the year was the decision by the Board of Governors to proceed with the construction of a new engineering building on St. George Street, in general accordance with the recommendations of the University Planning Committee. Detailed plans have been worked out during the year, with Professor W. G. McIntosh acting as co-ordinator between the departments concerned and the Superintendent, and at the time of writing the architects are proceeding with the working drawings. This is indeed a welcome development. Although we shall have to await the completion of other phases of the general university expansion before we shall be able to take our full complement of students, the proposed building will help tremendously in our efforts to give sound engineering instruction to as many students as possible.

Pending the new construction mentioned above, we have had to limit enrolment though in actual fact we did not this year have to refuse admission to any qualified student. This was because an earlier regulation was revived, requiring a student who fails his first year to remain out a year before applying for readmission. Thus, this year there were virtually no "repeaters" in the First Year. Because of the lapse of time this same situation will not exist next September and so we must expect to have to refuse admission to some applicants. How this may most equitably be done is being

---

studied by a committee under the chairmanship of Professor W. J. T. Wright. Some members of the committee visited three representative institutions in the United States, which have had this problem to deal with for some time, to study their methods. One likely result will be that we shall seek information and opinions from high school principals to assist us in making the necessary decisions.

A new method of dealing with interviews between prospective employers and members of the graduating class was forecast in last year's report. Under the highly efficient direction of Mr. J. K. Bradford, Director of the University Placement Service, interviewing was confined to a three-day period in February, thus eliminating constant interruption of academic work throughout the year. The experiment was a great success and met with the approval of employers, students, and staff. With only the most minor modifications it will be repeated next year, and it is significant that some other universities who sent observers here have indicated that they intend to adopt the plan.

The Sub-critical Reactor referred to last year is now in operation in the Chemical Engineering Laboratory of the Wallberg Building. Designed and built by Canadair Limited, it is the first atomic energy facility to be installed in a Canadian university, and will provide the necessary equipment for instructing graduate engineers in this growing and important field. I should like to acknowledge the co-operation of the Atomic Energy Control Board throughout the period of approval of the design, testing, and granting of an operating licence; of Atomic Energy of Canada Limited; and of Eldorado Mining and Refining Limited who generously have lent us the necessary natural uranium, worth \$100,000. Professor D. G. Andrews who joined the staff during the year is in charge. The reactor was inaugurated by the Honourable L. M. Frost, Q.C., LL.D., D.C.L., Prime Minister of Ontario, on June 19, 1958, at a ceremony under the chairmanship of Lieutenant-Colonel W. E. Phillips, B.A.Sc., C.B.E., LL.D., Chairman of the Board of Governors of the University.

The importance of university residences is well recognized by those within universities, but unfortunately many others, often those in a position to provide the necessary financing, regard residences as frills on the solid core of university necessities. Nothing could be further from the truth. If one thinks of them only in terms of their effect upon academic attainment, then the performance of the residents of Devonshire House this year provides excellent evidence of their value in this respect, for as a group they obtained far higher standing than the average performance in the various professional faculties involved. Now that residence accommodation in the colleges is strained to look after their own students, the colleges can no longer, as they have kindly done in the past, take in some students from the professional faculties; the result is that the only university residence open to such students is Devonshire House, which can house only two hundred. This means that only about half as great a proportion of students in the professional faculties can be accommodated in residence as in the Faculty of Arts, yet their need is no less, especially when the value of residence life is exemplified by the wise deanship of Dean Symons of Devonshire House.

It is with regret that I record the retirement of Professor W. G. McIntosh as Professor of Mechanical Engineering after many years of staunch service to the University. Hundreds of students of mechanical engineering have reason to be grateful to him for assistance and advice with respect both to summer employment and to permanent positions after graduation, especially during many years when there was not the demand for graduate engineers that there is today. His many friends in the Faculty will be happy to learn, however, that he will be returning to us to continue the important work in connection with the new engineering building.

It is a pleasure to record the establishment of a new first-year Metallurgical Engineering Bursary for \$400 by Dominion Magnesium Limited; and also the establishment by the Kimberly-Clark Corporation of Canada, Limited, of two new scholarships of \$500 each, with \$100 in each case for the University, for first- and second-year students. The Company willingly accepted a suggestion which I venture to hope may set a pattern for future donors. They left it to the University to set the

---

terms of award, with power to change them at any time. This provides broad coverage and a flexibility very desirable in view of changing conditions, and simplifies administration in a field that is becoming increasingly complex.

R. R. McLAUGHLIN

### *Report of the Director of the Institute of Aerophysics*

An agreement has been signed between the Crown in right of Canada, represented by the Chairman of the Defence Research Board, and the Governors of the University of Toronto which states the conditions for a grant of \$250,000 toward the relocation and expansion of the Institute of Aerophysics. In addition to providing substantial aid in the construction of a new building and the transfer of equipment to a new site made available by the University, this agreement permits the continued use of our existing building at Downsview Airport. The space available to the Institute for graduate instruction and research will be more than doubled by this arrangement. The assistance of the Defence Research Board is sincerely appreciated since the above grant was the key contribution which made our expansion plans possible.

Among other items the above-mentioned agreement provides for the continuation on a permanent basis of the presidential committee known as the Advisory Committee for Aerophysics Research. The membership is drawn from the University, Defence Research Board, National Research Council, Royal Canadian Air Force, and the Canadian aircraft industry. The major functions of this committee are to provide advice on the Institute's research policy and programme and to facilitate the exchange of information between the participating organizations.

The relocation of the Institute of Aerophysics marks the end of the first phase of development begun in 1949. The need for fundamental research in aeronautics was never greater than it is now. The level of output of basic information since 1949 by the staff and students of the Institute of Aerophysics shows clearly the extent to which a university can contribute to fundamental science. The reason for the high output is to be found largely in the sense of accomplishment which the staff member has when freely working in the field of his choice and which the student expresses in those long, self-imposed hours of patient endeavour far beyond the normal working day of government or industrial laboratories. It is interesting to note that the cost per project has steadily decreased as the Institute has grown. Undoubtedly an optimum will be reached, but our experience indicates that in the field of the aeronautical sciences the research institute is more economical than an equivalent number of small independent research groups. Perhaps the most important contribution is the mutual stimulation between teaching and research. Research items of a few years ago have now been included in our graduate and undegraduate courses. Gaps in our knowledge which were uncovered during the preparation of lectures have often become topics for investigation.

The students at the Institute of Aerophysics receive financial support largely from research grants. During the past year it has become evident that this method of support is not adequate to meet the rising enrolment, and restrictions must continue until some adequate system for assistance to graduate students is devised. Serious thought should be given to whether Canada can afford to restrict the number of key men in aeronautical research and development.

At the time the first artificial satellite was projected into orbit, the Institute possessed the only wind tunnel known to the writer which could simulate the conditions of free molecule flow associated with flight at extreme altitudes. Our design has become the basis for similar facilities in England and France. The Institute frequently supplies design information and test procedures to various laboratories of the Defence Research Board. The North Atlantic Treaty Organization requested the Institute to prepare a detailed review of the information available on the shock tube, a versatile instrument developed at the Institute which has opened up many new fields in

---

aeronautical science. This review is now nearly complete and will be circulated widely in book form. Other new laboratory techniques have been supplied directly to various members of the NATO organization. During the year under review members of the Institute have been requested to participate in about two dozen seminars and conferences on this continent and in Europe.

G. N. PATTERSON

### *Report of the Director of the Faculty of Household Science*

This year marks the fiftieth anniversary of the laying of the cornerstone of the Household Science building, the munificent gift of Lillian Massey Treble to the University of Toronto. Owing to her vision and the unerring planning of Professor Annie L. Laird, the building is one of the most up-to-date on the campus. The refurbishing programme of 1953 to 1957 has modernized all laboratories, thus contributing to the maintenance of efficient teaching and research facilities.

Our rapidly changing world and the impact of discoveries in the physical and biological sciences have created the need for specialized training in our academic area. Money invested in education and research will benefit people in all walks of life. The research fund established by the Household Science Alumnae is receiving enthusiastic support and it is expected that it will be materially augmented when the patent on the gluten-free bread is obtained.

The year has been a busy and stimulating one. Registration remains about the same for the four years, but in response to an active recruitment programme enrolment in the first year increased almost threefold. In the General Course the number of students concentrating in this area has likewise increased. The demand for teachers in our schools and colleges is in excess of the supply of fully qualified specialists. These general course students may qualify for a Vocational Certificate in Home Economics at the Ontario College of Education to teach in the high schools. Opportunities for our honour graduates in administrative and research positions in hospitals continue to expand. The demand for research dietitians in hospital metabolic clinics is increasing, and graduates are being encouraged to proceed to a Master's degree in nutrition.

Registration has increased in the honour course in Food Chemistry but is not sufficient to meet the demand for graduates. More Canadian food industries and government departments are enlarging their sections for inspection control and research related to foods, thus increasing the demand for graduates. Of 103 students registered in food chemistry in the General Course, thirty-two are concentrators, some of whom after graduation enter medical school or pursue food chemistry at the graduate level. A course in Pharmacology was introduced in the fourth year honour Food Chemistry course and made available for graduate students. The purpose of this course is to provide background to interpret the role of chemical additives in foods.

An Open House was held in January. Demonstrations and exhibits by members of staff and students of both departments created interest among the twelve hundred visitors.

In memory of the passing of Dr. Florence M. Quinlan who taught Physics for several decades to our first-year household economics and household science students, a fund for a prize has been created by the undergraduates. This prize is to be awarded to the student who ranks highest in her class in this subject.

Two fellowships were awarded to students in the Department of Food Chemistry, a Dupont fellowship of the value of \$1,500 for a science teaching specialist, and the Reuben Wells Leonard Fellowship of \$900 for graduate work.

We regret to report the resignation of two faculty members: Miss Ruth M. Bones, who has been awarded a Fulbright Grant for advanced study in textiles at the University of Leeds, and Miss Martha Binch who has been awarded a scholarship at the University of Tennessee for postgraduate work in art and design.

---

This report would be incomplete without an expression of appreciation to the Acting President, Dean Woodside, and the administration. My sincere gratitude is also expressed to the Council and my colleagues for their unfailing support and loyalty.

BARBARA A. McLAREN

### *Report of the Dean of the Ontario College of Education*

The right and readiness of individuals to change their minds remain a feature of the democratic process. Some people leave teaching every year for some other occupation. Every year men and women who have been employed in other activities choose to enter upon a career of teaching. On June 16 the special ten-week summer course for 1958 attracted 749 prospective teachers, an increase of 50 per cent over the 1957 class. In this class men outnumbered women two to one. Meanwhile no marked decrease is evident in the number of students applying for admission to the regular winter course. Why is teaching attracting more people today than heretofore? Improved salary scales, degree of security, growing prestige of teachers in society, challenge to personal leadership, and renewed concentration on subject-matter that is worthwhile are a few of the causes for this increase. Some people doubtless still "drift" into teaching. Most students, however, who come to the Ontario College of Education in summer or winter, come seeking an outlet for their gifts and a career in which they can spend their lives valuably. The shift of emphasis in education from the adjustment of personalities to the training of minds is making a strong appeal to young people.

In May a third floor of the new wing was completed, making available eight additional classrooms and five offices. Without this additional accommodation, it would have been impossible to carry on in the present building with the summer programme.

The interest of experienced teachers in the courses leading to graduate degrees in education is increasing steadily. Some 450 teachers and other school officials devote most of their summer vacation to the improvement of their professional qualifications. Many naturally come from Ontario, but other provinces are well represented. The regular winter session still attracts graduate students from foreign lands. More Canadian teachers, however, should be encouraged and released to attend the regular session. To assist such students there is a serious need for scholarship and fellowship funds, but much may be achieved by a reorganization of in-service training. Attendance is now required of doctoral candidates and some courses will be offered henceforth in the late afternoon during the winter session.

The emergency courses offered in recent years in the Department of Vocational-Industrial Arts and the large enrolments in the regular courses have reduced the shortage of teachers in vocational schools in all fields except those of drafting, printing, and the electrical trades. Plans have been made to make up the resulting deficiency. During the year members of staff visited nearly all the graduates in their classrooms and assisted them in the improvement of their teaching methods and shop organization. These visits have been rewarding for all concerned. Beginning teachers often hesitate to reveal a difficulty to an inspector, but are quick to request assistance from their college instructor. It is hoped that this "follow-up" practice can be continued and expanded as a regular part of the programme for teacher-training.

This session marks a milestone in the history of the Library School. To celebrate thirty years of operation the school held a one-day colloquium on April 16 at which "Adult Education and Our Libraries" was discussed by 220 librarians and trustees drawn from all parts of Ontario. In spite of a regular annual increase of candidates for the course in library science, a shortage of librarians still exists. Other provinces take their share of our graduates. Approximately one-quarter of the graduating class

---

of this year was placed in college and university libraries; one-quarter in special and school libraries, and one-half in public libraries. In thirty years the Library School has graduated 1,217 fully qualified librarians.

A stage of the Atkinson Study has been concluded by the Department of Educational Research only to give rise to the need for additional studies aimed at further extension of research into the human resources of the schools. Assistance has been given by this department to the Provincial Government in the revision of the system by which grants are made to local school authorities. Experiments have also been conducted with a view to determining the value of aptitude tests at the Grade XII level and the suitability of present methods of examining at the Grade XIII level.

The Guidance Centre has continued to enlarge its services and its range of materials. In addition to assisting the schools of Ontario and of other provinces, the Centre now makes available certain of its services and materials to properly qualified personnel workers in industry.

On June 30, 1958, Dr. A. C. Lewis retired. For a period of thirty years he has given leadership in education, first as an instructor in the University Schools, later as Headmaster of the Schools, and finally, for the past fourteen years, as Dean of the Ontario College of Education. During his long tenure of office Dr. Lewis has worked ceaselessly and tirelessly for the well-being of the University Schools and for improvement in the programme of teacher-training conducted at the College of Education. His colleagues are deeply grateful for all that he achieved for them collectively and individually. Dr. Lewis has been succeeded by Mr. B. C. Diltz, who for thirty years has been successively an instructor in the University Schools and a professor of methods in English and History in the Ontario College of Education.

It is with deep regret that I report the deaths of two of Ontario's most distinguished teachers of mathematics. Mr. W. J. Lougheed, professor emeritus of the College of Education, retired in 1945 from the professorship of mathematics and died on May 2, 1958. Mr. J. G. Workman, another great teacher and citizen—a Mr. Chips of University Schools—retired in 1944 and died on March 30, 1958. Words fail to express what these two old friends of so many pupils and teachers contributed in their lifetime to education. So long as great teaching is remembered these two scholarly gentlemen will not be forgotten.

During this academic year Mr. C. D. S. Bates has been on sabbatical leave in England where he has pursued advanced study in mathematics and science at the University of London. At the promotion of Mr. R. J. McMaster from instructor in English in the University Schools to assistant professor of methods in English in the College of Education, Mr. John W. MacDonald has been appointed instructor in the University Schools.

The 1958 Canadian Education Association Short Course in Educational Leadership was conducted at the Ontario College of Education from May 12 to May 30. Some eighty leaders in education from all parts of Canada discussed topics related to the role of the superintendent and the inspector. The College was honoured to be host to this distinguished gathering of educators.

B. C. DILTZ

### *Report of the Dean of the Faculty of Forestry*

The event of greatest immediate interest to the Faculty of Forestry during the past year was the celebration of the fiftieth anniversary of its establishment; this took place on Thursday and Friday, October 24 and 25, 1957, and was attended by more than half of all those who have graduated in Forestry from this University. Of special significance to the Faculty and its alumni was the Forestry Convocation held on the Friday evening, when the Chancellor of the University on behalf of the Senate

---

conferred honorary degrees on six Canadians who have been outstanding in the forestry profession of this country. Just prior to Convocation, members of the Forestry Alumni were guests of the Board of Governors at a dinner given in honour of the occasion in the Great Hall, Hart House. We are all most appreciative of the recognition thus given the Faculty's anniversary by the whole University.

An Open House was held at the Forestry Building and at Glendon Hall, when the students and staff were hosts to many visitors, and by means of exhibits and demonstrations were able to acquaint them with some aspects of the Faculty's programme. The apparent success of this undertaking suggests that it should be repeated in the near future.

The Faculty's second half-century begins just as the University is undertaking a major expansion programme. The decision in this regard to relocate the Forestry Building will, it is hoped, have a favourable bearing on the growth of the Faculty itself. In my report for 1956-7, in commenting on developments that had taken place over the past fifty years, I pointed out that provision for "certain aspects of forestry education, in particular Pathology, Economics, and Wood Technology, is far from adequate at the present time." With the proposed change, including a larger functional basement, there should be excellent accommodation for a wood technology unit, thus providing facilities for instruction and research in a branch of forestry that has been given far too little attention here up to the present time. Similarly an extension of the building itself will provide more adequately for research and graduate study in other aspects of forestry. Under present conditions such work (other than that accommodated at Glendon Hall) is restricted both in the number of graduate students that can be accommodated and in the scope of the programme undertaken.

During the past year six graduate students have been working towards their Master's degree, three in forest economics and three in forest ecology. Recently there has been a marked increase in the number of requests for advanced work at this level, and enquiries have also been received regarding possibilities of work towards a Ph.D. degree. It is important that the Faculty take cognizance of and make preparations within reason to meet these requirements.

In this connection and having in mind the problems raised in earlier reports, it is most gratifying to the Faculty to learn that provision is being made for a librarian, and that a position of Forest Pathologist may soon be established. In their different ways, both of these appointments should contribute greatly to our future progress.

During the year a number of grants were received for the establishment of scholarships and for other purposes important to the work of the Faculty. In particular, the Kimberly-Clark Corporation of Canada Limited has established a fellowship of the value of \$2,000 to encourage research and postgraduate study in silviculture. The Booth Lumber Company has established an endowment fund in support of the Faculty's library—a particularly significant development at the present time. Mr. Gordon G. Cosens, former Dean of the Faculty, has set up an endowment fund to assist members of the staff in travelling abroad for special purposes. The Canadian International Paper Company has provided special equipment that will greatly facilitate certain aspects of the soils research programme at Glendon Hall. Mrs. R. P. Wright has established an undergraduate scholarship of the value of \$150 to be awarded to the student having the highest standing in Wood Technology. To all of these donors, who have thus shown a constructive interest in the future of the school, we are most grateful.

During the year, the fifth bulletin in the University of Toronto Forestry Series, entitled *Some Aspects of Forestry Research in Great Britain*, was published. This bulletin is based on a series of three lectures given earlier at the Faculty by Dr. L. Leyton of Oxford University.

In conclusion, I should like to express my own deep appreciation to the officers of the University, members of the Faculty staff and student body, and to the Forestry Alumni Association, for the keen interest and support that have been given the Faculty of Forestry in its many activities during the past year.

J. W. B. SISAM

---

## *Report of the Dean of the Royal Conservatory of Music*

The enrolment during the past year has shown very little change from the average of the last three or four years. As far as the taking of extension lessons is concerned, the tendency seems to be a move towards the outlying branches, and it is a problem to find suitable premises in the suburbs to cope with this demand.

As regards the activities at the main building, all have been overshadowed by the extremely welcome news that the new Conservatory is likely to arise far sooner than we thought possible a few years ago. Endless meetings of the Building Committee, in consultation with the architects, have taken place, and I would like here to express my thanks to all the members of the staff who have given so generously of their time on these occasions. The multitudinous detail in planning a new music school of these dimensions can hardly be guessed by the average outsider, and, in an operation of this kind, it has always to be borne in mind that the Royal Conservatory, although located in Toronto, is to all intents and purposes a national school of music. It is an institution unique in Canada and the planning must be along these lines. It might perhaps be argued that the feeling in general regarding the Conservatory has been far too parochial in the past. With the coming of our new quarters, we hope to erect a school which will be worthy of the status the Conservatory enjoys in this country.

In training singing-actors and professional instrumentalists of all kinds, it is essential to teach them their trade in surroundings which are more or less comparable with those they will encounter during their careers. One would not dream of training a medical student without a hospital. Similarly, one should not dream of training a singing-actor without a theatre, yet that is what the Conservatory has been trying to do over the past years. With the coming of more commercial television, new opera companies, and an increase in musical theatre of all kinds, more and more of these artists are going to be needed, and the Conservatory is the only place in Canada where they can be adequately trained. It is along these lines that one has to plan for the future, but it is sometimes difficult to make the powers-that-be realize these facts.

The first stage in the demolition of the old buildings took place last summer, when the girls' residence disappeared to make way for the expansion of the Ontario Hydro offices. Work will continue in the rest of the old buildings until the new one is ready and the eventual move will be accomplished in one operation.

### *Faculty of Music*

The principal change to be noted in the Faculty of Music enrolment was an increase of approximately 50 per cent in the first year of the Music Education course. As yet it is impossible to know whether this represents a trend, but, if so, it would be a welcome indication that we will be making a greater contribution toward filling the chronic need for school music teachers. Enrolment in the Faculty as a whole was 121, of whom twelve were in General Music, fifty-eight in Music Education, twenty-five in the Artist Diploma course, seventeen in the Licentiate Diploma course, and nine enrolled simultaneously in both Artist and Licentiate Diploma courses. Twenty-two received the degree of Bachelor of Music, two the Artist Diploma, and three the Licentiate Diploma. The degree of Master of Music was conferred on two graduate students (one in Composition, one in Musicology), and one degree of Doctor of Music was conferred. In addition to professional music students, we offered musical instruction to eight students in the honour course in Music in the Faculty of Arts, twenty-five students in the General Arts Course, and sixty-six students taking music as a Religious Knowledge Option in various honour courses in Arts.

As would be expected, Faculty of Music students participated widely in performing activities both on and off campus. Over seventy-five individual recitals were given to fulfil academic requirements. Students appeared as soloists with the Conservatory and university orchestras and the University Chorus. They also took part in the productions of the Opera School, the Opera Festival, and the C.B.C. Opera Com-

---

pany, as well as many radio and television productions, recitals, and concerts. Many undergraduates serve as church soloists and choristers, as organists and choir directors, and as members of a wide variety of instrumental and choral organizations in the city.

Chamber music activities sponsored by the Faculty of Music ranged from a successful evening concert featuring diploma course students to an interesting and stimulating informal afternoon of ensemble playing and singing performed "in the round" by both degree and diploma course students—tangible evidence of a growing movement within the Faculty under the guidance of Professor Olnick.

Departing from the custom of past years, the University Chorus, conducted by Professor Johnston, and the University Orchestra, conducted by Professor Rosevear, presented separate concerts in Convocation Hall. The Chorus also participated in the Inter-Varsity Choral Festival at Guelph. Assistant conductors were Alan Reesor (a Music Education graduate now at O.C.E.) for the Chorus, and Ivan Kowaliw (third-year General Music) for the Orchestra. The Collegium Musicum, under the direction of Maestro Barbini, gave a varied programme of music for voices and small orchestra in the Conservatory Concert Hall.

Perhaps the most interesting innovation during the year was the series of "Score Study" lectures sponsored by the Faculty of Music. Co-ordinated with the local musical season, the lectures were provided for music students, the University as a whole, and the general public, and were largely attended. Lecturers included visiting musicians of international reputation, members of the Toronto musical community, and staff members. It is planned to continue the series in 1958-9.

As a rewarding experiment in public relations, staff members visited selected secondary schools in various parts of the province during the school year—an extension of a series of visits started in the spring of 1957. By means of these visits, valuable contact was established with school administrators, guidance counsellors, music teachers, and, of course, school students interested in music. Opportunities were provided to explain the offerings in music at the University of Toronto.

Campus-wide musical events worthy of note were the series of recitals by University Organist, Dr. Healey Willan, assisted by guest organists, Douglas Elliott, Antony Garlick, John Searchfield, John Sidgwick, and Frederick Silvester; and another season of out-of-doors concerts from the Soldiers' Tower in Hart House, presented by University Carillonneur, Mr. Leland Richardson, which brought pleasure to many, both in the Commencement season and during the summer months.

It is a source of satisfaction that, in addition to discharging academic responsibilities, members of the staff of the Faculty of Music continue to take a very active part in the musical life of the city and of the Dominion. This extension of our musical influence takes many forms, including participation in professional organizations, conducting, recital performances, adjudication, speaking engagements, composition, and acting in an advisory capacity for a multiplicity of musical undertakings.

Mention should be made, in closing, of the valuable service performed by the Secretary of the Faculty, Mrs. K. R. Kilburn, and by Miss Margaret Lovell, secretarial assistant.

### *School of Music*

The policy of reducing the faculty and student body to more compact units was reflected this year in a reduction in the number of students registered in the main building. However, the national scope of the School was emphasized by the fact that there was an increase in the number of students from outside Ontario—indeed, they once again represented every part of the Dominion, from Vancouver Island to Newfoundland.

The national influence of the School was further emphasized by a continued increase in the number of candidates applying for Conservatory examinations, and there is every reason to believe that next year this will reach the unprecedented figure of 40,000. The Examination Department, under the supervision of its Registrar, Frederick Silvester, and his assistant, Mrs. Bickell, is to be congratulated on the

---

remarkable efficiency with which it has managed to handle this expansion despite all the handicaps of time, space, and a limited permanent staff. The Board of Studies, also, is to be congratulated on the new syllabus it has prepared for these examinations, a syllabus that is constantly under revision to ensure the most representative materials and the highest standards.

The most significant development, of course, was the introduction of new courses into the School of Music. Designed by the Principal, and operated this year under his direct supervision, they were planned to offer to a limited number of talented students a system of regular instruction in all musical subjects at three levels—senior, intermediate, and junior. They were to serve as a preparation for those students who might eventually qualify for admission to the professional courses of the Faculty of Music.

There can be no question of the general appeal of these courses, the need for them, and their initial success. Upwards of two hundred applications were received, and a maximum quota of students accepted into each course. It has long been the complaint of the Faculty of Music that students tended to resist the discipline of course instruction because they were not accustomed to it. In this respect the new courses proved most effective, and the examination results at the end of this first year indicate that the School of Music is now on the way to preparing a talented group of students who, when they are ready to enter professional courses, will not only be accustomed to this discipline, but will be much more adequately equipped to undertake professional training at the higher levels than was ever the case before.

The number of course applicants for next year has increased, and it would be very regrettable if, for reasons of budget, the courses could not expand as rapidly as they should. The Principal has repeatedly stated his conviction that the success of this type of instruction will depend on the high standard of achievement and not on the numbers enrolled, but he has also pointed out that there is no lack of talented persons who are anxious to enter these courses and who should rightly be given the advantages of a rounded musical education. This last season, for instance, it was not even possible to accept all the qualifying scholarship students of the School of Music, and these, after all, represent only a fraction of the general enrolment.

Among the many student concerts and recitals of the season the most significant was the first Canadian performance of Carl Off's "Carmina Burana" by the Conservatory Chorus and Orchestra. Convocation Hall was packed for this event, and the work was given a remarkably fine performance and a tremendous reception by the audience. This work calls for an unusually large orchestra, and although the Conservatory Orchestra has always been augmented by a number of professional players for its larger public performances, on this occasion it was necessary to engage the smallest number of these players in many years. However, these were all string players!

The Opera School had a most successful season. In the absence of Nicholas Goldschmidt, on leave to the Vancouver Festival of which he has now become the Artistic and Managing Director, the Principal, together with Ernesto Barbini, took over the musical direction of the School. A programme of most unusual excerpts was presented in February, and in April the School entered on a new policy of presenting unfamiliar one-act operas in Hart House Theatre with its own soloists, chorus, and orchestra. These were greeted by press and public as among the highlights of the musical season. Many of the Opera School students took part in the Opera Festival and C.B.C. productions; and the School will now concentrate on its functions as the most significant training unit in the country by offering training in the fundamentals of every aspect of opera, and experience in performance of both traditional and contemporary repertoire.

The very important work of the junior orchestras, which were re-established last year, continues to grow, and it is hoped that, with the new development in string teaching, this will flourish even more in the future. The development referred to is an exciting one in the Conservatory's history. The desperate shortage of string

---

players and students of string instruments has led to desperate measures being taken. Through the good offices of a well-known Foundation a large annual sum of money has been given for three years to provide free study on string instruments for twenty talented children under eleven years of age. This striking innovation will, it is hoped, discover the best string talent in this country at an age starting from which it can be eventually developed to its maximum potential. It will be interesting to see the results of this experiment and it is hoped that this new programme will do something to meet the increasing demand for professional orchestral players in the near future. A curious situation has arisen in Canada, because for some years the supply of professional musicians, and particularly orchestral players, appeared to exceed the opportunities for them to earn a satisfactory living from their talents. Now, however, unless drastic steps are taken to remedy the situation, the demand in this country will soon exceed the supply.

The absurd imbalance in the instruments studied by students today will have to be remedied as soon as possible. After all, the art of music embraces many things and many instruments, and the encouragement of some to the exclusion of almost all others is a policy which makes no sense whatsoever. We must now take drastic steps to redress the balance. It is not merely a question of there not being professional work for a player of this or that instrument when he has qualified. One does not expect every student studying at the Conservatory to enter a professional career. It is the over-all aspect of music as an art that is the important thing and it is well known that if certain elements of any organism are developed to the exclusion of others, those others wither and die.

### *Summer School*
