I am very happy to report that the 1957 Toronto Summer School was in the main a great success, with 161 students taking course instruction (as compared to 95 last season) and 280 students taking private instruction (as compared to 218 last year). The one master-class in piano and the special courses were again only moderately successful and below the estimates, but the notable increase in registration was in the teachers' courses and the opera workshop. There would appear to be three main reasons for this increase: first, a surprising and highly gratifying number of fairly advanced students preparing for the teaching professions, in addition to teachers taking "refresher" courses; secondly, some changes in instructors; and thirdly, the reduction of the Summer School to a three-week period with instruction on six full days of the week, instead of the former four-week period with instruction on only five days of the week.

There was an excellent programme of special concerts and recitals, all well attended, and an unexpected programme by Lois Marshall for Summer School students and staff only. Mr. Schabas did his usual efficient job in helping to plan and handle the affairs of the Summer School, and I cannot pay high enough tribute to Miss Douglas for the spendid initiative she displayed as Social Convener.

### *Concert and Placement Bureau*

The Concert and Placement Bureau, during the past year, continued its work of arranging concerts for students, graduates, and staff members. As in former seasons, the Bureau administered concerts for the duration of the Canadian National Exhibition and from October to April at the Toronto Art Gallery. Several engagements were secured for the Toronto Opera Festival, and a ten-day tour of Ontario was arranged for the Hart House Orchestra, Dean Neel conducting.

At the beginning of the New Year, Dean Neel made a speaking tour of the Western Provinces; and in the early spring members of the Faculty of Music visited centres throughout Ontario, conferring with school heads and guidance counsellors. In both cases the Bureau was responsible for contacts, advance publicity, and travel arrangements. The Bureau Director made numerous trips throughout Ontario, and one visit to the Maritime Provinces.

---

This year's Special Events Series comprised eight concerts—three by the Juilliard Quartet, three by the Amadeus Quartet, and single performances by Leon Goossens, oboe, with Denis Mathews, piano (joint recital), and Ralph Kirkpatrick, harpsichord. Collaborating with the Director of the Faculty of Music, the Bureau assisted in the preparation of a new lecture series, free and open to the public, held on Thursdays at three in the afternoon during most of the academic year. On Thursdays of Special Events concerts, participating artists lectured on programme content. The Series this year attracted increased public support, and greater student attendance, both from the Conservatory and the University as a whole; the lectures also proving popular.

Publicity and administration of student recitals and programmes by school ensembles (Chorus and Orchestra, Conservatory Symphony, Opera at Hart House, etc.) were handled through the Bureau. The department's operations included considerable printed matter: the Monthly Bulletin, yearbooks, brochures, posters (including distribution), a large volume of direct mailing, press liaison, publicity and advertising in newspapers and periodicals, advance publicity for examinations—coast to coast, midwinter and midsummer—and press conferences. Requests for organists, choirmasters, choristers, accompanists, soloists, placement of music teachers, part-time employment for students, housing outside of the residence, and sale of used pianos and other instruments were handled by the staff. The department represented the Conservatory at two Music Teachers' Conventions in Toronto during the Easter season. For the Summer School, 1957, the Director served as assistant to the Principal of the Summer School, and the Bureau also took care of its social activities.

#### *Branch Activities*

The past year has seen a still further increase in the scheme of auditions at the branches. There is no doubt that it has played a considerable part in the raising of standards. Some two thousand students have been heard in audition during the year—a staggering undertaking, which was only made possible by the never flagging zeal of Mr. Ouchterlony, the Supervisor of Branches. This invaluable system has also made possible the much more complete supervision of branch teachers.

As regards registration, this year has seen a levelling off in most of the larger and centrally located branches, while those in the more outlying areas have shown a noticeable increase. Lack of suitable accommodation in some areas has been one of the difficulties encountered.

I would like to take this opportunity of thanking Mrs. Lawson for her splendid and untiring work in the Dean's Office.

BOYD NEEL

### *Report of the Dean of the School of Graduate Studies*

For the first time in recent years there has been a small but significant increase—of the order of five per cent—in the registration in the Graduate School; this may well be a forecast of what we must face in the next few years. If, in spite of increasing pressure of numbers, we are not only to maintain but to enhance the quality of the school, it is essential that we continue to consider, well before the event, what protective measures are necessary.

One obvious step is to scrutinize admission qualifications even more carefully than in the past. One promising development is the practice of the M.A.Sc. Committee in assessing a student's qualifications the summer before registration, course and thesis requirements being considered (as is now the case) immediately after registration. It may well be necessary for the committees administering other degrees to adopt this procedure. One obvious corollary is that applications for admission would be filed at least three months before the start of term—a requirement imposed by many other graduate schools.

---

Another possibility has been considered by the Policy Committee of the Association of Graduate Schools and will be presented at the 1958 meeting of the Association. In brief, a candidate wishing to enrol in a subscribing institution will write examinations sponsored by the Association in November of his senior year. Such examinations would not be of the type with which we are all too familiar, stressing merely factual knowledge, but would rather be designed to test a student's mental ability, imagination, and power to express himself clearly and concisely. The papers after being graded would be submitted to the graduate school in question, which would be free to make any use of the information it desired.

It goes without saying that the quality of the Ph.D. degree is the best criterion of the quality of a graduate school as a whole. It is gratifying to report from first-hand observation of final oral examinations for the degree covering the whole academic spectrum, that never have the demands on the thesis and on its defence been as severe as during this year.

One problem that has become increasingly acute, particularly in certain engineering departments, has been the pressure from industry for advanced training not for degree purposes for employees. Such adult education is a proper university responsibility, but to dilute graduate courses with such students can have disastrous results. After discussion, the Department of University Extension have agreed to sponsor certain courses generally similar to the graduate courses in demand, thus permitting the graduate courses to be restricted to graduate students proper.

Another problem is the would-be graduate student whose qualification or academic record is doubtful. In some cases, the student may be admitted for a probationary year as a graduate student not proceeding to a degree; in others even this is undesirable. With the generous co-operation of the Faculty of Arts, these latter are now refused admission to the School, but are advised to register as special students in Arts, thus being given a chance to make up undergraduate deficiencies and to demonstrate their quality.

There is another special category of Master's candidates, those proceeding to the degree on a part-time basis without a year of full-time residence (sometimes referred to irreverently as the "bits and pieces programme"). This procedure is confined to certain departments in Division I and is of interest primarily to school teachers. In practice it seems to have worked reasonably well. On the other hand it can lead to embarrassment for the School and frustration for the student. For example, a student proceeding in this way to the Master of Commerce degree failed to complete the programme required within the statutory four years and consequently was refused re-registration by the Institute of Business Administration. His petition for reconsideration was refused by the Council of the School and by the Senate, but the fact remains that the situation arose from the difficulty in assessing the quality of a mediocre student's work under such conditions. It is questionable whether it is wise to allow a student to proceed to this degree without a year's full-time residence.

It is a truism to say that a graduate school can be no stronger than its component departments; thus it is essential that extreme care be exercised in establishing a new graduate department. Several applications for the formation of such departments were considered and either deferred or rejected. The exception was the new Department of Medical Biophysics. The question of the terms of reference of the new Department was a complex one, but after exhaustive study by a special committee under the chairmanship of Professor George Duff, to which the School owes a debt of gratitude, the Council at a special meeting approved the report of the committee and established the Department.

The graduate students' loan fund has continued to prove its value and it is gratifying to report that its capital has been increased by \$2,000—a desirable step in view of the increasing demands on the fund.

It is understandable but nevertheless disappointing that there has been no significant increase in the fellowships supported by industry and private donors. In contrast there has been the phenomenal expansion of the Woodrow Wilson Fellowship programme, which now covers not only the social sciences and humanities but

---

the sciences as well. The value of the Fellowship has been increased to \$1,500 plus tuition, and for each Fellowship held in a given institution, the Woodrow Wilson National Fellowship Foundation makes an additional grant of \$1,000 for the support of students (not necessarily previous holders of the Fellowship) in the later years of their graduate work. In addition there is a grant of \$500 to be administered *by the graduate school* for any purpose it deems desirable.

In conclusion, it is hardly necessary to comment on the valuable role played by the Visiting Lecturers' Funds of the two divisions. It would be difficult to overestimate their contribution to the life of the University.

A. R. GORDON

GUEST PROFESSOR IN THE SCHOOL OF GRADUATE STUDIES, 1957-8

*Department of Mathematics*

Dr. Paul Erdos (Michaelmas term).

LECTURES GIVEN UNDER THE SPONSORSHIP OF THE SCHOOL OF GRADUATE STUDIES, 1957-8

DIVISION I

*Department of Anthropology*

Dr. Paul S. Wingert, Columbia University, New York. "The Role of the Mask in African Culture."

*Report of the Dean of the Faculty of Dentistry*

The total registration of students in all categories during the session 1957-8 was 427. This figure compares with 413 and 411 in the two previous sessions. However, two years ago when it was anticipated that the new dental building would be ready for occupancy at the beginning of session 1959-60, the projected enrolment for the past session was set at approximately 450 students. The decline from this figure to 427 is related to the subsequent delay in the commencement of the new building. With building construction now in progress, every effort will be made in September, 1958, to enrol the maximum number of students in the preprofessional and first dental years consistent with the limitations of the existing facilities.

This year's graduating class in dental hygiene, comprising fourteen students, is the largest since the inception of the course in 1951. In reviewing the geographical origin of the students admitted during the seven years since the establishment of the course, it is noted that students have come from five of the ten Canadian provinces. A significant group, approximately 30 per cent of all those admitted to the course, have emanated from Saskatchewan, under the stimulus of the government bursary programme available to dental hygiene students in that province.

The number of applications for admission received each year from holders of British Commonwealth and European dental degrees remains static. During the period 1953-7 the Admissions Committee of the Faculty has considered applications from sixty-one graduates of European dental schools, and sixty-nine graduates of British Commonwealth dental schools. In this five-year period, twenty-three European and twenty-two Commonwealth graduates were admitted, of whom eighteen European and twenty Commonwealth candidates have completed the necessary requirements of from one to three years of study, and have been awarded the Doctor of Dental Surgery degree.

The ratio of dentists to the population in Canada continues to deteriorate. In 1938 the ratio was one dentist to 2,672 people. On January 1, 1958, it was one dentist

---

to 2,981 people. The most serious phase of the twenty-year decline has occurred since January, 1953. At that time the ratio was one dentist to 2,686 people, which was only slightly less than in 1938.

The rate at which dentists are now being graduated gives no cause for optimism. The following comparative figures are rather significant. Sweden, with a ratio of 1:1,700 is presently graduating thirty-eight new dentists each year per million of population. The United States with a ratio of 1:1671 is graduating nineteen new dentists per annum per million of population, while Canada with a ratio of 1:2,981 is graduating only eleven new dentists per million of population each year. The new facilities at Toronto, and those recently provided at Dalhousie University, and the new dental school in Manitoba which enrols its first class this coming session, will do much to alleviate the present serious situation.

It was an exciting moment for members of this division of the University when late in March the excavation for the new dental building was commenced. The new facilities, designed to accommodate 125 students in a class, will fulfil the recommendations made in the 1950 report of the Ontario Health Survey Commission, which called for a minimum of 120 dental graduates each year in the Province of Ontario. Adequate facilities are also included for postgraduate and graduate work, and for an extensive research programme, already well begun, in the Division of Dental Research. Present indications are that while the building may be finished by the late summer of 1959, installation of equipment will delay our occupation of the new facilities until Christmas, 1959.

During the 1957-8 session the Faculty Council reviewed the mounting list of final examination papers. With the introduction of new courses of instruction, whether they comprise a short series of lectures or a series of lectures supplemented with laboratory or clinical assignments, the department concerned is usually desirous of setting a final examination paper. The plan of consolidation adopted by the Council embodies the principle that in the final year of the course, grading for which is weighted in favour of the clinical fields, a series of four comprehensive papers be written, rather than twice that number on segments of main course areas. A policy was adapted to the preclinical courses in the second and third years, with little or no change recommended in the examination schedule for the preprofessional and first dental years. The changes resulted in an over-all reduction of 20 per cent in the number of final papers.

One of the serious problems faced by students in a professional school is their isolation from the main university community. Two factors are responsible for this situation. First, in a large university with buildings spread over a wide area, students in one division fail to meet students in another, for geographical reasons. Secondly, the heavy schedule of lectures, laboratory classes, and clinical assignments leaves little or no time free during the day for intermingling with students of other divisions within the University. Under these circumstances, adequate provision of residences, where students from all faculties may mix freely with each other, is imperative. During the past two or three sessions, the high quality of work done by students of professional faculties residing in Devonshire House underscores the significant contribution being made through residence life. In the future, dental students will be further isolated geographically, but this dilemma can be minimized and at the same time the quality of academic achievement maintained or improved, if ample provision is made for the expansion of residences.

Our concern for the future supply of good students seeking admission to dentistry has been shared by all the dental schools in Canada. Under the guidance of the Council on Dental Education of the Canadian Dental Association, a nation-wide recruitment programme has been initiated. Coupled with the consideration of this matter, concern is also expressed over the cost of dental education. In addition to the fees for tuition, books, and other incidentals common to all students, the dental student is required to purchase \$1,000 worth of instruments during the four years of the course. It is true that the non-consumable items are carried into practice, but the burden imposed by this expenditure during his student days can be critical. It

---

is anticipated that some method will have to be formulated whereby a student can rent the instruments used during the course.

The alternative would be to provide a larger measure of financial assistance. During 1957-8, student aid in this Faculty, not including statutory awards, was provided for ninety-four students (approximately 25 per cent). The average assistance was \$229 per student. A further group of sixteen students received tuition fees and subsistence from the army. These are vital matters which merit our most serious consideration.

It is with profound regret that we record the passing during the early part of the session of Dr. M. N. Rockman, Assistant Professor in charge of Dental Radiology. Dr. Rockman's loyal service and devotion to duty will keep his memory fresh for years to come. In recognition of his contribution to the field of dental research, Dr. G. Nikiforuk was made an honorary member of the staff of the Research Institute in the Hospital for Sick Children. Dr. Egil Harvold, of Oslo, Norway, has been appointed Head of the Department of Orthodontics. Dr. Harvold was associated with the Cleft Palate Clinic at the Hospital for Sick Children during the period 1951-3. At the conclusion of the session Dr. G. V. Morton retires after serving the Faculty with distinction as a part-time clinical teacher for thirty-seven years.

It is with grateful appreciation that I record, once again, sincere thanks to all members of the academic and administrative staffs for their pleasant and helpful co-operation throughout the session.

R. G. ELLIS

### *Report of the Dean of the Faculty of Pharmacy*

During the past two sessions the curriculum in pharmacy has been under review by the Faculty Council. Certain changes have been effected in the current session and more have been approved for next session. These modifications have been made generally to provide better sequences of courses in several departments. At the same time the Faculty has been quite cognizant of its responsibility to keep closely in touch with the changing practices and needs of the profession in the light of scientific, economic, and social developments affecting the various branches of pharmacy.

In making certain of the curricular revisions, the Faculty Council has given attention to the matter of specialization in the fourth year. The curriculum has been designed to permit fourth-year students to select options which have been regarded as particularly applicable or basic to the respective "fields" or branches of pharmacy—for example, retail pharmacy, hospital pharmacy, industrial pharmacy. It is agreed that some degree of specialization is necessary, but how much can be effective and should be provided at the undergraduate level has to be determined. The subject is being kept under review.

Reference was made in the report for the previous session to the importance of provision for some specialization in the field of hospital pharmacy. The demand for pharmacists in hospitals is growing more rapidly than in any other branch of the profession. During two years—between 1955 and 1957—the two successive Fuller-Stauffer Surveys of Hospital Pharmacy revealed that the number of hospitals employing full-time pharmacists increased by approximately 75 per cent. Each year there is an unfilled demand for graduates in pharmacy with specialized training and experience to assume posts of chief pharmacist in large hospitals. The introduction of the Ontario Hospital Care Insurance Plan in 1959 seems certain to increase this demand.

Specialized education for this branch is provided elsewhere in three ways: (i) by including certain elective undergraduate courses; (ii) by a special graduate degree programme, to the Master's level; (iii) by a combination of either or part of the first two with a period of directed internship in a hospital, the university through its school of pharmacy co-operating. An effective internship programme in hospital pharmacy, just as in medicine, requires the defining of proper standards respecting

---

both the hospital and the programme. In addition, direction and close supervision of the internes are essential. Such standards have been evolved by the Canadian Society of Hospital Pharmacists and the Canadian Conference of Pharmaceutical Faculties. This Faculty has been co-operating in establishing such standards and in working toward the inauguration of hospital pharmacy internships in Ontario. The Faculty is also studying combined academic and internship programmes which might be established by the University.

The examination results in pharmacy were significantly better than in any recent session as shown by the following comparative composite figures for all years combined. The total number of candidates is shown in brackets under the year.
