The improvement was particularly marked in the first three years, the graduation year showing quite similar results to those of former years. While it is difficult to identify the cause, there is no evidence of a lowering of standards. Probably a combination of factors is responsible, some of which can be recognized.

As reported elsewhere in some detail the research programme in the Faculty continues to develop steadily although not as rapidly as might be desired. Our research laboratory accommodation has become quite inadequate for the number of workers. Virtually no technical assistance has been available. And, of course, more research fellowships and grants are needed. The first of these handicaps will be overcome under the expansion programme by the provision for this Faculty as recommended by the Advisory Planning Committee. Some technical assistance is to be provided next session and it is hoped that more may be forthcoming. The appointment of Dr. Stephan Kandel as Research Associate has bolstered the programme. Dr. Kandel was formerly in the Organic Chemistry Institute, University of Budapest, Budapest.

New annual awards established during the session are: Tamblyn Entrance Scholarship, \$500; Plaza Drug Stores Scholarship, \$150; and Pfizer Fellowship in Hospital Pharmacy, \$500. In addition the Warner-Lambert Research Fellowship in Pharmacy, \$1,500, was established in the School of Graduate Studies. The Canadian Foundation for the Advancement of Pharmacy again provided summer research grants totalling \$2,000. In gratefully acknowledging these generous gifts, we would express the hope that still further support of the research programme may be forthcoming. Additional fellowships will be essential if continued progress is to be made.

As has been mentioned in previous reports, members of the staff of this Faculty are not only fulfilling very creditably their intramural obligations to the University but they are also making significant contributions to Canadian pharmacy. Some only of their professional duties and offices are as follows: Professor G. C. Walker is Chairman of the Canadian Conference of Pharmaceutical Faculties and has been a guest lecturer at Civil Defence College; Professor H. J. Fuller is chairman of the Committee on Pharmaceutical Economics of the Canadian Pharmaceutical Association (C.Ph.A.); Professor R. M. Baxter is Secretary-Treasurer of the Canadian Conference of Pharmaceutical Faculties and was Local Chairman for the (American) Plant Science Seminar, 1957; Professor G. R. Paterson is Director of the Canadian Academy for the History of Pharmacy; Professor D. R. Kennedy and Professor F. W. Teare are editors in different capacities for the Canadian Conference of Pharmaceutical Faculties; Mrs. I. E. Stauffer is Chairman of the Policy Planning Committee of the C.Ph.A.

For their continuing loyalty and co-operation I am pleased to record sincere appreciation to all members of the staff, both teaching and administrative.

F. N. HUGHES

---

## Report of the Acting Director of the School of Architecture

The Committee on Selection of a Director for the School of Architecture recommended Dr. Thomas Howarth, B.Arch. (Manchester), Ph.D. (Glasgow) for the directorship of the School. His appointment has been approved and Dr. Howarth will assume his duties on or about July 1, 1958. Dr. Howarth is eminently qualified both in training and in experience for the post and should continue the excellent leadership the School has experienced for many years under Professors Wright and Madill.

The work of the School of Architecture has been carried on during the 1957-8 session in four different locations. The office, library, and lecture rooms have been in the Applied Science Building; first-, second-, and third-year architectural design in Examination Hall; fourth-year architectural design and the Division of Town and Regional Planning at 74 St. George Street, and fifth-year design in the Hygiene Building. This physical separation of our students has been detrimental to the morale of both staff and students. After a careful survey of the space available and lengthy discussions on the problems involved, the School requested permission to occupy the newly acquired Victoria Curling Rink on Huron Street. Although this does not offer a complete solution to our problem, we will be able to house the first-, second-, third-, and fourth-year architectural design in the Rink building, retaining 74 St. George Street for fifth-year design and Town and Regional Planning.

I feel that the proximity of the architectural library to the drafting rooms in the Victoria Rink building will be an added incentive to the research which is so necessary for students in architecture and town planning. The centralizing of offices, lecture rooms, library, and drafting rooms should improve the *esprit de corps* of staff and students. At best, however, this can be only an interim solution to the problem of physical accommodations that has faced the School for many years. The work in art will be done under a serious handicap, and the provision for adequate seminar rooms, construction laboratories, materials rooms, exhibition space, etc., can be provided only in a building designed for a School of Architecture.

The problem of physical accommodations made it necessary for us to limit the admissions to the first year in Architecture for the session 1957-8 to sixty students who were selected from a group of nearly ninety applicants. Our basis of selection was Grade 13 standing. While this method of selection of students in this field may be questioned, it seemed to us to be the most logical one. However, even with this careful scrutiny of applications, our failure rate at the end of the first year is 29.3 per cent. Since we will be faced with a similar problem of space even with our move into Victoria Rink, the limit of admissions to the first year has again been set at sixty students for the session 1958-9.

The Senate of the University has approved the awards made by Colonna of Canada Limited to students of the School of Architecture as follows: in the first-, second-, and third-year classes, an award of \$50.00 for the purchase of books to a student submitting the best essay in the History of Architecture course of each year, and \$100.00 to a student in the fifth year for a written thesis on some phase of modern architecture.

The Division of Town and Regional Planning has had an enrolment of twenty students representing several fields of undergraduate training as well as many nationalities. It is recommended that this curriculum be expanded to a two-year postgraduate course leading to a graduate degree.

There is a serious need in Canada for a degree course in landscape architecture. Such a course should be under the direction of the School of Architecture for there are many subjects in the School which would be included in the requirements for this degree. A proposed curriculum has been submitted to the President's office and I would respectfully recommend it to his attention.

I cannot close this report without expressing my deep appreciation to Dr. Sidney E. Smith for the opportunity that has been afforded me to take an active part in the affairs of the School of Architecture and the University during the past year. It has

---

been an enriching experience. The staff of the School has offered the most friendly co-operation on all occasions. I have been impressed also by the high quality of the students' work and their ability to carry on under the handicap of widely separated working quarters.

MILTON S. OSBORNE

### *Report of the Director of the School of Physical and Health Education*

The enrolment in the School has increased as was anticipated last year. The staff have been particularly pleased with the academic quality of the students entering the School in the past two years, which has been reflected in the lower number of failures in the annual examinations. The rearrangement of subject-matter in the School curriculum has now been completed and it is hoped that the sequence of courses will provide a better correlation, particularly in the professional subjects.

Much study and discussion have resulted from the decision of the Department of Education to alter the categories of specialists' certificates for secondary school teachers. Study of these changes is continuing, but no change is at present contemplated in the present three-year Bachelor degree course which leads to a Type A specialist certificate. Graduates of the School are able to continue their course of study in order to obtain a Bachelor of Arts degree. In this way, some students will qualify for a higher category in secondary school certification. It is expected that the present increased enrolment will help to relieve the acute shortage of teachers in this field of specialization.

The completion of the Women's Athletic Building will greatly facilitate the teaching of the students of this School. Staff changes have become necessary owing to the resignation of Miss Mitzi Fenn who has returned to the University of Texas for further study, and Miss Joan Rogers who has accepted a position with Mount Royal College in Calgary. The contributions made by the two new appointees to the staff, Miss Eleanor Hopkins and Miss Rose Hill, have been greatly appreciated.

The members of the Council of the School have greatly appreciated the interest and help of the Acting President and the Vice-President.

J. H. EBBS

### *Report of the Director of the School of Social Work*

This report is written with the knowledge that the School's first candidate for the degree of Doctor of Social Work has successfully completed all requirements. He is already *en route* to Japan where he will become a member of the faculty of the School of Social Work at Kwansei Gakuin University in Nishinomiya, Japan. Before the end of the calendar year 1958 the Director of the School, who is scheduled to attend the International Congress of Schools of Social Work in Tokyo, will be privileged to present to Mr. Lloyd Graham a doctoral hood, the gift of the Alumni Association of the School. All concerned regard this achievement with pride and satisfaction.

The foregoing reference to the Alumni Association deserves expansion. The sturdy quality of this loyal group was never more manifest than at the Alumni Breakfast, held in Montreal in June at the time of the Canadian Conference of Social Work. All regions and most provinces in Canada were represented, together with one person from beyond our borders. As those who receive issues of the Alumni Newsletter, *Social Scope*, know, the School is fortunate in having an active Alumni. Their efforts are centred on interpretation and recruiting, financial assistance to students through scholarships and loan funds, annual receptions for the graduating class, and

---

special events of one kind and another. At present the Alumni Association is laying the ground work to help the School plan a worthy celebration of its fiftieth anniversary a few years hence.

For the coming academic year two graduates of the School are being appointed to the teaching staff, Mr. Jack Byles of Ann Arbor, Michigan, and Mr. Ben Lappin of Toronto. Mr. Byles has been serving as group work supervisor at the Hawthorn Center, Northville, Michigan. This has involved field instruction for second-year students from the School of Social Work at the University of Michigan. Since 1952 he has been specializing in the study and treatment of emotionally disturbed children. Mr. Lappin has had extensive experience in the field of community organization. He has taught the introductory course in community organization for two years since the appointment of Dr. Murray Ross as Vice-President of the University, and he will assume enlarged teaching responsibilities and serve also as secretary to the staff. He is presently pursuing advanced studies toward his doctorate in social work.

In addition to the foregoing, two other appointments may now be recorded. Mrs. Marjory Seeley will join the staff as Assistant Professor. Mrs. Seeley is a graduate of the Manitoba School of Social Work and the School of Social Service Administration of the University of Chicago where she taught for five years. She has had considerable experience as a professional social worker in Winnipeg and Chicago in family welfare, medical and research-oriented clinical settings. Dr. Howard Jones will join the staff as Visiting Lecturer and Research Associate. Dr. Jones holds a University of London diploma in Public Administration, a Mental Health certificate in Psychiatric Social Work from the London School of Economics and his doctorate from the University of London. He has had twelve years as a practising social worker, latterly specializing in work with maladjusted children. During the last few years Dr. Jones has given significant leadership in the correctional field. A year ago, at his initiative, the first workshop on group dynamics was held at the University of Leicester in co-operation with the Tavistock Institute of Human Relations.

Professor Opal Boynton retired on June 30th, 1958, after twelve years of devoted service to the School. During the last two years, Professor Boynton was chairman of the group work teaching staff and carried major responsibilities for field consultation in this area. Her special competence was widely recognized. During the year under review she served as a consultant in Jamaica.

Several quite important changes were incorporated in the Calendar of the School during the year. For many years admission to the School was restricted to applicants whose undergraduate studies had included four prerequisites in the social sciences. These courses are not now required. The curriculum of the School is based on the assumption that students have a substantial core of undergraduate courses in the biological and social sciences. Students who have not done this preparatory work are expected to acquire the background of knowledge through independent reading, or through supplementary courses. If students have serious deficiencies in this regard, they may be advised to enrol in some undergraduate courses, thus lengthening their time of study.

A second change of some consequence is a discontinuance of the classification *occasional student*. Students not proceeding to a degree may now be admitted as *special students* in one or two categories: (1) students undertaking a planned programme of professional studies as full-time or part-time students, and (2) students wishing to take specific courses not exceeding four hours a week in any one academic year. Among other requirements for *special students* the minimum educational attainment ordinarily required will be Ontario Grade XIII certificate covering at least five subjects or evidence of equivalent standing, and completion of substantial courses at university level in four of seven subjects: anthropology, biology, economics, English, political science, psychology, and sociology. In exceptional circumstances arrangements may be made to satisfy these requirements through a qualifying examination set by the School.

After extensive study and discussion within the Faculty of the School it was decided to consult colleagues both in the University and in the welfare field on the

---

advisability of introducing block-field instruction in the second year. Educational, administrative, and financial implications of such a development will be reviewed with appropriate governing bodies of the University during the academic year 1958-9 with a view to implementation in 1959-60 if a formal plan is presented and approved.

Research requirements for the M.S.W. degree together with certain aspects of their administration have been subjected to most thoughtful examination by the Research Committee of the teaching staff. In the process the Committee had access to the *Report on Policies in Graduate Education* prepared by a Committee of Deans of Graduate Schools. Without in any way relaxing academic requirements it is hoped to make it feasible for a student to complete his research project during the summer term of his second year at the School.

Substantial progress has been made in identifying basic concepts in social work practice now taught in separate courses and in working toward greater integration between certain classroom courses and between classroom and field instruction. Some experimentation is now planned along these lines for the coming year. Advanced courses at the 300 level are attracting graduates of the School. A special bulletin was released in the fall of 1957 describing the advanced studies programme. The response and the performance have been most encouraging.

Several research documents and basic work papers were prepared for use at the 1957 session of the Round Table on Man and Industry, notably a scholarly contribution by Sir Geoffrey Vickers, Chief Consultant to the Round Table, on "Criteria of Well Being." Further research has gone forward in preparation for the third and final session of this significant undertaking scheduled for November, 1958.

In a professional school where research and application are so closely linked and where consultation in the field, whether in relation to social policy or to social practice, rests so heavily upon scholarly study and research, it is difficult to draw a line between research and formal publications, on the one hand, and research memoranda and expert consultation, on the other hand. The fact of the matter is, of course, that the operating agency in the community, whether it be a government department or a private agency, is to the social work educator what a laboratory is to the educator in engineering, or a hospital is to the faculty of a medical school. It is not surprising, therefore, that faculty members of the School of Social Work are extensively and intensively involved in a combination of community service, extension education, and consultation.

During 1957-58, senior members of the Faculty have given professional consultation in widely separated parts of Canada, from British Columbia to Newfoundland. One technical monograph was prepared at the request of the United Nations and is being incorporated in an official publication. Papers were prepared by two faculty members for the Social Security Conference convened by the Canadian Welfare Council in Ottawa in January, 1958. Members of the staff gave consultation to the Mental Health Division of the Ontario Department of Health, and to the Tyhurst Committee of the Scientific Planning Council of the Canadian Mental Health Association. Regular periodic consultation was given to several hospitals, general as well as psychiatric.

The record of leadership and formal participation in conferences included the Canadian Conference on Children, the first Canadian Mental Health Institute, the Ontario Seminar for Social Workers in Psychiatric Settings, a training conference for staff in the Indian Affairs Branch of the Department of Northern Affairs, the Canadian Conference on Education, the Canadian Conference on Social Work, the National Conference on Social Work (U.S.A.), the Senior Staff Training Course at the headquarters of Central Mortgage and Housing, and the National Association of Home and School and Parent-Teacher Federation. Faculty members served as members of boards and committees of seventeen local, twenty provincial and national, and eight international organizations. Apart from scholarly contributions in professional journals, social work and social work education was interpreted in feature articles in newspapers and magazines and over television and radio.

---

In concluding this report I wish to express on behalf of myself and my colleagues our deep sense of indebtedness to Dr. Sidney Smith for his friendly understanding and unfailing support during his distinguished presidency of this University. We shall never forget his buoyant spirit, his radiant confidence, and his unflagging efforts on behalf of the highest and best in the traditions and aspirations of this academic community. With like enthusiasm we record our sincere appreciation of the dignity and devotion which Dean Moffatt St. A. Woodside brought to his position as Acting President. He and his senior associates at Simcoe Hall have carried an enormously difficult combination of responsibilities with impressive effectiveness. And now we welcome with warmth, confidence, and pride our new President to whom we pledge nothing less than our best.

CHARLES E. HENDRY

### *Report of the Director of the School of Nursing*

Although its seeds were sown in 1920, 1933 marked the beginning of the School of Nursing as it exists today, and 1958 its twenty-fifth anniversary. A brief summary of its growth seems indicated in this report.

In 1920 the Canadian Red Cross Society subsidized for three years in a university in each province an experimental course to prepare nurses for the new field of public health nursing. In the University of Toronto there was established the Department of Public Health Nursing in which a one-year course for graduate nurses led to a certificate in Public Health Nursing. At the end of the three-year period the University took over full responsibility for this course.

Only brief experience with this first course was needed to show that for best results in the preparation of public health nurses the subject must be introduced much earlier in the general training, in fact from the beginning. The year 1926 therefore saw the beginning of a new arrangement of the preparation for public health nursing. A four-year course was established, with two years at the University and twenty-eight months at the Toronto General Hospital. This divided arrangement was still not completely satisfactory, and was discontinued from 1933 when the present School of Nursing was developed.

Meanwhile, in 1928, a course for graduate nurses called "Teaching and Supervision" was approved, to qualify for executive and teaching positions in hospitals and hospital schools of nursing. This course was placed temporarily in the Department of University Extension.

In 1933 the University made available the building at 7 Queen's Park, and, with help from the Rockefeller Foundation, the School of Nursing came into existence, absorbing the three existing but physically scattered courses. The basic course was reorganized so that the general and professional subjects were carried on concurrently in a diploma course of thirty-nine months. The School was accepted as a "training school for nurses" under the Nurse Registration Act of Ontario, and from this time gave the entire training for nursing in its own right. In 1943 the four-year degree course leading to the degree of Bachelor of Science in Nursing was established.

The School has continued to offer certificate courses for hospital school graduates in public health, in nursing education, and in hospital nursing service. The proportion of basic to post-basic students has however changed: the two groups are now about equal in size. Basic enrolment, though still not large, has increased considerably; but also it is much quicker and easier for the graduate of a hospital school to obtain a bachelor's degree at some other universities.

Twenty-five years ago the University of Toronto, along with other leading universities in North America, accepted the principle that nurses need to be educated women. From the beginning of its first Department of Public Health Nursing, the

---

School of Nursing here may be said to have been occupied in research. Certainly it has continuously broken new ground and given leadership in nursing education in Canada and in fact throughout the world. Although it has been offering an experimental training for nursing since 1926, and was established as a fully recognized school for nurses in 1933, it was not until 1943 that a degree was requested. The ten years between 1943 and 1953 were spent in finding, organizing, and adding to the usual content of nursing, and making it comparable in value with the other subjects given.

In the twenty-five years since 1933 the changes in medicine and nursing have been enormous. The basis of nursing remains general "supportive" care—helping the patient to rally his natural forces for recovery, and to make use of the therapy prescribed. The concept of this supportive care has however broadened to emphasize emotional support and health teaching. In addition, as has been pointed out by Dr. Esther Brown of the Russell Sage Foundation, the nurse has traditionally acted as physician's assistant in two particular ways: (1) she is responsible for continuous critical observation of the patient for changes in his condition, and for the decision as to what should be reported to the doctor, and when; (2) she assists the physician in carrying out technical procedures which are considered too complex or critical to be delegated. However, the constant addition of new procedures results in a continuous process of handing over to the nurse procedures that were formerly carried out only by physicians. This process has been greatly accelerated in the past few years. The need for an understanding of the physiological and chemical principles underlying these new procedures, machines, and medication is very great. One of the pieces of research most needed is to find the content from several of the physical sciences which is needed as a basis for nursing. It seems obvious that this can only be done within a university.

It has been suggested by a university president that a profession comes into the university when the demands of society are such that it *should* have a scholarly body of knowledge to which it can add continuously. The School of Nursing is grateful for the conditions under which it has been given its start in an undergraduate degree course; not only for the unstinted help which we have had from many departments, but for our form of organization and the expectation that we shall meet university standards. We have not wanted degrees which cannot be taken seriously. We do feel now, fifteen years later, as nurses in general do, the need for an opportunity to do research.

A small beginning has been made this year on a staff research programme, two members of staff having undertaken two small studies. In the hospital field a study of the nursing care of the cardiac patient is under way in co-operation with the Toronto Western Hospital; in the public health field, a study of nursing visits to newborn infants in co-operation with the East York Leaside Health Unit. It is hoped that these studies will be reported on in the near future. Meanwhile there is a continuous demand upon us from graduate nurse students for opportunities to undertake research in various aspects of nursing care. This of course awaits the establishment of a Master's degree in Nursing.

The teaching staff has continued to give much service in helping to meet nursing needs in the community. In October Professor Uprichard acted as consultant in education at a five-day institute in St. John for instructors from hospital schools of nursing, given by the New Brunswick Association of Registered Nurses, and was guest speaker at the annual meeting of the Association. In January Professor McPhedran conducted a five-day institute for nursing instructors, also given by the New Brunswick Association. The demand for committee work is reaching alarming proportions, and the extent to which this is justified will need careful consideration. There is a growing demand from practising nurses for night classes.

Miss Kathleen King will be on leave of absence for further study in the United States next year.

N. D. FIDLER

---

## *Report of the Director of the School of Hygiene*

The major development in the School of Hygiene during the session 1957-8 has been the review of several of the courses of instruction offered to postgraduate students. Since the opening of the building in 1927, the School of Hygiene has provided postgraduate instruction in the broad field of public health, and more recently in specialized aspects such as hospital administration, industrial hygiene, veterinary public health, dental public health, nutrition, and microbiology. With the present expansion in Canada and elsewhere of health services of all kinds, the time seems opportune to review the objectives and content of our various diploma and certificate courses.

For example, the School has for some years provided a two-year course leading to the Diploma in Hospital Administration. It is evident that with the implementation of Hospital Insurance Plans in Canada there will be a steady and increasing demand for university graduates trained in this field. For some time, our staff has desired to improve the content of instruction offered in the second year of this course, and an opportunity has now been provided to do this. The W. K. Kellogg Foundation has provided funds for a review of the course, with special reference to the content of the second year. Mr. K. S. McLaren, M.Ed., one of our own graduates, has been appointed to direct this project, with the rank of Assistant Professor.

The University of Toronto has offered since 1943 a Diploma in Industrial Hygiene (D.I.H.). This Diploma is under the jurisdiction of the Faculty of Medicine, but most of the teaching is done by the staff of the School of Hygiene, especially in the Department of Physiological Hygiene. A committee has now reviewed the objectives and content of the D.I.H. course in detail, and has made several important recommendations. Thus, it is recommended that the teaching now relate not only to the specialized branch of the field known as "industrial hygiene," which covers the effect on man of temperature, light, radiation, sound, ventilation, and other environmental factors, but also the whole broad field of "industrial health"—the health supervision of persons employed in commerce and industry. It is further recommended that this course be offered as a full-time programme in one academic year as well as on a part-time basis over two consecutive years. Applicants for the course will no longer be expected to have taken postgraduate instruction in public health, as for example in the course leading to the Diploma in Public Health. It is hoped that this new and much broader course will fill the need for an academic training programme for physicians who intend to practise industrial medicine. Already there has been an encouraging response to the new course.

The oldest established course offered by the School of Hygiene is that leading to the Diploma in Public Health; this course, likewise, is under the jurisdiction of the Faculty of Medicine. Another committee has begun to review the curriculum for this course. The D.P.H. has been the accepted hallmark of a qualified Medical Officer of Health in Canada for over forty years. In some provinces it is compulsory for the health officer to hold the D.P.H., and in all it is regarded as highly desirable. Accordingly, the curriculum can only be revised after very careful study. To initiate this work, a survey will first be undertaken of the present duties of health officers in Canada and in those parts of the West Indies, especially Jamaica, accustomed to sending physicians to us for training.

In keeping with this policy of reviewing teaching programmes, a group of staff members and other professional persons interested in the field known as veterinary public health has reviewed part of the curriculum for the Diploma in Veterinary Public Health course. It has become apparent that in Canada and several other countries, many of the duties in this field, for example the control of animal diseases transmissible to man, such as rabies, tuberculosis, and brucellosis, are carried out by departments of agriculture and not by departments of public health. Suitable modifications have therefore been introduced into the curriculum, and it is hoped that the Diploma in Veterinary Public Health course will in future provide valuable instruc-

---

tion for veterinarians who propose to work not only in departments of public health, but also in departments of agriculture, in Canada and overseas. This course is likely to provide valuable instruction for overseas candidates sponsored by the World Health Organization, as few universities offer this programme.

Much attention has been paid during the session 1957-8 to the provision of additional training facilities for graduates wishing to specialize in the important field of medical microbiology. A new course, which is to prepare candidates for the Diploma in Bacteriology, has now received Senate approval and will start in September, 1958. Enrolment will be limited to ten persons, and preference will be given to physicians, veterinarians, and science graduates with extensive experience in microbiology. It is hoped that this course will provide a number of well-trained medical bacteriologists for present vacancies, and for the new positions that will have to be created with the increasing demands on laboratories when hospital insurance and diagnostic service programmes go into effect in Canada at an early date.

It will be apparent that considerable attention is being devoted by the staff members of the School of Hygiene to a critical examination of their primary role as teachers of postgraduate students in medicine and certain related branches of knowledge.

During the year, the staff engaged in a new type of educational venture, when for three days in February a Refresher Course in Public Health and Preventive Medicine was held in the School building. The administrative arrangements for this course were under the competent direction of Dr. Ian Macdonald, Director of the Division of Postgraduate Medical Education, Faculty of Medicine. About seventy physicians attended, representing public health work at the federal, provincial, and municipal levels, as well as the armed services and general practice. This course evidently served a real need and it will be established as an annual event.

I have again to acknowledge the whole-hearted support of the teaching staff of the School, both full-time and part-time. There is a very special "atmosphere" in the School, to which graduates frequently refer, and this is largely a result of the very close and friendly contacts that exist between senior staff and students. This feeling of attachment to the School has recently been expressed by the formal constitution of the graduates of the School of Hygiene into the "University of Toronto, School of Hygiene Alumni Association." This group has already shown keen interest in the School by establishing the Donald T. Fraser Memorial Medal, to be awarded each year to the outstanding student in the field of public health attending courses in the School of Hygiene. This year the Donald T. Fraser Medal has been awarded to Dr. K. I. G. Benson, a member of the D.P.H. class and now working in British Columbia. The other major award in the School, the Robert Wood Johnson Award in Hospital Administration, has been awarded to Mr. M. Katz.

Dr. Colin G. Hunter resigns as Head of the Department of Physiological Hygiene at the end of the current session to take up an important appointment with the Shell industrial organization in England. Our good wishes go with him, as do our thanks for his whole-hearted efforts to improve the teaching programme in industrial health. The loss of Dr. Hunter, after little more than a year in office, might have seriously delayed our new programme of instruction in industrial health. Fortunately, Dr. J. Grant Cunningham, who has taught in the School for many years, has agreed to serve as Head of the Department of Physiological Hygiene for the forthcoming session. He will continue as Head of the Division of Industrial Health, Department of Health for Ontario.

Much of this report concerns the graduate courses of instruction offered by the School of Hygiene in the broad field of medical endeavour known as public health. These activities, together with research, constitute the main justification for the existence of the School of Hygiene as an independent administrative unit on the campus of the University of Toronto. The research work of the staff has been actively prosecuted during the year, as witnessed by an extensive list of publications. During the year, grants have been received from the Defence Research Board, the National Cancer Institute, the National Research Council, the National Health Grants Pro-

---

gramme of the Ontario and Canadian governments, the J. P. Bickell Foundation, the W. K. Kellogg Foundation, The Rockefeller Foundation, the University of Toronto Advisory Committee on Scientific Research, Hoffmann-La Roche, Inc., and the International Nickel Company of Canada, Ltd.

A. J. RHODES

### *Report of the Director of the Institute of Child Study*

The five-year programme of research in child mental health under grant #605-5-147 from the Department of Health and Welfare of the Federal Government terminates this June. At present our efforts are directed towards compiling a comprehensive report reviewing the progress which has been made during this period. It is as yet too early to outline the key findings. However, two facts have already made an impression on our professional colleagues: the fact that our central interest has been focussed on the development of the *well* child, and the fact that we have defined research extremely broadly so as to include all the ways (from rigid experimental design to teachers' insightful observations) by which accurate knowledge may be obtained about children.

During the five years our nursery and elementary schools have developed to serve two purposes: laboratories in which observations of children's development in physique, intellect, learning, social and emotional experiences may be made; and classrooms in which an environment conducive to the promotion of good mental health may be established and demonstrated.

The director and staff of the Institute have been more than gratified that the Board of Governors are taking over the financing of the entire Institute and thus making it a truly integral part of the University. Plans for this new era of our existence are already astir. We foresee an expansion of our teaching programme and a modification of our research efforts towards increased co-operation with our colleagues from other departments. At present arrangements are being made for a clinical service, and careful consideration is being given to expanded facilities for our activities as a resource centre to members of the community involved in work with children.

W. E. BLATZ

### *Report of the Director of the Institute of Business Administration*

In the session 1957-8 there were thirty-two full-time students enrolled in the graduate course in Business Administration leading to the degree of Master of Commerce. There were, in addition, 256 part-time students enrolled in evening classes proceeding to the same degree. This represents an increase of 23 per cent for full-time students while the number of part-time students remained about the same as in previous years. This is a very satisfactory result as any increase in part-time registration would increase the size of some classes to a level that would be undesirable for good graduate work, while our full-time classes should benefit from additional students. We expect further increases in full-time enrolment in future years. Sixty-four students are enrolled in our 1958 summer session.

The Institute has continued to develop its non-degree evening programme for men and women who have assumed or are about to assume positions of responsibility in the business community. In addition to the core subjects of Administration, Economics, Finance, Human Relations, Marketing and Production, classes were offered in Marketing and Distribution Research, Industrial Relations, and Personnel Psychology. The total registration for the non-credit programme was 220, representing 107 firms. Most classes were oversubscribed.

---

Special invitation seminars were arranged by the Institute during the term. Sir Noel Hall, Principal of the Administrative Staff College, Henley-on-Thames, England, conducted two seminars, one for graduate students and one for senior business executives, on executive development programmes in England. A conference was arranged with leaders from government, labour, management, the judiciary, and universities to discuss problems of conciliation and arbitration personnel.

The staff of the Institute and professors from other faculties who give courses in the Institute were guests of the Bell Telephone Company of Canada at a unique Faculty-Management Conference. The various phases of operations were described by senior officers of the company, and all aspects were discussed by faculty and management. We are indebted to the Bell Telephone Company for an interesting and beneficial experience.

We regret the resignation of three members of our staff: Dr. Monieson has accepted an appointment at Wharton School of Finance and Commerce, University of Pennsylvania; Dr. Livingston and Dr. Edwards are transferring to positions in business. Mr. W. Allan Beckett, M.A., was appointed Assistant Professor in September, 1957.

T. C. GRAHAM

### *Report of the Director of University Extension*

During the 1957-8 session, the Department of University Extension continued its recent policy of limiting the size of classes and of discontinuing classes considered to be not typical of university offerings. Because of this policy the total enrolment dropped from 18,341 in 1956-7 to 16,772 in 1957-8; no one is worried about this small decrease if, as we believe, the quality of education was improved.

In the General Course (Department of Extension) the most significant developments were the clarification of admission requirements for mature students and the disappearance of the "occasional" student category. Full admission requirements are now mandatory for students under twenty-five years of age; those over twenty-five receive some consideration, and those over thirty may be admitted, at least on probation, regardless of admission requirements. The General Course Committee also spent considerable time clarifying the policy pertaining to correspondence preparation for students taking courses in summer session. And the Committee decided to offer Russian, on a trial basis, during the 1958-9 session.

The Evening Course in Business was again popular. This year eighty-nine students—almost three times as many as in any previous year—gained their certificates after three years of serious study. Because of large enrolments during the past few years (502 in 1957-8), there are likely to be at least a hundred graduates per year from now on. Neither the recent stiffening of admission requirements nor the increase in fees has discouraged enrolment.

Since this course has been so successful, and since many students taking shorter non-credit courses have felt the need of a longer course with the discipline of examinations, a sub-committee of the Senate's Committee on University Extension has been appointed to examine the possibility of new certificate or diploma courses being initiated under the administration of the Department of University Extension. Many people who do not wish a full degree course, or who are graduates from one course and are seeking education in another field, would welcome the opportunity to study for three or four years in English, history, economics or psychology, etc.

The Evening Tutorial division maintained its high standard as it offered a large variety of cultural subjects and as it co-operated with other university departments and off-campus organizations to satisfy intellectually hungry adults. An enrolment of 5,057 students demanded the services of 120 instructors using classrooms in nine university buildings.

---

As an indication of the trend in recent years to more typical university subject-matter, the new courses included Canadian-American Politics, History of Relations of Soviet Russia with the Western World, How to Approach Poetry, Law and the Layman, Geology, Mineralogy, the Opera, and Modern European History.

Direct collaboration with other divisions of the University included: four courses held at the Royal Ontario Museum plus a special short course on English silver as an adjunct to the exhibition, Seven Centuries of English Domestic Silver; two refresher courses for graduate nurses conducted in co-operation with the School of Nursing; two winter courses, a weekend workshop, and a June summer school presented in partnership with the School of Social Work.

A course in Literature and Life was given in York Mills Collegiate; the Department assisted the Community Programmes Branch of the Department of Education in providing courses in English for New Canadians; a course in anthropology for teachers in Indian schools was given; and examinations were again held in the basic sciences and English on behalf of the Ontario College of Physicians and Surgeons for graduates of European medical schools.

Under the direction of the University Television Committee, the Department gave support to a new university educational television programme, "Live and Learn," which was presented in co-operation with the Canadian Broadcasting Corporation. This psychology series, given by Professor D. C. Williams, the first of its kind to be presented in Canada, opens up a vast new field of service by the University to the community.

The Business and Industry division pursued its difficult but important role of satisfying the needs of business and industry without perverting the function of the University. Steps were taken to persuade business groups to provide their own training courses; classes were limited in size; courses were planned more carefully; courses which consisted in reality of a series of after-dinner speakers were eliminated or reorganized. In this process the enrolment dropped from 4,314 in 1956-7 to 3,430 in 1957-8.

New courses included Business Taxation, Canada's Major Industries, Lighting Fundamentals, Machine Accounting, and Business Administration I. An intensive five-week refresher course in aerodynamics was given in co-operation with the Institute of Aerophysics, and a National Welding Seminar for graduate engineers and other senior personnel was offered with the help of the National Welding Society.

The correspondence courses satisfied the desires of 5,557 students, many of whom are unable to attend lecture courses in any university, and many of whom prefer the correspondence method, with its insistence on individual effort, to the lecture method. The new course for the Association of Administrative Assistants was begun during this session with an encouraging enrolment of two hundred students. With the successful launching of this course, ten correspondence courses for professional or semi-professional organizations are now being offered. Although there is still much room for improvement in these courses, a great deal of successful revision has been undertaken during the past few years. It is also of interest to note that each year these courses attract more students from countries other than Canada.

During the year, the Department lost the excellent services of Mr. W. R. J. Kirk, Assistant Director in charge of the Business and Industry division. Mr. Kirk resigned to take a position with the Society of Industrial and Cost Accountants, an organization with which the University has co-operated for many years. However, the Department was most fortunate in having Mr. J. A. McIntyre and Mr. H. L. Mills appointed as Associate Director and Supervisor of Business Courses respectively. Both have already given fine leadership.

The Director would like to thank all members of the Department of University Extension, the hundreds of academic and administrative members of other divisions of the University, and the host of off-campus people who have given such excellent service to the University of Toronto through the Department of University Extension.

J. R. COULTER

---

## Report of the Chief Librarian

While it is commonly acknowledged that a strong teaching staff is the essential instrument and the glory of a university, it is worth remembering also that successful teaching and research must be based on recorded knowledge, and cannot be carried on without access to the record. The record exists mainly in the printed word, and the sea of print has become so vast, and the streams flowing into it so numerous and broad, that the individual scholar can no longer attempt to provide himself with more than a small portion of the printed record which he is likely to need.

In the universities, professors and students alike are becoming steadily more dependent on libraries. And libraries, since there is not one in the world that can collect more than a fraction of what is printed, are becoming more dependent on each other. Increasingly, libraries are becoming bibliographic information centres through which any publication, which exists anywhere in the world, may be located and made accessible.

In order to receive benefits from co-operation among libraries, naturally we must give as well as receive. During 1957-8, the University of Toronto Library continued to borrow from institutions outside Toronto, and lent more than ever before to them, including some as far afield as Italy, Germany, Russia, India, and Japan. We continued to send cards to the national union catalogue in Ottawa for all additions to our collection, and cards to the Library of Congress union catalogue and Cyrillic catalogue for selected additions. We continued to exchange Toronto publications for those of more than seven hundred other institutions, and carried on an extensive exchange of duplicates with other libraries. We prepared copy for two special bibliographies which are to be published elsewhere. We continued to support the microfilm project and the *Canadian Index*, two co-operative projects carried on by the Canadian Library Association. As an unofficial participant in the Farmington Plan for co-operative acquisition by research libraries, our Library continued to take responsibility for coverage of Canadian publication in French. We continued to work closely with the National Library of Canada, which has undertaken to provide certain important but seldom-used collections which are too expensive for individual universities to buy. We have been represented on the Associate Committee on Scientific Information, set up by the National Research Council to work out means whereby the Council's library, through its own collections and others such as our own, may improve its effectiveness as a national library of science. And we are hoping to embark soon, along with the University Press, on a North American "pre-cataloguing" experiment which promises to bring about great improvements in the accessibility of current publications on an international scale.

While the Library is thus engaged in co-operating with other institutions, it is an unfortunate fact that books in Halifax or Vancouver are more accessible to members of the University, through the national union catalogue, than are many books on our own campus. Quite apart from the libraries of the federated colleges, there are more than 300,000 items in various parts of the University which are not represented in any central catalogue and so are of little or no use to the University at large. Moreover, these collections are growing by more than 15,000 items a year. I think it is essential that we arrange, as soon as possible, for all new additions to these outlying collections to be shown in the University Library catalogue. Such an arrangement is necessary if the University is to make effective use of its own collections.

Acting as a centre of bibliographic information does not excuse our Library from continuing to build up its own stock of books. The coming of higher enrolments, particularly of Arts undergraduates who account for more than half of our total circulation of books, makes it imperative that we begin heavier duplication of titles immediately, and that as soon as possible we set up some such undergraduate service as is now being considered. The addition of new members to the teaching staff, the addition of new subjects to the curriculum, and the steadily rising tide of publication in almost every field of study: all these things mean that our collection has to grow rapidly in order to meet the every-day needs of the University. Such growth will

---

be expensive (the average price per item purchased rose from \$6.22 in 1956-7 to \$7.93 in 1957-8), but beside the figures published by comparable institutions, our present provision of books does not seem at all extravagant. According to figures reported in the January, 1958, issue of *College and Research Libraries*, there are twenty-seven North American university library collections larger than ours. Moreover, since a library's working strength depends on nourishment, it is worth noting that the same report lists forty-two university libraries (including that of the University of British Columbia) which are growing faster than ours. It is not at all important that we should keep pace, numerically, with other libraries, but it is exceedingly important that our University should have ready access to a good share of the world's current publication. It is so important that I think the University might well set aside some portion of its research grants to provide the printed record which is fundamental to research. At a time when we are attempting to enlarge the student body and the teaching staff without sacrificing quality, we cannot afford to let our Library become less than first-rate.

#### *Order Department*

Miss Agatha Leonard reports the purchase of 15,572 items at an average price of \$7.93, a rise of 27 per cent over the previous year's average price. Since this unforeseen increase occurred after the budget was set, and since few of the standing orders could be dropped, considerably fewer "short orders" could be placed during the year; actually, 24 per cent fewer items were bought. In all there were added 18,879 volumes and 1,959 pamphlets, making a total of 682,472 volumes and 165,456 pamphlets, or 847,928 items altogether.

Exchange of duplicates brought 697 volumes into the library for 5,362 volumes sent out, amounting to an increase of 94 per cent over last year's exchange transactions.

Presentations amounted to 12,015 items, an increase of 29 per cent. Among a multitude of valued gifts the outstanding single volume is a copy of the first English edition of Euclid, with a preface by John Dee, presented by Dr. Sigmund Samuel. The outstanding collection received was the library of the late J. B. Tyrrell, which is rich in works on geology and northern exploration. Important manuscripts received include an unpublished novel by Duncan Campbell Scott, unpublished mathematical papers of Alfred Young (presented by Professor G. de B. Robinson), and the correspondence of James E. Wetherell with poets of his day (presented by Miss Alicia Wetherell).

#### *Periodicals Department, including the Binding Division.*

Miss Dorothea Tod reports that revision of the subscription list resulted in cancellation of sixty-six subscriptions and seven exchanges. However, 279 new subscriptions and nineteen new exchanges and forty-seven new presentations were added, making a net increase of 272 additions to the periodicals list.

In the Binding Division, 8,131 volumes were sent out to binderies, 1,769 pieces were put into pamphlet binders or boards, and 3,217 volumes were mended. A revision of binding policy and a rearrangement of working space were carried out towards the close of the year; it is hoped that they will speed up the work of the division without impairing quality.

#### *Catalogue Department, including the Government Documents Division*

Altogether there were 37,278 items catalogued, 19 per cent more than the record set last year. Of these, 8,972 were new titles; the remainder were additional volumes or parts or copies. Also, the catalogues were altered to account for more than 5,000 volumes which were either withdrawn, re-catalogued, or transferred to different locations within the collection. There were 139,917 cards made or altered, of which about 38,000 new cards (32 feet of cards) were filed in the main catalogue. To the union catalogue in the National Library of Canada were sent 15,061 cards, and 684 to union catalogues at the Library of Congress.

The enforced lag in book purchasing has enabled the staff to cut down the size of the huge backlog of uncatalogued material. By February, the backlog had been

---

reduced to about 18,000 items, and more whittling away has been done since that time, but much remains to be done before the work can be brought up to date.

Meanwhile recent developments in the field of cataloguing, and the growing complexity of our own collections, make it desirable that our policies and practices be scrutinized and possibly revised. As a preliminary step, Miss Lorna Fraser has obtained from the Canada Council a grant which will enable her to study trends in cataloguing in a number of major libraries.

#### *Rare Books and Special Collections Department*

Refurbishing of quarters for this new department was completed during the summer, and the Rare Book Room was officially opened in November. To the contents of the old "Art Room" cupboards have been added the De Lury collection of Irish literature, the J. B. Tyrrell collection, a "history of science" collection, and many other recent acquisitions. Progress has been made also in transferring out of the general bookstack the *Publications* of the Records Commission of Great Britain, a collection of Italian renaissance plays, and other rare material. Cataloguing of the George M. Wrong letters was completed, and the organization of other manuscript collections begun.

The number of items added and catalogued in this department, and figures on the use of the material, are included in the figures quoted elsewhere in this report. Miss Marion Brown has noted that use of material in the department thus far has been by faculty members and graduate students (68 per cent), undergraduates (20 per cent), and graduates and visitors.

#### *Circulation and Reference Department*

Miss Violet Taylor reports an increase this year of 7.5 per cent in the number of books lent to undergraduates for home use, and 14 per cent in loans to faculty members and graduates. The over-all increase is greater, proportionately, than the rise in registration, and thus it follows a trend which can be observed in the records of the past eight years. The total number of loans (245,852) is greater than in any previous year.

Restrictions on the borrowing of "stack copies" by undergraduates at the Central Circulation desk, and transfer of many recommended books out of the stacks into the Wallace Room, helped to produce a rise of 15 per cent in loans from that room. Serious losses of books from the Wallace Room during the past three years, through theft and mutilation, have made it clear that open shelves are not the most suitable means of providing recommended books in a large university, and that other arrangements will have to be made in the near future.

The services rendered by Miss Taylor and her staff, and by other service divisions of the Library, are indicated by the following figures:

*Book Resources on the Campus*

Two years ago, at the request of President Smith, an annual census was begun of all campus book collections excepting those of the theological colleges. The Assistant Chief Librarian, Mr. David Foley, who has compiled the census, has discovered some new collections each year, and this year's figures include forty-three collections, outside the federated colleges, which are not listed in the University Library catalogue. Of these, all but five received additions during the past year, and taken together these additions were approximately as numerous as additions to the University Library. It is hardly correct to refer to them as additions to the University's book resources, as most of them are not effectively available to the University at large; however, for the sake of comparison, the figures are these:
