ROBERT H. BLACKBURN

*Report of the Director of the David Dunlap Observatory*

The past year has been marked by the emergence of astronomy as an experimental science with the launching of the first artificial earth satellites. These bodies make new demands on observing routines and offer new scope for astronomical research. The Observatory has been drawn into this field of activity as a result of both interest on the part of staff members and requests from international organizations for observational data. The first satellite was observed within a few hours of the launching, and since then regular observations, both visual and radio, of all the satellites have been made, and some theoretical methods of interpreting the results have been developed.

Development of radio astronomy, in co-operation with the Department of Electrical Engineering, has reached the point of making continuous daily observations of solar activity as a contribution to the International Geophysical Year. Two additional antennas have been designed and constructed for observation of radio stars. Antennas and receivers were also adapted for radio observation of the artificial radio satellites.

Observations with the 74-inch and the 19-inch reflectors were shared among direct photography, spectrographic photography, photo-electric photometry and spectrophotometry, and they were directed towards several lines of continuing research such as stellar radial velocities, variable stars and binary stars, and towards several lines of new research such as studies of Cepheid variable stars and of abundances of the elements in stellar atmospheres. The parts played by the various members of the staff in these and other research activities are recorded under the Department of Astronomy elsewhere in the President's Report.

During the year the total number of visitors to the Observatory was 8,900, about evenly divided among school groups, adult societies and clubs, and family groups. This is double the number which visited the Observatory as little as two years ago, and it represents about the maximum number which can be accommodated in the two weekly periods which have always been allotted for visitors. Reservations for these periods are usually filled some weeks in advance. The growing public interest in astronomy, while gratifying, presents a problem to an observatory of which the primary function is to pursue astronomical research. On the one hand is willingness to satisfy the public interest and to foster good will toward the University, on the other is concern over the loss of research time on the part of the members of the staff. Government observatories have met the problem by employing a special qualified person to arrange displays, instruct school groups, and, generally speaking, to meet the public.

---

Observing time with the 74-inch telescope was 772 hours; 390 spectrograms, 51 direct photographs, and 473 recordings with the photo-electric spectrophotometer were obtained. With the 19-inch telescope 215 direct photographs were obtained, and 617 photo-electric observations were made.

J. F. HEARD

### *Report of the Director of the Computation Centre*

During the past year there has been further steady growth in the number of undergraduates and research workers learning about computing and using our machine. From 6 per cent four years ago, the use of the available machine time by staff and students in the University of Toronto has now grown to 50 per cent—a trend that may be expected to continue. It is interesting to note the new substantial use of the machine for research at the Ontario College of Agriculture. The calculation by Dr. Worsley of electronic wave functions for silver, the atom of largest atomic number yet computed by the Hartree Fock method (with exchange), was the last big calculation performed on FERUT. This machine, installed in the McLennan Laboratory in 1952, has been used to perform a very great variety of calculations and has served as the means for learning about computing and data processing both in the University and in many organizations in Canada. Now that the St. Lawrence Seaway and Power Development is near completion it is appropriate to recall that the first calculations made on FERUT, nearly five hundred hours of machine computing for the Ontario Hydro Electric Power Commission, were part of this impressive undertaking.

On April 11, 1958, FERUT ceased operating in the University of Toronto and was replaced some weeks later by an International Business Machines Type 650 Electronic Data Processing Machine with alphabetic device, auxiliary floating point, index accumulators, and magnetic tapes. This system provides at somewhat less cost a computing facility of appreciably greater capacity than FERUT. In data processing it should make practicable a wider range of function and therefore extend the usefulness of the Centre in the University both in teaching and in research. It is hoped that the substantial growth of scientific and engineering use of the Centre's facilities will be complemented in the not too distant future by both academic and practical developments in other fields of work for which the new machine provides a unique opportunity in this University.

The expansion of the University has stimulated interest in the advantages of using a data processing machine for managing the records of students, and in other ways, as an aid to administration. Professor Gotlieb has been consulted on some of the problems: in particular how to use machines for working out time-tables of instruction. It is already clear that this problem and others will not be solved without substantial research to find effective principles on which to base programmes for the machine. If these matters are faced imaginatively, the new machine can be used to investigate how to perform the work economically. Through pilot scale operations on the IBM machine an orderly transition from existing procedures to new high speed methods can be effected, and experience gained for the future.

The Computation Centre continues its association with the University of Illinois in computer research. Since his return to the University of Toronto, Professor McKay has been investigating the magnetic properties of magnetic cores for high speed storage in a computer, using his proposal for non-destructive read-out to increase speed.

In addition to acknowledging once more financial support from the National Research Council and the Defence Research Board of Canada, I have pleasure in thanking the President and the Board of Governors of the University for special consideration in connection with the changes that have taken place in the past year. The International Business Machines Company has also been very helpful.

W. H. WATSON

---

## *Report of the Director of the Royal Ontario Museum*

Whether or not 1957 becomes permanently identified in world history as the Year of Sputnik, the Museum's 1957-8 year is likely long to be remembered under that rubric. Satellites and orbs, artificial or natural, and extended voyages into at least terrestrial space played an unusual role in the activities of a year which was conspicuously started by the temporary erection outside the building of a full-scale model of a satellite launcher. Within, the year turned on the usual combination of conservation, research, acquisition, and exhibition. That most of these functions were marked by a certain boldness was perhaps a natural reflection of the moment when the first successful penetration of outer space caused a universal stimulation of the imaginative faculty of man.

Administratively, a further step was taken in the long process of integrating the Museum into the structure of the University while preserving at the same time its freedom to fulfil its obligations to the general public. This was accomplished through an independent survey on salaries and equivalences of rank. The results, although in some respects confirming a suspicion that the Museum is a satellite rather than an organ, were beneficial in two respects: a further improvement in salaries was achieved, and a procedure for regular and merited promotions within the staff, especially at junior levels, was instituted and produced an immediate improvement in morale by establishing a genuine incentive for professional progress.

The Museum's increased ability to seize an opportunity effectively was demonstrated by the rapid organization and smooth operation of the Division of Zoology and Palaeontology's expedition to Peru. The recognition by a former student of the significance of an untouched Pleistocene tar seep near Talara in northern Peru (a site very similar to the famous LaBrea tar pits in Los Angeles), the fortunate circumstance that the land is under the control of a subsidiary of the Imperial Oil Company, and the fact that a portion of the Special Grant of the Province of Ontario could be devoted to the purpose, enabled the Museum to send a team of its experts, properly equipped, to the site in short order. In one season's work plus an additional trip by Dr. R. R. H. Lemon, he, Dr. Gordon Edmund, and Mr. Ralph Hornell were able to obtain a very extensive series of both vertebrate and invertebrate specimens of well-preserved South American prehistoric fauna parallel to the known material from California. This find provides most important research material for many years to come, as well as a substantial number of effective exhibition pieces. The local support and assistance of the oil company were of great material aid to the successful completion of the mission.

The increasing recognition of the Museum's active part in the life of the community was marked by an unusual number of non-museum events held in the building by request. These began with the opening reception for the plenary session of the International Geophysical Year congress, an occasion which gave the many foreign delegates and their wives an immediate opportunity to become aware of the richness of Royal Ontario Museum collections. The Board and staff were particularly pleased in view of his long and sympathetic interest in the Museum and its problems that the Museum should have been chosen as the setting for the University's reception to honour President Sidney Smith when he paid his first return visit after becoming Secretary of State for External Affairs. Another extraordinary event occurred in the spring when the Junior Women's Committee of the Toronto Symphony Orchestra sponsored in the Museum the first exhibition by the recently formed Society of Decorators. These novel activities gave valuable experience for the conduct of such affairs in the future and introduced many new visitors to the Museum.

Each of the research departments and nearly all the curatorial staffs were engaged actively in the furtherance of a wide variety of research projects. Although none of the several books completed during the period had achieved publication by June 30, the list of publications by members of the staff gives some notion of the range of work in progress. It should be noted that in this as in any other museum

---

the end result or visible evidence of research activity is not confined by any means to books, articles, or lectures. Labelling of specimens or exhibits in the permanent galleries, study, cataloguing and organization of new additions to the collections, organization of special exhibitions, exploration and testing of new techniques of conservation, assistance in identification of objects for private collectors or other less well-equipped institutions, and preparation of papers for delivery at professional meetings are all aspects of the unceasing research which occupies a major part of the time of curators and senior technicians. This holds true for all divisions.

The exhibition programme for the year was both broad in scope and intense in tempo. As usual the Division of Art and Archaeology was the most conspicuous in this important area of museum activity, for it is here that public and student alike have their best opportunity to explore a specific aspect of a subject, to rediscover the multiple values of familiar objects in new contexts, and to make the acquaintance of new objects or important pieces borrowed for the occasion from distant and sometimes ordinarily inaccessible collections. An early show of the season was an admirable illustration of the value of seeing familiar objects in new associations: this was an exhibition arranged as a memorial to the late Dr. C. T. Currelly, the founding genius of the old Royal Ontario Museum of Archaeology. It emphasized the extraordinary breadth of his interests and the quality of his taste.

The distinguished collections of Persian paintings belonging to Mrs. Samuel Bronfman of Montreal and New York and Mrs. Charles Slatkin of New York were joined with museum objects in a choice exhibition entitled "Persian Paintings and Islamic Applied Arts," which took place too early in the season to attract the attention it deserved. The third and final instalment in the sequence of exhibitions devoted to Dr. A. E. MacDonald's great collection of maps illustrative of the history of the exploration of Canada enjoyed wide success. Shown under the title of "Up North," it was devoted to the Arctic and turned on the four-century search for the Northwest Passage, a story which was brought to a dramatic conclusion by concurrent events in the Arctic itself. This show had the benefit of the first full-dress installation undertaken by the Division's new Display Chief, Mr. Harley Parker. "Pioneer Ontario" at the year's end presented the familiar McCrea models in entirely new and most effective settings. The Canadiana Gallery, dislocated during the earlier part of the year by building activities, was able to mount two exhibitions, "The Seven Years War in Canada" and an ambitious show "Krieghoff, Bartlett and Kane." The latter celebrated the opening of the new wing.

The most ambitious exhibition ever undertaken by the Museum, the climax of more than two years of intensive effort, had the honour of being opened in a gala atmosphere by the Governor-General on the evening of January 13. This was "Seven Centuries of English Silver," a survey of changing taste and fine craftsmanship, which brought together what critics have described as the finest assemblage of top-quality pieces ever arranged in this field. It drew nearly 60,000 paid visitors over a two-month period and had an installation worthy of the subject. The preparations for the show resulted in the discovery of an unexpected number of major pieces in private Canadian collections and stimulated very wide interest. Precious objects were graciously lent by Her Majesty the Queen, His Royal Highness the Duke of Brunswick and Lüneburg, the Worshipful Company of Goldsmiths, King's College, Cambridge, and a large number of other private, corporate, and institutional owners. A distinguished series of lectures in connection with the exhibition was delivered by Mrs. G. E. P. How of London, who was the exhibition's learned and charming English consultant, Mrs. Lydia Bond Powel and Mr. Carl Dauterman of the Metropolitan Museum, Mr. Marvin Ross of the Dumbarton Oaks Foundation, Mrs. Katherine Buehler of the Boston Museum of Fine Arts, and Mr. Paul Grigaut of the Detroit Institute of Arts. A handsomely illustrated and designed catalogue was also produced and serves as a permanent handbook on the subject of English domestic silver from the late thirteenth to the early nineteenth century.

Smaller shows within the Division ranged from comic strips to incunabula from the collection of Carl Dair and from Selwyn Dewdney's copies of Indian pictographs

---

in Quetico Park to "Gentleman of Fashion," which was drawn from the large collection of prints given by Warren K. Cook Co. Exhibitions in the continuing series devoted to master photographers were given of the work of Sam Tata and Roloff Beny.

The Division of Geology and Mineralogy, although heavily preoccupied with the second stage of the rebuilding of its permanent galleries, contrived an illuminating exhibition on the subject of artificial satellites and, for the International Geophysical Year, an insight into working methods of Soviet scientists in the Antarctic through a splendid set of photographs. The Division of Zoology and Palaeontology, likewise heavily preoccupied during the year with such other commitments as the building of its new reptile gallery and the Peruvian expedition, nonetheless contributed two short-term shows of special interest, one devoted to "Arctic Birds" and the other to "Reptiles"—many of which were shown alive.

A great deal of field work in addition to the Peruvian expedition was carried out or continued. New work was undertaken at Chubb Crater in Northern Quebec and, although Dr. V. B. Meen's personal participation was prevented at the last moment, he had completed the planning and has since had all the fresh data made available to him. He was able to continue a project in eastern Ontario, especially on radioactive occurrences in the Wilberforce-Bancroft area. All members of his Division have continued geological and mineralogical field research in other parts of Ontario. Dr. Urquhart was able to carry out further elements in his famed tracking of Monarch butterfly migrations and Dr. Wiggins made further progress in the field collecting of Trichoptera. The Division of Art and Archaeology was deeply disappointed to be unable for want of funds to start work on the Central American site reserved for the Museum but has been very active closer to home. The second season at Serpent Mounds was curtailed but made substantial progress which was of great benefit in planning the third season's campaign in 1958. Field investigation and some digging were carried out at Inverhuron and English River. All the ground-work was laid for an extended ethnological project in the north, which was, however, only just begun in the field at the end of the session. The first season in the important recording project on Indian pictographs, which is being conducted jointly with the Quetico Foundation under the supervision of Mr. Selwyn Dewdney, contributed to a splendid start and mapping for the subsequent seasons permitted the full planning of campaign for the 1958 season.

A happy event took place on March 22 when, with the Honourable J. Keiller Mackay, Lieutenant-Governor of Ontario, in the chair, and in the presence of a most distinguished gathering of guests, the new wing of the Sigmund Samuel Canadiana Gallery was officially opened. This occasion had had to be delayed for several months because of a strike, but the obvious pleasure of the Honorary Chairman in seeing this generous project carried through to completion in his ninety-second year was in no way diminished by the delay. The new space includes a fine gallery on the main floor, a smaller gallery for historical portraits on the lower floor and additional offices and storage. The wing was inaugurated with an exhibition of the works of three principal early figures in the history of Canadian art, Krieghoff, Bartlett, and Paul Kane.

The special lecture series was greatly strengthened by an increase in appropriation and a working arrangement with the Department of University Extension. The series of lectures in connection with the Silver Exhibition has been mentioned. Other distinguished lecturers presented in the Museum were Dr. Michael Stettler, Director of the Bernese Historical Museum; Dr. Cooney of the Brooklyn Museum; and Mr. Peter Floud of the Victoria and Albert Museum, London. Extension courses by members of the staff included Adventures in Archaeology, Art in Japan, and the second series of So You're Going to Europe. Special courses were given on Fashion Design, Furniture Design, the Museum as a Teaching Aid, and Talking of Textiles.

Upwards of twenty staff members did cross-appointment teaching in the University and the whole of the curatorial staff devoted much time and effort to occasional speaking to clubs and other organizations throughout Ontario. Papers delivered at

---

professional congresses here and abroad have been recorded in the Staff Bulletin as well as much of the routine travelling by staff members. It is worth noting that efforts to obtain travel grants for Museum personnel are beginning to bear fruit. Among those awarded during the period were one from UNESCO to Mrs. Barbara Stephen for study in Japan and one from the Canada Council to Mr. Harley Parker to study installation techniques in Europe. The Museum's own enlarged travel appropriation enabled a sizable delegation to attend the meetings of the American Association of Museums in Charleston and of the Canadian Association at Windsor, and made it possible for many individual members to attend professional congresses in their own disciplines.

All divisions have made notable acquisitions in the course of the year. The improvement of the collections has been greatly aided by the continuation of the special grant of \$100,000 first offered last year by the Provincial Government, by gifts and bequests from generous donors, and through the Museum's field work. Detailed lists of acquisitions appear in an appendix, but some of them may be noted here.

The Division of Zoology and Palaeontology has made two remarkable additions. The Peruvian expedition already mentioned produced four tons of Pleistocene fossils from the LaBrea tar pits near Talara in northern Peru. There are many hundreds of unusually well-preserved specimens, both vertebrate and invertebrate, which will be the objects of research for many years to come, as well as being welcome exhibition material. The opportunity to purchase the James Alexander Munro research collection of western birds (8,299 specimens) and mammals (2,723 specimens) at once filled a major gap in the Museum's systematic ornithological holdings and raised it to top rank in the field of North American birds. The Munro ornithological collection includes representatives of 310 of North America's 376 genera and 588 of its 795 species. The mammal section is equally rich. This department was further enriched by the gifts of the Charles Long collection of East African birds and the Arthur Smith collection of Ontario birds. A large collection of invertebrates from the Mississippian Rundle formation was added to the Museum's rich holdings by Dr. C. H. Crickmay of Calgary.

The collections of the Division of Geology and Mineralogy continued to improve through a large number of gifts and exchanges, through field work and through purchase. Perhaps the most noteworthy additions were to the gem collection, especially an imperial blue faceted topaz weighing 3,000 carats, the blue "Star of Lanka" sapphire of 194 carats (both by purchase), and a large black opal given by Dr. Sigmund Samuel. The "Star of Lanka" has been installed in a special new type of pillar case and attracts vivid attention.

All departments of the Division of Art and Archaeology benefited by the acquisition of both distinguished single pieces and blocks of study and research material. Among these may be mentioned a life-size antique marble figure of an athlete after Polycleitus, a portrait bust of Thucydides in marble and a large bronze patera with the handle in the form of an Apollo, all by purchase for the Greek and Roman Department; a very rare eleventh-century Shinto standing figure in wood and a bronze standing Buddha of the ninth century from Negapatam in southern India, both by purchase for the Far Eastern collections; a set of Queen Anne crewel-work bed hangings, by purchase for the Textile Department; an important portrait bust of a nobleman in terra cotta (about 1580) by the Venetian sculptor Alessandro Vittoria, by purchase, and a silver cup and cover of 1740-1 by Christian Hillan of London, as a bequest of Mrs. G. Howard Ferguson, for the European Department; an XI Dynasty limestone stele by gift to the Egyptian Department, and seven other reliefs of various periods purchased from the Metropolitan Museum; a superb Baga "Nimba" in wood, by purchase, and a notable Teotihuacan mask in onyx, by gift of Mrs. Edgar Stone, to the Department of Ethnology; the unique Edith Chowne Pierce collection of early Canadian glass by gift of Dr. Lorne Pierce, a seventeenth-century painting, "Fishing Station in Newfoundland," by Gerard van Edema and the 1511 Ptolemy Atlas among the numerous and valuable gifts of Dr. Sigmund Samuel, and

---

a distinguished set of butternut panelling by purchase from a house in Fenelon Falls, all for the Canadiana Collections. A remarkable collection of ancient sculptures and fragments in stone and clay from Cyprus, numbering over three hundred pieces, was transferred to the Museum from the National Gallery in Ottawa. Its full importance cannot be assessed until it has had very thorough study, but it is certain to throw light on early trade and cultural relations between the island and some of its mainland neighbours. It ranges from the early Bronze Age to the Roman period.

The Division of Education is working at full capacity and cannot at present undertake any additions to its teaching commitments. The unique winter service, teaching with case exhibitions in distant schools, brought nearly nine thousand pupils in the Lower St. Lawrence and Muskoka districts into direct contact with the Museum this year. The twelve unaccompanied travelling cases went to some fifty additional secondary schools. Some twelve hundred elementary schools have also used material circulated by the Division of Education. The highly popular Saturday Morning Club, working under the devoted leadership of Miss Eugenia Berlin, concentrated for the year at all age levels on "The Use of Clay by Different Peoples"; the two special exhibitions which resulted were a clear demonstration of how successfully the children's horizons are extended and what intelligent and imaginative use they make of Museum collections. The Division co-ordinates all the teaching by institutions other than the University which use the Museum, and collaborates in the preparation of the various Extension courses held here. This year it also played a principal role in organizing the experimental Camp Museum arranged for the international Centenary World Camp for Girl Guides held at Elmsdale, Ont. The Camp Museum was visited by some six thousand persons. As usual it made a major contribution to the annual workshop sessions arranged by the Ontario Historical Association to aid the volunteer staffs of local museums. In the course of the year the Division was responsible for the direct instruction within the Museum of over sixty thousand children, 51,231 attending in 1,777 class groups, and also, in the course of the teachers' trips to remote schools, its members reached 9,138 more in 224 classes.

The first of many staff changes by appointment, promotion, or resignation during the year was the appointment of Mr. Henry Trubner of the Los Angeles County Museum to the Curatorship of Oriental Art and Acting Curator of the Far Eastern Department. Miss Helen Fernald as of June 30 became Research Curator of Oriental Art in order to be able to devote full time to completing the first part of the projected five-volume catalogue of the Chinese Collections. In the same Division Mr. John Lunn, formerly Director of the Verulamium Museum at St. Alban's, Herts., joined the staff as Assistant Curator in the Classical Department and Mr. Harley Parker of the Ontario College of Art became Display Chief, a new and key position. The resignation of Mrs. D. B. Gill from the Modern European Department and the retirement of preparator Mr. Michael Gedeonoff were particularly regretted. In Zoology and Palaeontology Dr. E. Crossman and Dr. R. R. H. Lemon came to the Museum as Assistant Curators of Ichthyology and Herpetology and Invertebrate Palaeontology respectively. Dr. Lemon is also in charge of the latter department. This Division regrettably lost the services of its popular artist, Mr. Archie Reid, and of both its long-time Secretary and its Librarian, Miss M. Easto and Miss E. R. McClure. Between the several divisions five junior staff members were to be awarded on July 1 (in accordance with the provisions of the new system) merit promotions to Assistant Curatorships and several more to the new rank of Curatorial Assistant, which is designated as an in-training step for advancement into the fully professional curatorial grades. They will be listed in the next report. Mr. Harold Vanstone, Artist-Preparator in Geology, is to be congratulated on having earned his B.A. extramurally.

The Office of Information Services has had a very busy year and has been obliged to increase its staff in order to keep abreast of the increasing demands made upon it. It performed with particular effectiveness during the Silver Show, and has found time in addition to its more obvious duties to carry through a large part of the planning for two major projects starting with the new fiscal year and to be of unob-

---

trusive assistance at one time or another to nearly every department in the Museum. Its odd jobs have included devising a successful strategy for rescuing a shipment of reptiles in danger of rotting on a strikebound ship at Montreal and arranging for several extremely welcome gifts of objects or financial assistance to the Museum. A report for the first ten months of the year showed that the office had placed 1,208 stories and 226 pictures about Museum activities in the press for a total of 4,249 inches of news space and for eleven months of the reporting period had arranged fifty-five radio appearances (604 minutes) and twenty-five television appearances (294 minutes). The natural sciences and archaeology have enjoyed great popularity as themes for television and radio programmes, though the spectacular English silver exhibition commanded the greatest concentration of attention from both media.

Attendance as a gross statistic is only one yardstick for measuring the effectiveness of a museum and is hardly the most important. However several interesting and even significant facts emerge from discrete studies of Royal Ontario Museum statistics recently concluded. The Museum was used in one way or another by 545,742 persons during the reporting period and over-all attendance has increased by 26 per cent in the past three years. Use by school children in class groups has remained about stationary, since the Division of Education cannot handle more than the present load, and is obliged to refuse a substantial number of requests. Use by university students tends to follow the curve of the steady increase in registration. Free adult attendance has, however, taken a sharp upward turn in the past three years, attaining within the period under review an increase of 49 per cent. This would appear to be the result in large measure of the programme of special exhibitions and greatly improved information services. Its full significance is not yet understood.

The Museum received its eleven millionth visitor since its original opening in 1912 sometime during the month of May. The visit went unnoticed and the anonymous visitor, discovered through statistics, departed without a souvenir of the occasion. That is the way we like it to be. A museum is a very personal thing from which each man or child carries away approximately what he is willing to put in. It is a spiritual transaction and the least among the visitors is as welcome as he who comes bearing material gifts for the good of all. The fact that well over eleven million people have now made use of the Royal Ontario Museum would seem to indicate that the vision of the founders, the devotion of staff throughout these years, and the confidence of government, University, and trustees have been well placed. The Museum seeks only further ways to increase its usefulness to the community of which it is a real and vital part, and to the fellowship of scholars within the University.

ROYAL ONTARIO MUSEUM ATTENDANCE, JULY 1, 1957, TO JUNE 30, 1958

T. A. HEINRICH

## *Report of the Director of the University of Toronto Press*

A special report from this office to the President and the Press Committee of the Board of Governors dated October 25, 1954, made the following recommendations:

Erection of a new Press Building to comprise all the following departments . . . (a) Bookstore, (b) Publications Department, . . . (c) Accounting Department, . . . (d) Administrative departments. The purpose of the above building would be to provide an academic centre on the campus for all Press activities except the processing of actual printing orders, and to establish the publishing activities of the Press in a situation that would encourage their maximum use by the university community . . . (A site) might be the area east of the new University Extension Building, facing south-east toward the front campus. . . . The Press appears to possess the financial ability to underwrite all normal building and alterations costs . . . without additional grants from other University funds.

For the implementation in full of these recommendations, the University of Toronto Press expresses its warm appreciation to the Board of Governors and to the University administration. Their support of the Press has been continuous during its recent years of rapid expansion. It is also a cause for satisfaction that the Press has been able to assume the whole cost of constructing and equipping the new building in which it is now housed on the front campus.

Certain changes will be made in the organization of the Press departments located in the new building. Physical separation of the business office of the Publications Department from the former Bookstore necessitated some duplication of services, which will now be eliminated. Processing of telephone and mail orders, warehousing, shipping, receiving, correspondence, and invoicing will henceforth be consolidated in a single Book Department, which will conduct both retail and wholesale activities. An efficient arrangement of offices, furnished with the most modern equipment available, will be provided for the editorial and administrative departments on the upper storeys. The retail facilities on the main floor, and the warehousing area in the basement, will not be excelled by any on the continent. Congestion during the college opening period will be minimized although it would be uneconomical to build a structure or to employ a staff of sufficient size to eliminate this entirely during the few days following autumn registration. However, flexible arrangements throughout the selling areas have been carefully planned to permit reorganiza-

---

tion from time to time during the year to suit the season and to provide the best possible facilities to both staff and student visitors.

The bookselling activities of the University of Toronto Press are secondary in importance only to its functions as a publishing house and as a quality printer. The continued development of high standards in these departments will make it one of the great academic assets of this institution of learning. Fifty-three books bearing the imprint of the Press were published during the past year, in addition to the usual large number of issues of its seven scholarly journals. I should like again to thank the members of our staff who made this record possible. During the past year the Press has continued to receive special visits, both in the printing plant and at the editorial offices, from leading printers and publishers of the world. Indeed, the still wider development of its international connections has been one of the features of the recent history of the Press. This fact is evidenced by growing export sales—already much the largest among Canadian book publishing houses.

The University of Toronto Press will be participating during the next year in a study of manufacturing methods in scholarly book publishing. This study is being financed by a special grant from the Ford Foundation, acting through the American Association of University Presses. This programme is separate from the Ford Foundation programme of support of publications in the humanities and social sciences which began last year, and under which this institution expects to receive collateral assistance to the extent of \$42,500 over a five-year period.

The list of publications issued by the University of Toronto Press during the year ended June 30, 1958, follows:

M. JEANNERET

## Report of the Director of the Connaught Medical Research Laboratories

In this year of recession it is pleasing to note that the total number of persons employed by the Connaught Medical Research Laboratories in research and production has increased. In June, 1957, the number was 592. In June, 1958, the number is 634 in spite of some losses from our scientific staff for whom replacements have still to be found. Dr. D. M. Young, for many years a Clinical Associate of the Connaught Medical Research Laboratories, left us to assume a position of great responsibility as Director of Laboratories of the Toronto General Hospital. He carries with him the very best wishes of all his colleagues in the C.M.R.L. So too does Dr. Angus Graham, who has left us to occupy an important post in the Wistar Institute, Philadelphia. His former colleague, Dr. L. Siminovitch, also left to take a position in the Ontario Cancer Institute, for which he had been preparing himself for several years. Our greatest loss, because it represents no corresponding gain to anyone, was the untimely death of Dr. C. W. J. Armstrong. Of this, more will be said later.

### *Poliomyelitis Vaccine*

The period under review saw the solution of most of the problems which harassed our capable staff during the earlier years of production of Salk vaccine. The main cause for concern this year was an exasperating uncertainty in the supply of monkeys from India and recurrent difficulties in obtaining them elsewhere, with consequent disturbance of production schedules. During the autumn of 1957, orders for poliomyelitis vaccine to a total of 4,000,000 doses were received from the government of the United Kingdom. A schedule of deliveries was established requiring shipments starting in January, 1958, and completion of the order by the end of

---

June, 1958. All deliveries were made ahead of schedule and new orders on behalf of the British Government were received during the summer of 1958.

Some forty other countries also benefited by the surplus of poliomyelitis vaccine which was produced this year by the Laboratories, in excess of the requirements of purchasers in Canada, both governmental and private. All members of the polio vaccine department deserve the highest commendation for the success with which they maintained, at the maximum rate, the production of vaccine of unsurpassed quality. At the time of writing, the incidence of paralytic poliomyelitis is at the lowest figure ever recorded in Canada. We need to remind ourselves that it cannot be assumed that this satisfactory fact is due entirely to the extensive programme of vaccination which has been conducted in Canada. Nevertheless, the fact gives us understandable pleasure and encouragement.

*Dr. C. W. J. Armstrong*

On February 28, 1958, the Laboratories suffered a grievous loss through the death of one of our most valued senior staff members. Dr. Armstrong died suddenly at the wheel of his car as the result of a coronary occlusion. Dr. Armstrong was in charge of the potency testing section of the polio vaccine department where he supervised with meticulous care an enormous number of routine tests and an active programme of research. His energy and critical imagination are seriously missed.

#### *Laboratory Infections*

After the blow of February came March and two more tragedies to mar the record of a year which otherwise was a most successful one. Two members of the staff, Ferdinand Jakubec and David O'Hara, who worked with the monkey colony, died within the same month of an acute infection which was proved to be due to Virus B, a dangerous virus which is carried by some of the monkeys when they arrive. The disease produced in monkeys by this virus is usually mild and often unrecognizable. In humans, unfortunately, the disease is usually fatal. So far, efforts to make a vaccine against this disease have not been successful, but the attempt is being continued with great energy both in the Connaught Medical Research Laboratories and elsewhere.

#### *Asian Influenza*

During the summer and early autumn of 1957, radio, television, newspapers, and magazines deluged the public with alarming stories of imminent invasion by a sinister disease called Asian Influenza. A ray of hope was held out by the further information that many laboratories were working night and day to produce a vaccine to prevent the dreaded infection. Reporters felt duty-bound to keep the public informed almost from day to day on progress in the battle for production of vaccine. It is hard to know what to do about this sort of hysteria in our kind of a democracy! In June, 1957, the Connaught Medical Research Laboratories received samples of the new virus of Asian Influenza and, at the request of the Department of National Health and Welfare, started to develop and test a new vaccine. As soon as suitable methods were developed, mass production was started in August. Deliveries began about the first of October. It was the plan of the ten provincial departments of health, co-ordinated by the Department of National Health and Welfare, to use any vaccine which became available for the protection of essential services, including the armed services and the health services. It was fairly clear from the reports collected by the World Health Organization that the epidemics in other countries were not accompanied by high mortality, but that the attack rate could be very high in crowded communities. Disruption of industry and of public services might be expected. For once the problem was not to save the lives of "women and children first" but to keep the workers at their work!

Previous experiences with vaccines against other types of influenza did not suggest that a high degree of protection could be provided by a single injection of vaccine—which was all that could be given before the epidemic was likely to strike. A reduction of attack rates by 50 or 60 per cent seemed to be as much as one had reason

---

to expect. Even this modest benefit seemed worth working for, particularly as any experience gained might be valuable if a subsequent epidemic of greater virulence should occur. For the protection of "essential personnel, the provincial departments of health estimated that they should have about 500,000 doses before the epidemic arrived. This proved to be an impossible goal, but the Connaught Medical Research Laboratories did deliver nearly 400,000 doses during October and November. Considering that the Laboratories were not producing any kind of influenza vaccine at the time of onset of the 1957 pandemic, it is gratifying to note that our 400,000 doses constituted the bulk of the influenza vaccine used by the departments of health in Canada. At this time (June, 1958) Asian Influenza seems to have almost disappeared, as far as any danger of an epidemic is concerned. Needless to say, our hectic efforts to produce vaccine cannot be credited with this happy outcome. It was, however, good vaccine and the experience which was gained in its production and testing will be valuable should a similar emergency arise again.

#### *Dufferin Division*

In May, 1957, two new buildings for the Dufferin Division were authorized by the Board of Governors. One was a laboratory building to house sections of the polio vaccine department which were still trespassing on the space of other departments. It also provided space for the C.M.R.L. Library which is still in the School of Hygiene building. The laboratories were occupied in June and it is expected that the library will move into its new quarters in September. The second building is a large structure, now nearing completion, for the storage of supplies and equipment used for manufacture of products of all kinds. These new buildings, and others which are being planned, are part of a programme to move the activities gradually from the College and Spadina Divisions and collect them together in the Dufferin area, not under a single roof, but at least in reasonable proximity to each other.

J. K. W. FERGUSON

### *Report of the Warden of Hart House*

In my reports during the last few years I have outlined the basic educational objectives on which Hart House is founded and I have tried to indicate some of the problems with which we will be faced during the next decade. The past year has emphasized their importance. In a wide variety of ways the House serves its members and the whole University community in increasing numbers. We are conscious of the inadequacy of our facilities to satisfy many of our present needs. It is our judgment that long before the time when we may expect to have extra accommodation we will need to make temporary provision for the number and variety of extracurricular activities which will be an inevitable accompaniment of increased enrolment.

We are, of course, cognizant of the priority that must be given to strictly academic needs; I, however, would be derelict in my duty if I did not once again stress the fact that much of the value of a university education lies in the informal contacts between student and student, student and graduate, student and teacher. In my judgment these aspects of a university programme are just as vital as the more formal facets of a strictly curricular nature and it is imperative that adequate consideration should be given to these matters. We, however, are happy to report that in spite of our concern on this major problem we have concluded a very satisfying year.

The event of outstanding importance was the Eleventh General Assembly of the International Union of Geodesy and Geophysics. From September 2 to September 14, 1957, the total facilities of the House were in use. I cannot refrain from commenting on the fact that the use of the House in this way by academic bodies and learned societies is just one of the contributions that we can continue to make—over and

---

above the normal use of the House by the university community during the academic year. It seems likely that during the coming years we will have more, rather than fewer, of such events and, in so far as they do not interfere with the normal programme during the academic year, we are always ready to co-operate with the university authorities in our role as hosts to such groups.

In this connection it is of interest to report that during the year we provided services to various alumni, philanthropic, educational, and religious groups to a total of approximately 23,000 persons. This is, of course, in addition to our undergraduate membership and the membership of almost 1,400 university graduates who are senior members of Hart House and who, through their continuing association with the House, maintain a viable link with the University. As we look forward to the future it is a serious concern that we should continue to render this type of service. Our first obligation is, of course, to the student body, but the use of the House in this broader way for 365 days in the year contributes much of value to the life of the university community.

One of the very interesting events of the autumn term was the visit to a Hart House Debate of Senator John F. Kennedy of Massachusetts. We were also pleased to welcome the Honourable Donald M. Fleming, Minister of Finance. The attendance at six debates during the year showed a considerable increase over the previous year.

The Music Committee sponsored the usual programme of concerts and recitals. That given by Miss Zara Nelsova, cellist, was, in the judgment of many people, the finest concert which has been given in our Great Hall in many years. In co-operation with the Hart House Orchestra Associates a series of five concerts by the Hart House Orchestra attracted capacity audiences. We received with great pleasure the news that the Hart House Orchestra would represent Canada this coming August at the Brussels Exposition. The Glee Club had a good year. In December it offered for sale a long-playing record of Christmas music under the title, "Christmas Carols." I regret to report that Mr. Ward McAdam, who has directed the Club so capably for eight years, has found it necessary, because of pressure of other duties, to tender his resignation. I take this opportunity of thanking him for a job well done.

The Art Committee sponsored a total of fourteen exhibitions in our own gallery. Canvases by Aba Bayefsky, Kazuo Nakamura, and Gordon Smith were added to the collection of paintings owned by the House.

By arrangement with the Texas A. & M. College, two students were selected to represent a Canadian university at the Third Student Conference on National Affairs, held last December. This project brings together students from a large number of American universities to meet with public leaders and educators. Our representatives presented a careful report on this significant event in the columns of *The Varsity*.

In the summers of 1954 and 1955 we carried on an exchange with students of the universities of Finland. This project has been continued and during the past few weeks we have welcomed to Canada eight Finnish students for a work-study seminar. Past experience has proven the value of this type of meeting between the young people of two countries which, though dissimilar in many ways, nevertheless have much in common. In a world of chaos, mistrust and suspicion we are proving once again that it is possible for young people of differing languages and cultural backgrounds to live together in mutual confidence.

The facilities of the Caledon Hills Farm continue to be used by hundreds of students throughout the academic year. We are at present planning an improvement of facilities in order to increase the usefulness of the farm.

Approval has also been given by the Board of Governors to proposals for renovation, refurnishing, and improvement of the lighting in the Music Room and the Debates Room. It is also planned to refurnish the guest rooms. These three projects will be carried out during the summer of 1958.

In conclusion, may I say that it is our hope that in an expanding University we shall not lose sight of those intangible factors which prevent a large university becoming an "educational assembly line." I am much concerned that we should not forget the individual student and all those facets of his life and activity which

---

are just as important a part of his education as his formal instruction. In spite of the difficulties which I have indicated, it is our hope that, not in lesser measure but in greater, Hart House may continue to play a vital role in the education of the "whole man."

JOSEPH McCULLEY

### *Report of the Chairman of the Board of Syndics of Hart House Theatre*

*Family Reunion, Misalliance, Mr. Arcularis, and The Trojan Women* gave us another successful season artistically and financially (revenue increased by 10 per cent). Members of the University Alumnae Drama Club were invited to share our production of the *Trojan Women*; this experiment was an unqualified success. Next season we hope to try another new venture: the showing of some specialized films. As usual about a hundred students received training from Robert Gill and hundreds more participated in their college and faculty shows, so that the Theatre was occupied almost every night, and, thanks to our new electronic switchboard, "lighting" was always admirable.

The Syndics are now being forced to re-examine in the light of current ideas an old question: the function of Hart House Theatre, which for years, under our capable director, Robert Gill, has been producing actors and actresses for the professional theatre of Canada and which, in fact, has been in a small way a Theatre School. But is the time now ripe for expansion into an official Theatre School—national or otherwise—as was planned when Mr. Gill was brought to Hart House Theatre twelve years ago? Would it now be possible to finance a second auditorium and to engage several more instructors? If possible, would it be wise? Is it preferable for students who wish to become professional actors to work in their spare time under Mr. Gill and after graduation go to London or New York to train further and to widen their horizons? The Syndics are not yet agreed on these questions and expect to discuss them at length during the next season, which will offer us the traditional pattern: plays by Miller, Kaufman and Marquand, Mayer and Giraudoux.

C. C. LOVE

### *Report of the Director of Falconer Hall*

Falconer Hall was opened in November, 1952. Since that time it has gone through various changes. The resident students have been reduced from twenty-two to fourteen, office space has been re-allocated, and more common rooms for student use have been provided. This year a room in the basement, once the "salad pantry" of Wymilwood, was made into a common room where students could have lunch or supper. This proved of great assistance to many students taking part in the evening sports programme. Otherwise, activities have been much the same as in the last two years. In the early years the staff of the Department of Physical Education undertook the promotion of this building among students and the public. With the pressure of teaching this has not been possible for some time and the results are becoming evident. We go forward or we go backward, and this building is no exception. If Falconer Hall is to remain for any length of time, then an active campaign should be started to increase its use. The decrease in the use of the building by non-resident groups is particularly alarming since this is the chief source of revenue. The first full year of operation, 1953-4, showed a total of 318 meetings with an approximate attendance of 16,000. This year there have been 263 meetings with an approximate attendance of 13,270. The student meetings have varied little throughout the five-year period; in the first year of operation we had 101 student meetings, and this year 94.

---

In my report of last year I indicated that we had reached a plateau. It is obvious that unless the use of the building is promoted further we will not remain on this plateau.

As usual, various faculties and schools were represented among resident students. There were five from the School of Nursing, four from the School of Graduate Studies, three from the School of Social Work, one from Music, and one from the Library School.

Through the vigilance of Mr. Russell and his staff, the building has been kept in excellent condition; in fact we have had comparatively few maintenance difficulties this year.

The Advisory Committee has met three times. This Committee was set up by President Smith in 1952. Over the years Dr. Smith found time to attend many of the meetings and we were sorry to see him leave. However, Dr. Bissell was a member of the original Committee and so we welcome his return. We record with deep regret the death, in February, of Dr. Florence Quinlan. Dr. Quinlan was one of the four women on the Committee since its inception. Her interest and encouragement will be sorely missed.

The domestic staff has remained constant. To them I extend my appreciation for their work throughout the year.

ZERADA SLACK

### *Report of the Director of the University Health Service*

In general, the health of the student body remained excellent during the year, despite the temporary inroads made on it during the autumn when Asiatic influenza struck our community. Lasting about three weeks in its very prevalent stage, about 550 cases among our University and College residences came under our care. Probably many more experienced minor variations of the illness and never sought our help, as is also the case with many other students living at their homes in Toronto. As the number of cases far exceeded our Infirmary capacity, it was necessary to care for the majority in their own place of residence. It is a pleasure to acknowledge the ready assistance and co-operation rendered to us by the various residence officials and staff without whose help our task would have been much more difficult. Fortunately, among those patients under our observation, none developed any serious complication.

While influenza constituted our major epidemic, an outbreak of Salmonella infection occurred in several students of the School of Nursing during June of 1957 while they were on duty off the university premises. A few of the victims were ill for several weeks, but fortunately all recovered uneventfully. No other epidemics were encountered.

All our other usual activities were continued, with our Athletic Injury Service, Psychiatric Consultation Service, and our Infirmary again serving their useful functions to about the average attendance in past years.

Our tuberculosis detection programme continued, with the discovery of two proven active cases and the referral to his own doctor for further investigation of one patient exhibiting doubtful activity, discovered immediately prior to his final examinations. Again, we acknowledge with thanks the assistance rendered in our Chest X-ray Survey, and throughout the year, by the Gage Institute of the National Sanitarium Association.

Salk poliomyelitis vaccine was made available, without charge, by the Department of Health and was administered by ourselves to a total of 5,889 doses to a total of 3,815 students. Many of these had received their first and second inoculations before entering University. However, 2,358 students received their initial dose, 2,074 students received their second dose, and 1,457 students received their third dose under our auspices.

With many of the other departments, at the end of the year, we found ourselves temporarily without quarters as the initial stages of the university expansion plan

---

were instituted. Our sympathies extend to those charged with the very difficult task of co-ordinating all the necessary moves and of soothing the occasional ruffled feelings of those immediately involved. We have every prospect that adequate temporary quarters will again be available to us for the fall term.

Once again, the Director and the Assistant Director (Women's Division) attended the worthwhile Annual Meeting of the American College Health Association.

## *Report of the Director of the University Placement Service*

The University Placement Service is very sensitive to national economic trends. The reduction in employment opportunity during the past year has made it necessary to adjust the emphasis in our procedures in order to provide the type of service most suitable to the immediate needs of the undergraduate body. In so doing, it has shown the necessity for the development of a flexible pattern of administration capable of rapid adaptation to broader economic changes as well as to the seasonal ones.

It should be observed in the following tabulated report of work accomplished during the last year that while demand for the graduating class has been reduced by 20 per cent, the actual number of interviews on campus by employers approximates that of other years. This has meant a closer co-ordination of the time made available to the University by recruitment officers who have visited the campus this year. It has also materially increased the amount of individual student vocational counselling required from this department. Time for this work was secured at the expense of alumni placement.

Summer employment, under the administration of Students' Administrative Council, has increased in interviewing load by 15 per cent. This was in part due to a disquiet among the student body caused by critical shortages of employment in the first two years of all courses. In one group of engineering departments, the Faculty was able to make a personal appeal directly to the industry most concerned, and so place most of their undergraduates. Others were not so fortunate and we expect that lack of summer employment may be reflected in the financial difficulties of many students in their second and third years next year.

We are pleased to record the success of the concentrated engineering recruitment programme which was sponsored by the Faculty of Applied Science and Engineering last year. Owing to the material and willing co-operation of the academic staff, the actual interviewing for full-time employment was effectively condensed into a three-day period. At the same time that this procedure reduced to a minimum the lost academic time caused by students "shopping" a protracted series of companies, it forced the undergraduates to carry out intensive individual research into employment opportunities through their departmental libraries during the pre-Christmas term. In consequence, applicants approached the serious matter of choosing their vocations with a greater maturity and certainty than has been possible before. In carrying out this type of recruitment programme the University of Toronto has broken new ground in the procedures used. In consequence the experiment has aroused intense interest in universities in both Canada and the United States.

The following tabulations give the comparison between the work accomplished during the past and the preceding academic years:
