## *Report of the Director of Athletics and Physical Education for Men*

With the completion of the third year of the new one-year programme in physical education, it is now possible to see some resulting educational benefits and also to predict some serious problems for the years immediately ahead.

The percentage of students failing to complete the requirements has dropped noticeably, from 8 per cent in 1954-5 to 3 per cent during the 1957-8 session.

Swimming activities continued to be an important part of the total programme, and it is gratifying that so many students take advantage of this opportunity to work for and earn the awards of the Royal Life Saving Society and the Canadian Red Cross Water Safety Division. The experience our students gain throughout the session in water safety training and intelligent handling of emergencies is of immense value during the summer months at summer camps, city playgrounds, etc. This year 797 students elected to take swimming and some 450 awards were granted.

Of the total registration of 1,857 freshmen (an increase of 220), 1,539 participated in the required programme. Exemptions for ex-service personnel, for active membership in the C.O.T.C., U.N.T.D., and U.R.T.P., for one year's standing, for medical reasons, and for mature age, accounted for the other 318. In all, 1,173 students selected intercollegiate and intramural athletics, participating in sports of their own choice. The carry-over value of our programme is demonstrated by the fact that of the 4,299 registrations in inter-faculty sports, 57 per cent were from second and higher years.

One important educational value of our programme that is often overlooked is the opportunity offered to students to act as team managers and games and tournament officials. There were 487 student managers and officials this year, 50 per cent of whom were enrolled in first year. Not only does the student benefit in the way of responsibility, but the Department is enabled to operate smoothly and efficiently.

It has been a matter of considerable pride that this department has been able, to date, to provide competition for students in all years. However, with the anticipated increase in enrolment over the next few years, it will become increasingly difficult to fulfil this service on the same scale. The required programme in physical education will, of necessity, be given preference in the use of facilities to the detriment of students in higher years. This situation will continue to test our ingenuity until such time as the new athletic building is a reality.

It is of interest to note that many European students now on the campus are enthusiastic participants in our programme. Indeed, some of them have performed a worthwhile service as volunteer instructors in judo, weight training, gymnastics, swimming, and water polo. This blending of Canadian and European cultures in athletics and physical education has been of great benefit and is making a valuable contribution to the student body in particular and to the University as a whole.

In addition to our regular programme, the department is endeavouring to survey the problem of physical fitness of male university students in all years. This study should provide some interesting and controversial results.

W. A. STEVENS

## *Report of the Secretary of the Athletic Association*

If winning championships is any criterion, our teams made an impressive record by winning intercollegiate titles in exactly half of all sports in which we entered teams—ten senior championships out of twenty and three intermediate championships out of six in which we competed.

In John McManus's third year as senior basketball coach, he and his team distinguished themselves by winning the intercollegiate championship for the first time since 1940, with nine wins out of ten league games and a record of three wins in

---

nine exhibition games played against strong United States college teams. The senior hockey team continued as perennial champions, finishing on top of the four-team league for the fourth successive year. Western bounced back in senior football and took the championship without a playoff. Other senior championships were won in soccer, track, swimming, boxing, fencing, badminton, squash, and sailing. Our intermediate teams won in football, soccer, and harrier.

Inter-faculty competition increased in popularity as shown by the figures—4,299 persons competing in various sports (an increase of 492 over last year), and 383 athletes on intercollegiate teams, to make a grand total of 4,682 entries in our athletic programme.

An era ended with the death of Mr. T. A. Reed, Financial Secretary of the Athletic Association from 1914 to 1947, and architect of the organization we have today. Mr. Reed was the first permanent secretary of the Athletic Association and during his tenure of office the physical plant developed to a remarkable extent—first the move from the temporary gymnasium on what is now the Trinity playing field to the magnificent and (at that time) modern quarters in the Athletic Wing of the new Hart House; then the addition of the east side concrete bleachers at the Stadium in 1924, followed by the completion of Varsity Arena in 1926. The capital cost of the Stadium additions and the new Arena were paid off in full four years before Mr. Reed's retirement and a considerable reserve of cash was available to commence the further modernization of the Stadium to what it is today. His history of the Athletic Association, *The Blue and White*, is a permanent record of the athletic side of the University he loved so well and served so faithfully. We owe him much.

Dr. Wallace Graham completed his second year as President of the Athletic Association, ably assisted by the academic and graduate representatives as well as by the seven undergraduate members.

J. P. LOOSEMORE

### *Report of the Director of Physical Education for Women*

In the session 1952-3 there were 471 women enrolled in this department. In 1957-8 there were 819. During this five-year period there has been one staff member added but no increase in facilities. This year ninety-nine classes were held for a total of twenty-two weeks in seven buildings. Many of these classes were too large for the most effective teaching. It was necessary for the first time to utilize the hour from one to two o'clock to accommodate everyone. In addition, extra space had to be found for the School of Physical and Health Education. This group required thirty-six hours in the gymnasia and the pools. It appears certain that the department has now reached the saturation point in regard to space in which to work.

In the gymnasia some fourteen activities enabled 916 students to take classes once a week. In the four swimming pools thirty-three classes were held weekly with an enrolment of 397. A total of 111 life saving and swimming instructors' examinations were completed. Swimming continues to attract the largest number of students. We were particularly successful in teaching non-swimmers this year. Of the ninety-three non-swimmers who entered in September, all learned to swim and ninety succeeded in passing the test—three lengths of the pool. Apart from swimming, the greatest interest centred in the classes for individual sports such as badminton, golf, and tennis. This year, however, an increased interest in gymnastics has been apparent. It is difficult to say whether this is due to the large numbers of New Canadians now attending the University or to the publicity given to physical fitness. Team sports in the intramural programme provided credit in physical education for 105 students. This is a small number but has been a constant one for many years. It cannot increase until more facilities are available. The staff of the department gave supervision and coaching for many of the activities in this programme. It is a tribute to the ingenuity of the staff and the fortitude of the students that under so many adverse conditions only nine students, less than 1 per cent, failed to complete their requirement.

---

In addition to the activity classes, a brief course in artificial respiration was offered by the staff of the department. Two lectures on health were given by Dr. Marion Hilliard.

For the second year, entering students were given Kraus-Weber tests. This test was a pass or fail performance and was used as a screening device for courses in the physical education programme. It was not meant to determine the degree of physical fitness of the individual but rather to establish a borderline condition of muscular action below which corrective procedures were indicated. Some 734 students were tested, and 103 were deficient in some degree. This year considerable follow-up work was done with these students, with the result that out of the 103 failures sixty-five were able to complete their tests. The study is continuing under Professor Jackson.

One resignation occurred this year, that of Miss Lyn Rutherford. She is being replaced by Mrs. Nancy Webb, a graduate of the University. Miss Eleanor Hopkins, who joined the staff in September, 1957, is continuing with us. To all the staff I extend my grateful thanks. One hopes that they may receive their just reward when the Women's Athletic Building, now in the advanced planning stage, is finally completed.

ZERADA SLACK

### *Report of the Secretary-Treasurer of the Women's Athletic Association*

The highlight of this past year from the point of view of the women athletes of the University was undoubtedly the finalizing of plans for the long-awaited Women's Athletic Building and the clearing of the land on which it is to stand.

The intramural programme has for many years "marked time" until adequate facilities were available for women students. It is hoped that there will be a noticeable increase in participation as soon as space permits. In 1957-8, thanks to student and staff enthusiasm and efficiency, it was possible to organize competitions in eleven of the sports sponsored by the Association.

Victoria College teams again won six of the championships, while the basketball went to second-year Physical and Health Education and the volleyball to the Ontario College of Education. In the major intramural gymnasium sports, twenty-two teams were entered in basketball and fifteen teams in volleyball.

University of Toronto teams this year were entered in six official intercollegiate meets. Of these, Toronto again won the tennis and badminton. Toronto teams also participated in unofficial competition with other teams in indoor archery, bowling, fencing, gymnastics, and intermediate basketball.

The Intercollegiate Badminton Tournament was held at Toronto this year and appreciation is due to the Carlton Club which granted the use of its courts for this event. The men's Athletic Association again most kindly provided accommodation for the intramural basketball and swimming finals.

A. E. M. PARKES

### *Report of the Commanding Officer of the University Naval Training Division*

The University Naval Training Division was designed to train university students to become officers in the Royal Canadian Navy (Reserve). In addition, the U.N.T.D. administers the Regular Officers' Training Plan for the Royal Canadian Navy. Cadets who have successfully completed three periods of winter training and two periods of summer training are promoted to commissioned rank in the R.C.N. or R.C.N.(R).

As in past years, the winter training has been carried out at H.M.C.S. York on

---

the Toronto waterfront, where excellent facilities are available for training. Summer training was carried out at H.M.C.S. *Stadacona* in Halifax, Nova Scotia, and at H.M.C.S. *Naden* in Esquimalt, British Columbia. Summer cruises during 1957 included three cruises to Europe and three cruises along the west coast of Canada and the United States.

In the past year, the quotas of the U.N.T.D. have been cut throughout Canada so that the number of cadets taking summer training on the east and west coasts and at sea would not exceed the number that could be adequately trained with the equipment available. As a result, the number of cadets enrolled in the first year in 1957-8 at the University of Toronto was limited to twenty-five.

Lieutenant (E) D. H. Smith, R.C.N.(R), who has been Staff Officer U.N.T.D. for the past three years, relinquished this appointment in June, 1958, and has transferred to the R.C.N. This officer contributed much to the successful operation of the U.N.T.D. A new Staff Officer U.N.T.D. will not be appointed next term, and considerable reorganization in the administration of the Division will have to take place. Chaplain (P) C. D. Jay replaced W. O. Fennell as Chaplain of the Division; both these officers are on the staff of Emmanuel College. Lt. Cdr. E. R. Fenton will continue as Executive Officer. Instructor Lt. Cdr. W. Lancaster will retire as Navigation Officer after several years of service. His successor has not been announced.

H. U. Ross

### *Report of the Commanding Officer, University of Toronto Contingent, Canadian Officers Training Corps*

The University of Toronto Contingent, C.O.T.C., offers three plans whereby selected undergraduates may qualify for commissions in the Canadian Army while at university. The three-year C.O.T.C. programme is designed for students who intend to enter the militia or the regular army on graduation. Students who join the regular army on the Regular Officer Training Plan receive complete subsidization for up to four years at university, while those accepted for the Subsidized Dental Plan are given financial assistance during their third and fourth dental years. Training in all plans takes place on a one night per week basis during the academic year and for a minimum of twelve weeks during summer holidays.

The intake quota for the C.O.T.C. was sharply reduced this year from forty-five to eighteen. Recruiting was disappointingly slow, perhaps because of the uncertainty as to the future of the militia. The total strength of the unit was reduced to seven contingent officers, forty-nine C.O.T.C., nineteen R.O.T.P., and five subsidized dental students. This total represents a decrease of 30 per cent from last year's strength. Henceforth the Resident Staff Officer is no longer on the establishment of the C.O.T.C. In accordance with the policy announced in December, 1957, the R.S.O., Major H. W. Thomas, was posted out of the unit in May, 1958, and will not be replaced. Major Thomas will be sorely missed in both the training and the administration of the unit.

Changes in the contingent officer establishment included the resignations of Major W. A. C. H. Dobson and Major R. H. Lockwood. In February, 1958, Lt. Col. A. S. Michell, C.D., of the Faculty of Forestry, resigned as Commanding Officer and was succeeded by Lt. Col. A. C. M. Ross, C.D., s.c., of Victoria College. Lt. Col. Michell commanded the unit for over three years and in him the Contingent has lost a much-valued officer. University lecturers assisting with training were Dr. G. Tatham, Department of Geography, and Professors R. A. Spencer and J. T. Saywell of the Department of History. The grant for payments to outside lecturers has been cut for next year by over two-thirds. This will entail the cancellation of the course in military geography and will throw an added burden on the contingent officers.

Members of the unit continued to win honours during summer training. 2/Lt.

---

W. B. Hudgins won the Honour Cadet Shield at the Dental Corps School, and J. G. Edmeads was awarded the trophy as Best Cadet at the Medical Corps School. Six third-year officers were selected for service with the 2 Canadian Infantry Brigade in Europe for the summer of 1958.

A. C. M. Ross

### *Report of the Officer Commanding the University of Toronto Squadron, Royal Canadian Air Force*

In common with the other service units on the campus, the University of Toronto Squadron was restricted by smaller quotas than formerly in its intake of recruits into the reserve training plan. This reduction is in keeping with the change in function of the Squadron indicated in last year's report, and also reflects the changing concept of the function of the reserves in Canada's defence system. It was a matter of great concern, however, that even these reduced quotas could not be filled. This was particularly so in the field of technical services, which also suffered in number of graduands recruited. This unfortunate result was due to an increased campaign of recruitment on the part of industry, and to the fact that the Services are regarded by lower levels of university administration as merely another hiring agency on the campus. The time has come when the plain fact must be recognized and impressed upon all university personnel concerned that the Service units have a different significance in Canada's future and that they must be accorded a treatment consistent with that difference.

Once again there have been several staff changes in the Squadron. Flight-Lieutenant J. R. Kennedy, of the Department of Physical Education, transferred to the Supplementary Reserve, and Flying Officer E. E. Kopetsky, of Whitney Hall, joined the staff as Assistant Training Officer. Squadron Leader A. L. Jewett, the Resident Staff Officer, left to go to Training Command Headquarters, and was replaced by Squadron Leader J. P. McDonald from Staff College. The Assistant Resident Staff Officer, Flying Officer D. R. Watson, was posted to flying duties on jets, and his position will not be filled.

In view of Squadron Leader A. B. Weston's forthcoming sabbatical year away from Canada Flight-Lieutenant W. H. Dray was promoted to Squadron Leader and took over acting command in May, 1958.

A. B. WESTON

### *Report of the General Secretary-Treasurer of the Students' Administrative Council*

It would be practically impossible to list in order of importance the various projects undertaken by the Students' Administrative Council in the year 1957-8. One activity, however, deserves particular mention: the work of the committee concerned with Hungarian Student Relief. Following the revolution in Hungary, the Students' Administrative Council by campaign raised the amount of \$7,800 to help the freedom fighters who came to this country and who were admitted as students to the University of Toronto in the autumn of September, 1957, exclusive of the Sopron Group. Twenty-one such students, seven of them married, were the direct responsibility of the Students' Administrative Council. The committee consisted of Mr. Robert Langlois of Trinity College, Mr. Bruce M. Stewart of the Faculty of Medicine, and Mr. David Watson of the Faculty of Applied Science and Engineering. Information was obtained from the Secretary or Registrar of each faculty or college concerning the names of the Hungarian students registered. A list was compiled from the replies, and extensive questionnaires which had been prepared were sent out to

---

each of these students accompanied by an explanatory letter. Two budgets, one for married students, the other for single, were drawn up and the financial situation of each student was recorded. This information was supplemented by personal interviews in every case, and the needs for clothing, text books, and fees were added to the record. The standard budgets took into account all of the capital of the individual plus any regular income which he might have. Loans were not considered income. The University very generously provided \$400 toward the fees of the students who were being helped by the Council. The balance of any fees was defrayed from the Relief Fund. Regular living expenses, food and rent, were set against monthly income. Deficits were calculated to the standard which the committee set and until the end of the academic year. Some of the students interviewed were found to be financially sound according to the budget drawn up by the Council. Others needed assistance in varying degrees. Since the Council was virtually underwriting nearly thirty people for the year, it was felt essential to have a balance in the fund, preferably in the region of \$1,000, for unexpected emergencies. Trinity College loaned the Council some \$850 for this purpose (money collected for an Arts student); the Hungarian students, the charge of the Council, were all in professional courses. The arrangement was that when the Council fund was expended, the money loaned from Trinity could be drawn upon to whatever extent was necessary. Any balance remaining at the year's end was to revert to Trinity. The committee from the Council can look back with satisfaction at the number of Hungarian students they have looked after, and an amount of \$380 was returned to Trinity College.

Mention should be made of two other campaigns, the United Appeal Campaign and the SHARE Campaign. Both these campaigns were organized through the Council office. Student contributions for the United Appeal amounted to \$6,071. The Council had the co-operation of Professor F. R. Lorriman in this campaign. The total amount collected from staff and students of the University, and turned over to the United Appeal, was \$32,118, an increase of more than \$4,000 over the previous year. The SHARE Campaign, in support of the World University Service of Canada, was successful to the extent that \$5,294 was collected. Of this amount, the students of the Faculty of Applied Science and Engineering contributed \$1,142. This latter amount will be used specifically to bring to the University from Egypt a student who will enrol in the School of Graduate Studies. The student has been selected by correspondence from the National Committee of the World University Service of Canada and the National Committee of the World University Service in Egypt.

All of the standing committees of the Council had a very active year. The Publications Commission published eighty-seven issues of *The Varsity*, the undergraduate newspaper, now in its seventy-seventh year of publication; *Torontonensis*, the annual yearbook of the University of Toronto, and a new publication, the *Freshman's Handbook*.

The Finance Commission is probably the most important commission of the Council. As well as supervising the collections and disbursements of the campaigns already mentioned, the Commission scrutinized and approved the income and expense of all the widely diversified activities which the Council administers.

The Student Service Commission organized a most successful Blood Campaign for the Canadian Red Cross and helped immeasurably in the United Appeal.

The External Affairs Committee is responsible for the administration of the local activities of the National Federation of Canadian University Students, World University Service, and Weekend Exchanges. The Treasure Van sale was sponsored by this Committee and brought in for World University Service the amount of \$1,020. Several seminars were held at Caledon, and the Carabin Weekend, an exchange of forty students between the University of Montreal and the University of Toronto, again took place. For the first time the Council gave a welcoming luncheon to our Montreal visitors which filled the gap of a lack of association between Council members and the visiting Carabins.

The University of Toronto Debating Union instituted a tournament held in Toronto which was attended by twenty debaters (besides Toronto's own eight) from

---

eight Canadian and American universities. The importance of this function held for the first time is illustrated by the fact that a civic luncheon was given to Council members, Toronto and other Canadian debaters, and our visitors from the United States, at which His Worship the Mayor presided.

The Blue and White Society was again responsible for the Homecoming arrangement for graduates marked by the traditional Float Parade which demonstrated the ingenuity and imagination of the student body. The increasingly popular Christmas Tree celebration in the Great Hall of Hart House was arranged by this Committee, as well as the Winter Carnival.

The Council formed a committee under the chairmanship of Mr. Vincent Kelly to discuss with the Librarian the problem of Library hours. Every co-operation was received from the Librarian and his staff and as a result of a number of meetings, a great improvement in library hours to the benefit of the whole student body was achieved.

Other activities of the Council included the sponsorship of the University of Toronto Symphony Orchestra, under the direction of Professor R. Rosevear, and the University Chorus, under the direction of Professor Richard Johnston. The concerts were held in Convocation Hall. At the Inter-University Choral Festival held at the Ontario Agricultural College, the University of Toronto Chorus fittingly represented this University before a large and appreciative audience. The University of Toronto Band, a Council activity, was also a worthy representative of Toronto, at both the home and away intercollegiate games. Each year the Band makes a significant contribution to the Remembrance Day Service.

Other services operated by the Council were the Undergraduate Loan Service, the Housing Service and the Student Book Exchange which sold in excess of \$7,630 worth of used student textbooks. Practically all profits from this operation went to the students; the Council retaining only sufficient money to operate this service.

Without the diligent contribution of every member of the Council, the year would not have been as successful as it was. As has been the case with previous Councils, the personal association of the members of the Council of 1957-8 will be the basis of lasting friendships, and of continued loyalty to the University.

The President of the Students' Administrative Council was Mr. David Price of the Faculty of Medicine; the Vice-President was Miss Diane Hallamore of the Faculty of Applied Science and Engineering; the President's representatives on the Council were the Rev. Father Lavery, Vice-President and Registrar of St. Michael's College, and Professor W. G. MacElhinney of the Faculty of Applied Science and Engineering. It would be difficult, if not impossible, to express adequately the debt which the Council owes to them.

E. A. MACDONALD

---

## RESEARCH

### *Faculty of Arts*

#### ANTHROPOLOGY

*Reported by Professor T. F. McIlwraith*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Professor E. S. Carpenter did ethnological work among the Baleh Iban, Sarawak, Borneo.

Dr. R. W. Dunning has completed his study of social and economic change among the northern Ojibwa. Also completed is a report on present social conditions of the Southampton Island Eskimo for the Northern Research Co-ordination Centre, Department of Northern Affairs.

Professor J. N. Emerson accompanied Professor McIlwraith during the Puckasaw Pit culture investigation on the north shore of Lake Superior in June. He directed the second season's field work of the University of Toronto-National Museum of Canada joint archaeological expedition at Ault Park, Sheek Island, near Cornwall, Ontario, during July and August; carried out the analysis of an ancient dug-out canoe recovered from Haines Lake, near Oak Ridges, Ontario; and directed the annual fall student excavations at the Old Indian Fort site near Vandorf, Ontario. During the fall and spring terms Dr. Emerson continued to co-operate with Dr. J. E. Anderson of the Department of Anatomy in the organization and development of the physical anthropology laboratory.

Professor T. F. McIlwraith continued work on prehistoric archaeological remains on the north shore of Lake Superior.

Dr. W. J. Mayer-Oakes conducted archaeological surveys and an excavation project in Saskatchewan for three months during the summer of 1957 as consultant to the Saskatchewan Provincial Government. Two reports, "An archaeological survey of the Boundary Dam Reservoir" and "Excavations at BDR #6 (the Andrist site), near Estevan, Saskatchewan," were prepared on this work and have been distributed in limited edition by the Saskatchewan Museum of Natural History. During the winter of 1957-8 he completed a study of archaeological materials from the Valley of Mexico and prepared a report, "Excavations at El Risco, Mexico, D.F."

#### ART AND ARCHAEOLOGY

*Reported by Professor P. H. Brieger*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Professor P. H. Brieger began research on fourteenth- and early fifteenth-century illustrations to Dante's *Divine Comedy* and continued to collect material on illuminated Bibles in the thirteenth century. Under the auspices of the National Gallery of Canada he made a survey of art departments and the methods of teaching history of art at the universities in the Maritimes and Quebec.

Professor J. W. Graham carried on with his studies of Bronze Age palaces of Crete and on objects in the Greek collection in the Royal Ontario Museum.

Professor G. S. Vickers completed his survey of the early mediaeval manuscripts in the collection of the Philadelphia Free Library.

Dr. W. H. Vitzthum is doing research on Italian Mannerist drawings.

Professor F. E. Winter during the summer months travelled in Italy, Sicily, Yugoslavia, and Turkey, on research grants from the University of Toronto and the American Philosophical Society. He was engaged in a study of Classical and Hellen-

---

istic Greek fortifications and town-planning. During the winter he has been working over the data collected on this trip.

## ASTRONOMY

*Reported by Professor J. F. Heard*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Professor J. F. Heard has continued studies of radial velocities of selected stars and of the orbital elements of two eclipsing variable stars, and has initiated observations of artificial satellites in collaboration with international groups.

Professor Helen S. Hogg has continued observations of globular star clusters, and has undertaken extensive bibliographies of both globular and galactic star clusters.

Professor Donald A. MacRae, in collaboration with members of the Department of Electrical Engineering, has developed equipment for radio observations of the sun and radio stars. He has also initiated studies of equivalent widths of stellar absorption lines, and has interpreted the variation of the orbital elements of artificial satellites in terms of atmospheric densities.

Professor Ruth J. Northcott has continued studies of the orbital elements of three spectrographic binary stars.

Professor Oke has developed an exposure meter for the spectrograph of the 74-inch telescope; has made a study of the variation of energy distribution in several Cepheid variable stars; and has continued the determination of spectroscopic absolute magnitudes of selected stars.

Professor L. Searle has made a theoretical study of the recombination spectrum of interstellar hydrogen; has made an analysis of the chemical composition of the star R Coronae Borealis; and has initiated a spectrophotometric study of selected stars of population II.

Graduate students have completed the following theses:

*For the M.A. degree:*

DEMARQUE, P. (under the direction of Professor J. B. Oke). Construction of stellar interior models using electronic computation.

## BOTANY

*Reported by Professor D. S. Van Fleet*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

BAILEY, D. L. Researches continued on *Cladosporium fulvum*, the tomato leaf mould fungus; initiation of comparative studies of three fungus parasites of strawberry leaves.

BANNAN, M. W. Factors influencing cell length in coniferous woods.

BRAY, J. R. Analysis of data on atmospheric carbon dioxide concentration during the past hundred years. Multidimensional rating techniques for plant communities. Primary productivity in terrestrial ecosystems.

CAIN, R. F. Investigations of mycological and bryological flora. Thirty-four additional species of Hepaticae recorded for Ontario. Field studies of fungi in Southern and Northern Ontario, California, and the Rocky Mountain States. Cultural characteristics and taxonomy of Ascomycetes, especially *Ceratocystis*, *Podospora*, *Coniochaeta*, and *Microascus*, and coprophilous fungi of Tahiti and soil fungi of Australia. Cryptogamic herbarium increased by 1,997 fungi and lichens and 1,434 bryophytes, bringing the totals in the herbarium to 121,758 and 27,984 respectively; the herbarium total is 149,742 specimens.

---

DUFF, G. H. Metabolic physiology of the growing point. Growth and morphogenesis in forest trees. Flavonoid compounds of plants, anthocyanin and associated pigments.

FORWARD, DOROTHY F. Metabolism of organic acids of potato tubers in relation to oxygen supply.

GARAY, L. A. Studies of tropical American orchids as a Guggenheim Fellow on sabbatical leave. Preparation of monographs at Harvard University.

MORGAN-JONES, J. F. Perithecial development in the *Gnomoniaceae*.

ROTHFELS, K. H. Comparative cytology of black flies. Chromosomes of mammalian cells in tissue culture (with L. Siminovitch). Cytotaxonomy of species of *Anemone* (with M. Heimburger).

SOPER, J. H. Geographical distribution of vascular plants of Ontario. Completion of the first section of a book on the wild plants of eastern Canada, with Dr. A. S. Rao. The vascular plant herbarium was enlarged by the addition of 4,352 specimens, bringing the total to 104,826 sheets.

VAN FLEET, D. S. Histochemical studies of enzyme localization in cell division and tissue origin.

Graduate students have completed the following theses:

*For the M.A. degree:*

CHANG, P. S. Y. (under the direction of Professor Rothfels). Cytological studies on four species of *Papaver* and their hybrids.

COULTHARD, Miss A. M. (under the direction of Professor Bailey). Studies on the histology of infection by *Cladosporium fulvum* Cooke.

DUNBAR, R. W. (under the direction of Professor Rothfels). The salivary gland chromosomes of seven forms of black flies included in *Eusimulium aureum* Fries.

HAUSCHILD, A. H. W. (under the direction of Professor Duff). The interconversion of serine and glycine in *Zea mays* L.

*For the Ph.D. degree:*

BASRUR, V. R. (under the direction of Professor Rothfels). Cytotaxonomic studies in black flies and midges.

JULIEN, J. B. (under the direction of Professor Bailey and Professor Rothfels). Chromosome behaviour in the ascus of *Venturia inaequalis*.

## CHEMISTRY

*Reported by Professor A. R. Gordon*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

BEAMISH, F. E. Chemistry of the platinum metals.

BROOK, A. G. Organosilicon and organogermanium chemistry. Stable free radicals.

GORDON, A. R. Theory of solutions.

LE ROY, D. J. Kinetics of atomic and free radical reactions. Photochemistry.

LISTER, M. W. Periodate and tellurate complexes of nickel and cobalt. Decomposition and certain reactions of the hypochlorite ion.

MCBRYDE, W. A. E. Absorptiometry. Formation of chelate complexes of certain transition elements.

MCINTOSH, R. Physical chemistry of gas solid interfaces.

PAGE, J. A. Photochemistry of riboflavin.

POLANYI, J. C. Reactions involving excited states. Pulse photolysis.

RAE, J. J. Chemical mechanisms involved in dental caries.

WETMORE, F. E. W. Electrode processes and molten salts.

WRIGHT, G. F. Oxymercuration of norbornenedicarboxylic esters. Demercuration of organomercurials. Calculations of molecular structure. Electric moment determinations.

---

Graduate students have completed the following theses:

*For the M.A. degree:*

CHIU, D. D.-K. (under the direction of Professor Wright). Oxymercuration of bridged cyclooctenes.

McINTYRE, J. D. E. (under the direction of Professor Wetmore). Mechanism of electrode processes: a study of hydrogen overpotential on aluminium.

MARKS, A. G. (under the direction of Professor Beamish). Separation of rhodium and iridium from base metals by ion exchange.

*For the Ph.D. degree:*

BEECKMANS, J. M. L. (under the direction of Professor McIntosh). Study of the surface structure of sodium chloride of high specific surface.

BERMAN, S. S. (under the direction of Professor McBryde). Ion exchange studies: the separation of the platinum metals. An absorptiometric determination of iridium with tin (II) chloride and hydrobromic acid.

DIEFENDORF, R. J. (under the direction of Professor Le Roy). Kinetics of the reactions of atomic hydrogen with propane.

MURPHY, J. W. (under the direction of Professor Wetmore). Electrical conductivity in molten salt systems.

SAWATZKY, H. (under the direction of Professor Wright). I. Conformation of organomercurials. II. Polymercuration of benzene. III. Conformation of 2,5-dimethyl-2,5-dimethoxy-3,4-diphenylhexanes.

#### CLASSICS, UNIVERSITY COLLEGE

*Reported by Professor R. J. Getty*

Professor G. Bagnani is proceeding with his critical edition of Petronius.

Professor F. M. Heichelheim has continued his work on the Giessen papyri and ostraca, on the Greek coins in the Fitzwilliam Museum, Cambridge, on Celtic gods, and on his *Ancient History* and *Ancient Economic History*.

Mrs. F. M. H. Norwood is proceeding with her work on the ancient novel.

Professor R. M. H. Shepherd has been engaged in a study of Greek choral lyric, especially in Aeschylus and Pindar.

Professor M. D. C. Tait has made further progress with his work on myth and dialectic in Plato in libraries abroad.

Professor D. F. S. Thomson has continued his studies of Catullus and of Erasmus and other Renaissance and post-Renaissance Latin literature.

Professor W. P. Wallace has done further work on the coins of Euboia.

Professor L. E. Woodbury has advanced his studies in the history of Greek thought, particularly those concerning Protagoras of Abdera.

#### CLASSICS, VICTORIA COLLEGE

*Reported by Professor H. G. Robertson*

Professor D. de Montmolin is proceeding with his work on the origins of aesthetics and rhetoric in ancient Greece.

Professor J. R. Grant has begun a new study of Aristotle's *Constitution of Athens*.

Professor G. L. Keyes has been investigating some phases of early Greek religion and is making a study of the political thought of St. Augustine.

#### CLASSICS, TRINITY COLLEGE

*Reported by Professor G. M. A. Grube*

Professor G. M. A. Grube continued his study of the Greek and Roman literary critics.

Professor Mary White is continuing her work on the Tyrannies in early Greece.

Professor A. Dalzell is making a study of literary circles in Augustan Rome.

---

CLASSICS, GRADUATE DEPARTMENT

*Reported by Professor G. M. A. Grube*

Graduate students have completed the following theses:

*For the Ph.D. degree:*

McQUEEN, D. J. Superbia under St. Augustine.

EAST ASIATIC STUDIES

*Reported by Professor W. A. C. H. Dobson*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Professor W. A. C. H. Dobson has been engaged in preparing the manuscript of "The Grammar of Early Archaic Chinese," being volume II of a historical grammar of the Chinese language, of which volume I, "Late Archaic Chinese," is presently in the press.

Professor C. C. Shih has been engaged in studies of society in Late Chou times, and has continued with his history of Chinese Classicism in *Han* times.

Mr. Richard H. Robinson has completed his doctoral thesis "Madhyamika studies in fifth-century China", which has been submitted to the University of London. He has also engaged in studies in the history of Chinese Buddhism from A.D. 200-600, and has collaborated in research for a grammar of modern English.

Mr. R. M. Smith has engaged in studies in Indian chronology.

ENGLISH, UNIVERSITY COLLEGE

*Reported by Professor A. S. P. Woodhouse*

Members of the staff have engaged in the following research and critical studies: Professor A. S. P. Woodhouse on Milton, preparing and editing the first volume of the Columbia Variorum Commentary on Milton's poems; Professor N. J. Endicott on his study of Sir Thomas Browne (which he is continuing in the British Isles in the summer of 1958); Professor J. R. MacGillivray on Wordsworth; Professor F. E. L. Priestley on the influence of Newton in English literature and thought in the eighteenth century, which he proposes to conclude in the term's leave of absence granted for 1959; Professor H. S. Wilson on Gabriel Harvey; Professor D. B. Grant on a new study of Hazlitt; Professor J. B. Bessinger on a book on the Robin Hood cycle, and on a concordance of Anglo-Saxon poetry (with the Rev. J. F. Madden of St. Michael's College) and a dictionary of Anglo-Saxon poetry (also with the Rev. J. F. Madden); Dr. F. W. Watt on two studies in Chaucer, to be completed in the summer of 1958 in Great Britain.

ENGLISH, VICTORIA COLLEGE

*Reported by Professor H. N. Frye*

Professor Kathleen Coburn is continuing her monumental edition of *The Notebooks of Samuel Taylor Coleridge*, of which the first part appeared in the fall of 1957. Professor Kenneth Maclean is continuing research on the theme of sensibility in the latter half of the eighteenth century. Professor Millar MacLure, after completing his study of the Paul's Cross Sermons, is now at work on a study of George Chapman, the Elizabethan poet, dramatist and translator of Homer. Professor David Hoeniger is continuing work on his edition of Shakespeare's *Pericles*, for the New Arden edition of Shakespeare. Professor Northrop Frye, whose *Anatomy of Criticism* appeared in the spring of 1957, is continuing research in the field of critical theory. Professors Frye and MacLure are also working on a projected literary history of Canada.

---

ENGLISH, TRINITY COLLEGE

*Reported by Professor Gordon Roper*

Professor Arthur Barker's fellowship at the Folger Library, Washington, in 1957, was renewed for the summer of 1958 to enable him to continue research on his work on Sir Thomas More's *Utopia*.

Professor George Falle was awarded a grant by the Folger Library, Washington, to enable him to carry on research there during the spring on John Dryden.

Professor Gordon Roper and his colleague Professor Harrison Hayford of Northwestern University have completed an annotated bibliography of the writings of and on Herman Melville.

ENGLISH, GRADUATE DEPARTMENT

*Reported by Professor H. S. Wilson*

Graduate students have completed the following theses:

*For the M.A. degree:*

ALSTON, R. C. A study of the relationship between the signed poems of Cynewulf and the suggested Latin sources.

BRASWELL, Mrs. L. N. Saint Eustace and "Sir Isumbras": a study of relationships between the saint's legend and the Middle English romance.

BRINK, A. W. A study of the development of Bertrand Russell's social criticism, with special reference to his essays.

FLAHIFF, F. T. C. A study of the mystical elements in the writings of Emily Brontë.

JOHNSTONE, C. K. Joyce's use of metamorphosis in *Chamber Music* and the *Portrait*.

KNOWLTON, Sister MARY ARTHUR. The treatment of the Church by the authors of a group of Middle English romances.

VICHERT, G. S. The effect of courtly love on the Gawain cycle.

*For the Ph.D. degree:*

BILSLAND, J. W. De Quincey's theory of literature of power.

MALLOCH, A. E. A critical study of Donne's *Biathanatos*.

REANEY, J. The influence of Spenser on Yeats.

FRENCH, UNIVERSITY COLLEGE

*Reported by Dr. J. A. Walker*

Members of the staff have been continuing the following research projects:

BALTHAZARD, I. G. "La pensée sociale et morale dans l'œuvre d'Eugène Brieux"; correspondent contributor for French VII, Bibliography.

FINCH, ROBERT. Lyric poetry in the age of Louis XV.

HAYNE, D. M. Continuing research in modern French-Canadian literature.

HUMPHREYS, H. L. Bibliography of French syntax.

JEANNERET, F. C. A. Continuing research on C.-F. Ramuz.

PARSONS, C. R. "L'art dramatique en France des origines à nos jours."

ROUILLARD, C. D. Following *The Turk in French History, Thought and Literature, 1520-1660*, published in 1940, the author is preparing a continuation covering the age of Louis XIV.

SANOUILLET, M. "L'histoire de Dada."

FRENCH, VICTORIA COLLEGE

*Reported by Professor W. H. Trethewey*

Members of the staff were engaged in research as follows:

COOK, A. L. Louise Labé.

RIESE, LAURE. Les salons littéraires parisiens de la Princesse Mathilde à nos jours.

TRETHEWEY, W. H. The French text of the *Ancrene Riwe*.

WOOD, J. S. Sondages dans le roman français du 19<sup>e</sup> siècle au point de vue sociale.

---

ROMANCE LANGUAGES AND LITERATURES, GRADUATE DEPARTMENT

*Reported by Professor J. H. Parker*

Graduate students have completed the following theses:

*For the Ph.D. degree:*

BARCLAY, T. B. The role of the dance and dance lyrics in the Spanish *Comedia* to the early eighteenth century.

CURRAN, Miss V. I. Philippe-Joseph Aubert de Gaspé: his life and works.

DAWSON, T. L. C. Religion and anticlericalism in the novels of Benito Pérez Galdós.

UKAS, M. W. The theory and practice of Italian tragicomedy during the sixteenth century.

GEOGRAPHY

*Reported by Professor D. F. Putnam*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Professor D. F. Putnam has completed a revision of *Canadian Regions*.

Professor George Tatham has continued his studies on the history of geographic thought.

Professors D. P. Kerr and Jacob Spelt have undertaken studies on the location of manufacturing industries in Southern Ontario.

Mr. Georges Potvin has carried on some studies on changes in land use in the City of Toronto.

Mr. Ali Tayyeb spent some time investigating the recreational resources of the Owen Sound area.

Mr. W. G. Dean is studying the patterns of land form and vegetation in Northern Ontario.

Mr. F. B. Watts is investigating the records of original vegetation in the Prairie Provinces.

Mr. David Erskine is working on land use problems in Prince Edward Island.

Miss Joan Goodfellow has begun work on some land use problems in New Brunswick.

Miss Sona Ozanian is studying factors relating to industrial location in Southern Ontario.

Graduate students have completed the following theses:

*For the M.A. degree:*

CUNNINGHAM, G. L. The Manitoulin District: a geographical survey.

*For the Ph.D. degree:*

WOOD, H. A. The Département du Nord, Republic of Haiti: a study of land utilization and settlement.

GEOLOGICAL SCIENCES

*Reported by Professor G. B. Langford*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Professor F. W. Beales: (1) A continuation of the research on the genesis of limestones with particular emphasis on precipitated limestones. (2) Stratigraphic research on the Black River Group in Southern Ontario.

Professor R. E. Deane: (1) Research in sedimentation in a glacial lake, glaciers, and glacial geology. This work was carried out on an expedition to northern Ellesmere Island for the International Geophysical Year from May until August, 1957. (2) A study and analysis of the samples obtained from the Lake Hazen area, the results of which will be used for a final report on the area. (3) A continuation of the investigation into the surficial geology of the Welland Canal area. The results of these

---

investigations were submitted to the St. Lawrence Seaway Authority. (4) Continuation of the preparation of an "Index and Annotated Bibliography of the Pleistocene Geology of Canada."

Professor M. A. Fritz: (1) A study of the coral fauna of the Upper Abitibi River Limestone, James Bay Region. (2) A study of the coral fauna from Silurian, Lake Timiskaming.

Dr. D. H. Gorman: A continuation of research on the development of clay minerals.

Professor W. W. Moorhouse: Preparation of a book on the study of rocks in thin section.

Professor E. W. Nuffield (with P. R. Van Loan): (1) An X-ray diffraction study of the two triclinic minerals roehmerite and leightovite. (2) X-ray diffraction studies of dust on the lungs of humans and laboratory animals as a part of an investigation by Dr. A. M. Fisher of the School of Hygiene. (3) An investigation of the precession X-ray diffraction technique resulting in the preparation of a manuscript on the subject suitable for students at the graduating level.

Professor P. A. Peach: (1) Calibration of Free Fall magnetic separator, a continuation of research on the laboratory separation of minerals on the basis of mass susceptibility. (2) Studies of the anomalous electrical conductivity of serpentine minerals.

Professor F. G. Smith: Calculation of solubility and ionization of solutes in aqueous solutions at high temperatures and pressures.

Graduate students have completed the following theses:

*For the M.A. degree:*

DELL, C. I. (under the direction of Professor F. W. Beales). A mineralogical analysis of some Pleistocene sands of Southern Ontario.

*For the M.A.Sc. degree:*

INNES, G. M. (under the direction of Professor W. H. Gross). Economic significance of the distribution of metals through geological time.

SINCLAIR, A. J. (under the direction of Professor W. H. Gross). An evaluation of the Erzberg molybdenite deposit, Greenland.

*For the Ph.D. degree:*

SAHA, A. K. (under the direction of Professors W. W. Moorhouse and R. E. Deane). The mode of emplacement of some granitic plutons in southeastern Ontario.

#### GERMAN, UNIVERSITY COLLEGE

*Reported by Professor H. Boeschenstein*

Professor M. Sinden is continuing her work on the verse plays of Gerhart Hauptmann. Dr. L. Hofrichter has completed a book on the German poet Heinrich Heine. Dr. H. Wiebe has continued his study on the reconciliation of form and passion in Goethe and Kant. Professor H. N. Milnes is continuing work on the mediaeval lyric. Professor H. Boeschenstein has completed a study of biographical problems in the German novel.

#### GERMANIC LANGUAGES AND LITERATURE, GRADUATE DEPARTMENT

*Reported by Professor H. Boeschenstein*

Graduate students have completed the following theses:

*For the M.A. degree:*

MCMANUS, JOHN E. Hermann Hesse's attitude toward education.

RESKE, HERMANN. Melchior Meyr als Dramatiker.

SWEDIUK, Miss HELEN. The role of man and society in the works of Carl Zuckmayer.

---

HISTORY

*Reported by Professor D. G. Creighton*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Professor J. B. Conacher has continued research on the Peelites and the party system, 1846-59.

Professor G. M. Craig has been working on a history of Upper Canada.

Professor D. G. Creighton has carried on his research in Canadian history.

Professor H. I. Nelson continued research on the diplomacy of the First World War and on the peace-making, 1919.

Dr. W. W. Piepenburg has continued research on the English Revolution, 1640-60, with special attention to the impact of the revolution on ecclesiastical institutions.

Mr. E. E. Rose has been engaged in research on problems in the early history of English legislation.

Dr. J. T. Saywell has continued his work on the Canadian party system.

Professor R. A. Spencer carried on his research on the German *Bund*, 1815-66.

Graduate students have completed the following research work:

*For the M.A. degree:*

BRÜCKMANN, J. English coronations, 1216-1308.

KEYSERLINGK, R. H. The German National Liberal party in the Reichstag, 1871-7: structure and politics.

SANDQUIST, T. A. English coronation ceremonies, 1377 to 1429.

THURLOW, J. T. Opinion on Theodore Roosevelt's American domestic policy from some central Canadian newspapers, 1901-1909.

*For the Ph.D. degree:*

FRENCH, G. S. Wesleyan Methodism in Upper Canada and the Maritime Provinces: the heroic age, 1780-1855.

ITALIAN, SPANISH AND PORTUGUESE

*Reported by Professor G. L. Stagg*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Mr. T. B. Barclay completed his study of the dance and dance lyrics in the Spanish *comedia*, which was accepted by the University of Toronto for the degree of Ph.D.

Professor J. Cano has continued his examination of secular poetic folklore of districts of the Province of Toledo, Spain.

Professor S. B. Chandler is making a study of the Italian novel in the nineteenth century, with special reference to Giovanni Verga.

Professor B. M. Corrigan is investigating Italian problems in the Coleridge Notebooks.

Professors Corrigan and J. A. Molinaro have completed their translation of Castiglione's *Il Cortegiano*.

Mr. T. L. C. Dawson completed his study of religion and anti-clericalism in the novels of Benito Pérez Galdós, which was accepted by the University of Toronto for the degree of Ph.D.

Dr. U. Leo has completed studies of Mazzanti, the *Poema de Mio Cid*, and a literary motif in Goldoni and Molière. He is also preparing a paper on Dante and a book entitled "Stilforschung. Methodische Betrachtungen."

Professor K. L. Levy has continued his research in Antioqueño literature.

Professor D. Marín has carried on research for a short history of Spanish civilization.

Mr. W. T. McCready has pursued further his investigations into heraldry in

---

Spanish drama and continued work on a bibliography of articles dealing with the early Spanish theatre.

Professor Molinaro has completed two papers on Castiglione's translators and is preparing a critical edition of Pietro Aretino's *De le lagrime d'Angelica*.

Professor Molinaro and Mr. McCready have brought to a conclusion their critical edition of Cañizares' *Angélica y Medoro*.

Professors Molinaro and J. H. Parker and Miss E. Rugg have completed their bibliography of *comedias sueltas* in the University of Toronto Library.

Professor Parker has continued his studies of the literary relations of Lope de Vega and Juan Pérez de Montalván.

Professor G. C. Patterson has studied the authorship of the *Libro de Alexandre*.

Professor G. L. Stagg has continued his inquiries into the composition and revision of the works of Cervantes.

Professor N. H. Tayler has investigated the origins of the theatre of Tamayo y Baus.

Mr. M. W. Ukas completed his study of the theory and practice of Italian tragicomedy during the sixteenth century, which was accepted by the University of Toronto for the degree of Ph.D.

## MATHEMATICS

*Reported by Professor I. R. Pounder*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Professors J. H. Chung and D. A. S. Fraser have extended their results on randomization tests for a multivariate two-sample problem.

Professor A. J. Coleman has brought work begun ten years ago to completion with the discovery of a simple method of evaluating the Betti numbers of compact Lie groups. He also discovered a new recursive method of calculating the characters of the symmetric group, and has expressed Weyl's formula for the dimensions of the irreducible representations of simple Lie groups in terms of the fundamental invariants of the groups. He has made a significant step forward on the problem of giving an intrinsic characterization of the density matrix for the many fermion problem.

Professor H. S. M. Coxeter has directed the research of Mr. D. W. Crowe on groups generated by unitary reflections, and of Mr. J. H. Lindsay on non-orientable surfaces. With Professor Tutte he investigated quadrics in finite projective spaces.

Professor G. F. D. Duff has continued his work on the theory of mixed boundary value problems for hyperbolic differential equations. He is supervising the Ph.D. thesis of Mr. Martin Eisen which will be an application of this theory to differential systems.

Professor Fraser has completed his book on statistics.

Dr. Hiroshi Nagao has studied the modular representation of symmetric groups and has improved a method of Robinson-Taulbee.

Professor R. B. Potts has worked on problems of traffic dynamics and the application of statistics to traffic flow.

Professor G. de B. Robinson has continued his studies on the representations of the symmetric group and has directed the preparation of a Ph.D. thesis by Miss D. M. Johnson.

Dr. R. O. A. Robinson has been studying various schemes for classifying the newly discovered fundamental particles. He has recently completed a paper on the competitive production in high-energy collisions of the two basic types of heavy nucleon.

Professor P. G. Rooney continued his studies of the Gauss and Laplace transformations. He also studied the representation theory of the Fourier transformation and of Watson transformations. He continued to direct the work of Dr. Dinesh Chandra, a National Research Council post-doctoral fellow.

---

Professor J. A. Steketee has continued work on the application of the theory of dislocations to geophysical problems.

Professor A. H. Wallace has completed work on analytic equivalence of algebroid curves. He has in course of preparation a study of co-bounding varieties.

#### NEAR EASTERN STUDIES, UNIVERSITY COLLEGE

*Reported by Professor F. V. Winnett*

Mr. W. G. Lambert continued work on the Akkadian wisdom texts.

Professor W. S. McCullough has been preparing numerous articles for the forthcoming "Interpreter's Dictionary of the Bible."

Professor J. W. Wevers has been investigating prosodic features in linguistic structures, and form criticism of Hebrew poetry.

Professor G. M. Wickens has been working on the final stages of his "Dictionary of Modern Arabic," and has translated an important work on eschatology by the Arab mystic al-Muhāsibī.

Professor R. J. Williams has been preparing articles for the "Interpreter's Dictionary of the Bible," and for Hasting's "Dictionary of the Bible." He has also completed a manuscript on "Coptic Papyri and Ostraca in the Giessen Universitätsbibliothek," and continued work on the history of the fable in the literature of the ancient Near East.

Professor F. V. Winnett has been engaged in preparing the final report on the excavations at Dhibān, Transjordan, in 1950-1.

#### PHILOSOPHY

*Reported by Professor F. H. Anderson*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Professor F. H. Anderson has been making ready for publication his Arensberg Lectures on "Francis Bacon: His Career and his Thought."

Professor W. Dray has been examining the topic "Speculative philosophy of history as a mode of inquiry."

Professor D. P. Dryer has completed a monograph entitled *Kant on the Verifiability of Metaphysics*.

Professor I. T. Eschmann is studying the influence of late mediaeval legalistic philosophies in certain traditional misrepresentations of the ethical doctrine of Aquinas.

Mr. D. Gallop has been preparing translations of a number of the lesser known philosophical Latin texts of Francis Bacon.

Professors E. Gilson and A. C. Pegis are examining the role of theology in a Christian metaphysic.

Professor T. A. Goudge is continuing his research in the philosophical aspects of evaluation, with special reference to the history of man.

Professor W. M. D. Long is engaged in a re-examination of Dewey's philosophy of education.

Professor L. E. Lynch is conducting research in order to ascertain precisely the specific amendments made to the doctrines of Aristotle's *Organon* by Aquinas and other later mediaeval logicians.

Professor R. F. McRae has been writing on the unification of the sciences by French thinkers of the eighteenth century.

Professor A. A. Maurer has been preparing the Patristic and Mediaeval Section of Doubleday's forthcoming "History of Philosophy."

Professor J. Owens has completed his "History of Ancient Western Philosophy."

---

Mr. G. B. Payzant has been investigating "propriety" as a factor in aesthetical theory.

Professor D. Savan is continuing his study of the expression of Spinoza's philosophy in his life as contrasted with his writings.

Dr. C. W. Webb has been examining the antinomies involved in the formulation of an ontology. One of these, the "Antinomy of Individual," is the subject of a paper.

Dr. J. M. O. Wheatley has been making a semantic analysis of "optative concepts."

Graduate students have completed the following theses:

*For the M.A. degree:*

BRODEUR, C. C. The development of Ralph Barton Perry's general theory of value.

BROENEN, R. A. The habit of *Synderesis* in the philosophy of St. Thomas.

CATAN, J. R. The unicity or plurality of the separate substances in Book Lambda of Aristotle's *Metaphysics*.

MACKINNON, L. A. *The Phaedrus*: the immortality of the soul and its relation to the rest of the dialogue.

NIETFELD, F. J. Aristotle's concept of matter in *Physics*, Book I.

O'CONNOR, D. D. The theory of *suppositio* in the logic of Peter of Spain and Thomas Aquinas.

PECKHAM, J. B. Truth in the Proposition—according to St. Thomas in his Commentary on the *Posterior Analytics*.

SCHEUERMANN, R. The signification of *est* in the Proposition according to St. Thomas Aquinas.

SNYDER, J. J. The basis of friendship for the happy man as outlined in the eighth and ninth books of the *Nicomachean Ethics* of Aristotle.

YEE, R. W. The inapplicability of the Aristotelian categories to God in the *De Divisione Naturae* of John the Scot.

*For the Ph.D. degree:*

GREGOR, M. J. Kant's Applied Ethics: a study of the application of the Categorical Imperative in the *Tugendlehre* of the *Metaphysik der Sitten*.

HARDY, H. L. M. Causality in emergent evolution.

HENRY, W. C. The analysis of knowledge in John Stuart Mill and William Whewell.

LAPIERRE, M. J. The objective concept: a study in the noetical theory of Gabriel Vasquez.

POOLE, C. F. A critical examination of Hobhouse's theory of knowledge.

ALLIN, E. J. Low temperature spectroscopy.

CRAWFORD, M. F. Induced infrared absorptions.

FARQUHAR, R. M. Relation of the isotopic constitution of lead in lead ores to the geological ages of the ores.

HALLETT, A. C. H. Flow properties of liquid helium; electrical conductivity of dilute metal alloys at low temperatures; variation with temperature of the coefficient of thermal expansion of metals.

---

MCNEILL, K. G. Measurement of very small radioactivities, including those of the human body.

RUSSELL, R. D. Relation of the isotopic constitution of lead in lead ores to the geological ages of the ores.

SCOTT, D. G. Evaporated metal films.

STEENBERG, N. R. S-matrix theory (with I. T. Wang). Fission and alpha decay of oriented nuclei (with R. Sharma).

STRIJLAND, J. C. The influence of high pressure on some physical properties of gases and liquids.

WELSH, H. L. Raman spectroscopy of gases. Spectroscopy of gases at high pressures.

WILSON, J. T. The age and manner of formation of mountains and of the earth's crust.

*For the M.A. degree:*

CARSWELL, A. I. Preliminary work on the measurement of diffusion in compressed gases.

CHAMBERS, J. F. A.C. demagnetization as a method of palaeomagnetic cleaning.

HARVEY, J. S. M. Accurate presentation of a derivative function on a cathode ray oscillograph.

KISS, Z. J. Induced rotational infrared spectrum of hydrogen.

KRIEGLER, R. Infrared photo-electric spectrophotometer for short light flashes.

LANDAU, A. Study of the visible region in the absorption spectrum of  $\alpha$ -oxygen.

MADAIAH, N. Development of a superconducting thermometer.

MAY, A. D. The Raman effect of gases at high pressures.

STRANGWAY, D. Theoretical background and design of an apparatus for measuring the variation of susceptibility of rocks with temperature.

WANG, I. T. On time-dependent perturbation theory.

*For the Ph.D. degree:*

BARRINGTON, R. E. Probability of multiple emissions of secondary electrons.

CHANDRA, S. The effect of substrate temperature on evaporated silver films.

CHO, C. W. Effects of intermolecular forces on the infrared electronic absorption bands of oxygen.

EISELE, K. M. An experimental study of dissipative mechanisms in liquid helium.

HOOD, P. J. Palaeomagnetic studies of some Precambrian rocks in Ontario.

MACDONALD, R. E. Field induced absorption in hydrogen.

STEVENS, J. R. The mechanical behaviour of a high polymer over a temperature range which includes the rubberlike and the glasslike states.

VASEK, L. J. Photo-electric effect in insulators.

## POLITICAL ECONOMY

*Reported by Professor V. W. Bladen*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Professor F. N. Beard was working on a book on the theory and practice of Canadian accounting.

Professor V. W. Bladen continued his studies of the classical literature of political economy.

Professor A. Brady continued his studies of British democracy.

Professor J. Burnet continued her study of Puritanism in Ontario.

Professor S. D. Clark did work on the social effects of suburban development in Canada since World War II.

Professor A. W. Currie began work on a study of the capital market in Canada, 1800-1950.

---

Professor J. H. Dales did research on the growth of manufacturing industry in Ontario and Quebec, from the standpoint of (a) energy supplies and (b) the theory of the location of industry.

Professor W. T. Easterbrook continued working on his study of the climate of enterprise, a study in comparative economic history.

Professor H. C. Eastman completed research on some aspects of speculation in the Canadian foreign exchange market and began research on pricing policy and the Canadian tariff.

Professor J. E. Eayrs finished writing "Canada in World Affairs, 1955-1957," for the Canadian Institute of International Affairs; resumed research for a projected first volume of "A Diplomatic History of Canada, 1898-1919"; and completed an essay on Canadian-American relations for the American-European Foundation.

Professor V. C. Fowke (Harold A. Innis Visiting Professor in Economics, from the University of Saskatchewan) worked on his study on food and agriculture in Canada, 1939-50.

Professor P. W. Fox continued research on the political theory of Louis XIV and on various aspects of Canadian government.

Professor P. J. Giffen did work on the social backgrounds of juvenile delinquents.

Professor O. Hall continued work on the movements of population to newly industrialized communities, and on family responses to the crises of illness.

Mr. D. G. Hartle began a study of the effect of welfare programmes on the distribution of income in Canada, and continued his study of the characteristics of the employment predictions of a sample of employers.

Professor K. F. Helleiner worked on the origins of egalitarian ideas, and a history of subsistence crises.

Professor Wm. C. Hood continued his preparation of a second study for the Royal Commission on Canada's Economic Prospects to be entitled "Financing of Economic Activity in Canada."

Professor B. S. Keirstead completed his work of the past four years for the Government of Newfoundland and continued his work on the theory of capital.

Mr. H. I. Macdonald continued his work on the relation of foreign investment in Canada to Canadian economic development since 1926.

Professor D. C. MacGregor continued his study of the financial aspects of social security.

Professor C. B. Macpherson continued work on formative influences in democratic theory.

Professor N. Morse (on leave from Acadia University) worked on his study on agriculture of Nova Scotia.

Mr. E. P. Neufeld worked on the revision of his book on the operations of the Bank of Canada.

Professor John Porter (on leave from Carleton College) continued to work on studies of Canadian *élites*, particularly higher civil servants and trade union leaders.

Mr. S. G. D. Smith continued research on political parties in Western Canada since 1917.

Professor S. Stykolt completed a monograph on "Economic Analysis and Combines Policy," and began research on pricing policy and the Canadian tariff. He has also begun a study of pricing problems in the Polish economy.

Professor M. G. Taylor continued his studies of health insurance and of public administration in Canada.

Professor S. G. Triantis continued his study of problems of economic development with particular reference to saving and capital formation. He also undertook research in the expansion and changes in composition of the foreign trade of the U.S.S.R.

Professor E. M. Wallace continued her study of late nineteenth-century British Liberalism.

Mr. David Winch worked on problems of highway economics and finance.

---

Mr. Leo Zakuta completed his study of changes in C.C.F. ideology, structure, and morale.

## ZOOLOGY

*Reported by Professor K. C. Fisher*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

ATWOOD, C. E. (1) Studies on the taxonomy and behaviour of the family Diprionidae; (2) a study of the sense organs of mosquitoes (with C. C. Steward); (3) a study of the comparative ecology of the two closely related sawflies *Pikonema alaskensis* Roh. and *P. dimmockii* Cresson (Hymenoptera, Tenthredinidae) (with P. J. Pointing, Ph.D., 1957); (4) comparative morphology of the eggs of the lepidopterous family Geometridae (with P. Syme).

BECKEL, W. E. (1) Insect endocrinology: (a) structure and function of oenocytes in Lepidoptera; (b) effect of juvenile hormone on the micro-anatomy of pupal-

---

adult transformation in Lepidoptera; (2) bionomics of the mosquitoes of Algonquin Park.

BUTLER, L. (1) The linkage map for the tomato chromosomes; (2) the four-, six- and ten-year population cycles of Canadian fur animals.

CLARK, G. M. (1) Continued work on the genetics of mating-type determination in variety 9 of *Tetrahymena pyriformis*; (2) continuing work on dose-rate studies in *Tetrahymena pyriformis* with a chromatographic analysis of the X-irradiated organisms and the complete medium on which it grows; (3) effects of X-radiation on diploid and tetraploid buckwheat, *Fagopyrum esculentum* variety of silver hull; (4) ecological distribution studies on ciliates from the major lakes in the Province of Ontario.

CHURCHER, C. S. (1) An investigation into the process of aging in the post-cranial skeleton of the red fox; (2) the examination of the fossil canids from the Tar Pits of La Brea, Peru.

CRAIGIE, E. H. (1) Continued investigation of vascularity of visual centres in the brain and of morphological features related to hibernation; (2) consideration of distinctive osteological characters of certain mammals.

ENGELBERT, V. E. (1) Behaviour and activity of nuclear components in glandular tissue; (2) activity of the nucleus in red blood cells of vertebrates.

FALLS, J. B. (1) A study of small mammal populations in several forest types in Algonquin Park, Ontario; (2) bird song in relation to environmental factors and territorial behaviour.

FISHER, K. C. (1) Quantitative analysis of response of animals to light temperature and chemical stimuli; (2) the effects of insulin on isolated tissue.

FRY, F. E. J. (1) Estimates of mortality in lake trout populations; (2) relation of size of fish to susceptibility to capture by gill nets; (3) co-operation in a study of char hybrids.

IDE, F. P. (1) Ecology of aquatic insects of stream; (2) taxonomic studies of Ephemeroptera; (3) studies of pollution of streams.

LANGFORD, R. R. (1) Productivity in natural waters in relation to nutrient methods; (2) development of improved plankton-sampling devices; (3) development of an activity meter for fish.

MCCALLION, D. J. (1) Origin and establishment of organization patterns in the nervous system and its substrate in the chick embryo; (2) heterogeneous induction of neural tissues by adult mammalian organs in the chick embryo; (3) inhibition of differentiation in embryonic organs by differentiated adult organs in chick and frog embryos.

NEAL, G. M. (1) Cataloguing and filing rotifera records and separates; (2) identification of rotifers in plankton samples.

RIGLER, F. S. The uptake and release of phosphorus by *Daphnia magna*.

SULLIVAN, C. M. (1) Continued investigation of physiological mechanisms of responses of fish to temperature; (2) analysis of responses of selected invertebrate animals to light.

TALLAN, I. (1) The application of infection technique to the study of the nature of genetic particles (kappa) found in the cytoplasm of *Paramecium aurelia*; (2) further investigation of the nature and mode of action of a co-factor demonstrated to be required for successful infection by kappa particles; (3) the study of the inheritance pattern of surface antigens in *Tetrahymena pyriformis*, variety 9.

WALKER, Norma Ford. (1) Continued research in the general field of mongolism: (2) in co-operation with Miss Elizabeth Doidge, records were assembled of negro mongoloid imbeciles (108) in institutions for the mentally retarded in the United States, this study being part of a larger comparative study of mongoloid imbeciles of various racial groups; (3) studies of the inheritance of nephrogenic diabetes insipidus have been extended with special emphasis on the association of this disease with mental retardation; (4) a second large pedigree involving the inherited type of foetal digital amputations is being investigated.

## Faculty of Medicine

### ANAESTHESIA

*Reported by Professor S. M. Campbell*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Professor S. M. Campbell has completed a clinical study of the analgesic and hypnotic effects of dipipanone hydrochloride.

Dr. H. Barrie Fairley, with the assistance of a grant from the J. P. Bickell Foundation, has carried out a study of pump oxygenators and complications arising from their use in open heart surgery on animals in developing a technique of anaesthesia which is now in use clinically for the Department of Cardiovascular Surgery under Dr. W. G. Bigelow. It is expected that further investigations of pulmonary function and metabolic changes during anaesthesia for cardiac surgery will be carried out on patients in the coming year.

Dr. Iain MacKay, working with Professor Werner Kalow in the Department of Pharmacology, has been evaluating the various vaporizers manufactured for the use of "Fluothane," both in the laboratory and in the operating room.

Dr. S. L. Vandewater, with Dr. W. M. Lougheed, has continued investigations on animals under a National Health Grant on hypothermia and hypotension as related to subarachnoid haemorrhage. Also, in collaboration with Dr. W. M. Paul of the Department of Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Dr. Vandewater has studied the effects of hypothermia on pregnant animals. Dr. Vandewater is carrying out a study of one thousand consecutive unselected operative patients for incidence, cause and treatment of hypotension during surgery and in the early postoperative recovery period.

Dr. R. A. Gordon and Dr. Brian Marshall have been studying the incidence of regurgitation and aspiration during anaesthesia and methods of treatment of the aspiration syndrome.

### ANATOMY

*Reported by Professor J. W. A. Duckworth*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Dr. J. E. Anderson has: (1) continued his studies on the physical anthropology of the Iroquois Indians by analysis of the data from the skeletons of Faherty site

---

ossuary; (2) carried out studies on ancient and modern vertebrae, noted the relation between cervical arthritis and occlusion of the foramen for vertebral artery in the cervical transverse processes; (3) organized a comparative osteology collection of mammals with a scheme for their identification, which should prove valuable both to students of comparative anatomy and also in medico-legal work.

Continuing work on the phenomenon of cell alteration in tissue culture, Dr. A. A. Axelrad has shown that the cells of an altered line in continuous culture, when injected into newborn mice belonging to the strain of origin of these cells, grew as malignant tumours, leading to the early death of nearly all the mice injected. The same cells rarely gave rise to tumours in adults of the same strain, showing that an immune mechanism in the host, developing after the first day of life, is capable of preventing the growth of these malignant cells.

Recently Dr. Axelrad has developed a technique for studying the chromosomes of leukaemic cells taken directly from the animal and has found that in several "spontaneous" and several virus-induced leukaemias in mice the number of chromosomes is abnormal and this number may vary from cell to cell. This suggests that an interference with the genetic mechanism of the cell may be an important feature of this disease.

Dr. S. H. Bensley continued her studies on the fibrous connective tissues, with the aid of a grant from the Banting Research Foundation, along three lines. (1) Physical and chemical changes in the intercellular substances. It was found that after application of histamine and hyaluronidase in the mouse and in alloxan diabetes in the rat there was an hydrolysis of the ground substance, fraying of the fibres, and a consequent loss of hydration of the intercellular substances. (2) Relation of mast cells to changes in the intercellular substances. It was observed that mast cells developed *in situ* following changes in the intercellular substances described above. These cells developed by incorporating into the cytoplasm in the form of granules the modified substances in their environment. (3) The effects of an alkaloid, extracted from *taxus canadensis*, on the peripheral progressive oedema produced in mice by histamine plus hyaluronidase. It was found that this alkaloid, "Taxenin," reversed and prevented many of the local and systemic effects of histamine and hyaluronidase.

In collaboration with Dr. Madeline Maykut of Pharmacology, studies were carried out on the microscopic effects of long-acting local anaesthetics on the sensory nerve fibres of cutaneous nerves of guinea pigs. This study revealed that many of the sensory fibres were still damaged after more than an hour after the return of sensation in the skin, and that, in general, the regeneration of damaged sensory fibres was more rapid and complete with the less toxic anaesthetics.

Dr. W. G. Bruce Casselman and Dr. Keith F. A. Ross developed techniques for studying living muscle cells by interference microscopy, using either the A.O.-Baker or the Cooke-Dyson instrument. An improved method was also devised for the preparation of isotonic media of various refractive indices for use in these studies. Investigations are in progress comparing the muscles of genetically dystrophic mice and their normal litter-mates. The results indicate definite differences in the concentrations of solids in the A- and I-bands of the two groups. Dr. Casselman completed his studies of hepatoma and ascitic tumour cells by interference microscopy and histochemical techniques. Dr. Ross, with the co-operation of Dr. E. A. McCulloch of the Department of Medicine, continued studies which he commenced in England on microscopical refractometry of erythrocytes as a clinical diagnostic technique.

Dr. J. W. A. Duckworth has continued his investigations with the post-natal development of the sinu-atrial and atrioventricular nodes in man, and has also been studying the pathways of the conducting tissue bundles in the various types of congenital heart defect.

Dr. A. F. Howatson has continued his electron microscope studies of tumour cells with particular reference to virus-infected cells. Virus-like bodies have been discovered in a transplantable mouse plasma cell tumour. Studies on the identification and properties of this presumed virus are being carried out. The fine structure of cells

---

growing *in vitro* under various conditions and changes in cell morphology occurring after exposure to large doses of X-ray and after infection with certain viruses have been investigated.

Dr. T. S. Leeson, who was awarded a British Medical Association Research Scholarship for 1957-8, has been engaged on the study of the fine structure of the mesonephros and metanephros of the rabbit.
