Some observations made during toxicity studies on tranquilizing phenothiazin derivatives have been followed by systematic investigations in collaboration with Dr. I. C. Radde. Using radioactive iodine, a suppressing influence of Nozinan, one of these derivatives, on thyroid function in rats was found. Clinically effective vapour concentrations of the general anaesthetic, Fluothane, have been measured in collaboration with Dr. I. MacKay of the Department of Anaesthesia.

Graduate students have completed the following theses:

*For the Ph.D. degree:*

ASTON, R. The action of some depressant drugs on the central nervous system.

DAVIES, R. O. Studies on usual and atypical pseudocholinesterases in the serum of man.

MCCURDY, D. H. A study of the cholinergic receptors in the guinea pig ileum and frog's rectus abdominis muscle.

## PHYSIOLOGY

*Reported by Professor Charles H. Best*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

In Professor R. E. Haist's section, Dr. M. A. Ashworth has investigated the effect of the continuous intravenous infusion of fructose on the islets of Langerhans and,

---

with Dr. Rosemary D. Hawkins, the influence of this procedure on the glucose-6-phosphatase activity of liver and kidney. With Dr. Hawkins he has further studied the effects of continuous glucose infusion on the level of activity of this enzyme in animals pretreated intensively with insulin. Dr. Hawkins and Mr. H. Schachter found that the continuous infusion of glucose into alloxan-diabetic rats led to no significant change in the glucose-6-phosphatase activity of liver, whereas in animals not receiving alloxan a definite depression in activity was obtained. With Dr. E. Schönbaum and Mr. Schachter, Dr. Hawkins has investigated the effects of glucose infusion, ACTH infusion, and shock secondary to limb clamping on the glucose-6-phosphatase activity of liver and the steroid output of the adrenal gland, and has done preliminary work on the effect of insulin on the glucose uptake by adrenal slices. With Miss G. V. Kovacs she is observing the action of continuous infusion of sodium fluoroacetate on the islets of Langerhans, and diabetes production. Dr. Hawkins, Dr. Ashworth, Mrs. R. Moscarello and Mr. T. Friedlich are investigating the influence of environment on the metabolic changes following injury. Mr. R. Kajioka, Mrs. Moscarello and Mrs. C. J. Baines have followed the effects of potassium infusion in shocked and non-shocked rats. Mr. S. Wong is studying the effects of extracts of certain native plants on experimental diabetes.

Professor J. Markowitz, in collaboration with members of the staff of the Ontario Veterinary College, has advanced several problems in experimental surgery.

In Professor James Campbell's section research on diabetes was continued. Dr. P. Riet Correa, Professor of Physiology and Director of the Institute of Experimental Physiology of the University of Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil, and the recipient of a travelling fellowship from the Rockefeller Foundation, has made a study of the insulin activities of the blood of normal, prediabetic, and diabetic animals. With Dr. G. A. Wrenshall, and ably assisted by Mrs. M. A. Riet Correa, he has studied the formation of insulin by slices of the pancreas *in vitro*, and the passage of insulin from the pancreatic tissue into the medium. Dr. Campbell, Dr. G. F. Wilgram, and Dr. Jean M. Patterson, with the co-operation of Dr. Lena Lewis of the Cleveland Clinic Foundation, have compared the modes of transport of fats in the blood during absorption of fat from the intestine, with mobilization of fat from body depots to the sites of utilization. Dr. Kunigunda Zymantiene has studied the effects of intra-arterial infusion of serum from diabetic dogs on the secretion of insulin, the effects of lymph duct cannulation on diabetes, and the flow of bile in prediabetic dogs. Mr. H. Socol found that in the liver tissue of diabetic animals the amount of co-enzyme A and the activity of the condensing enzyme are reduced. Mr. G. R. Green and Mr. Socol found that an increase in the co-enzyme A of the liver tissue is a response of rats exposed to low environmental temperature. Mr. Green is studying the factors that influence the production of citrate by tissues.

In Professor A. M. Rappaport's section, Dr. J. W. Martyn continued the study of the effect of hepatic or systemic arterial autoperfusion on dogs subjected to irreversible haemorrhagic shock. There was an increase in central venous pressure, in carotid flow, a decrease in hepatic arteriovenous oxygen difference, and a normal myocardial function as indicated by the electrocardiogram. Toxic hepatitis was induced in rats by intraperitoneal injection of bromobenzene. The pathology was studied by *in vivo* transillumination of the liver and by routine histological methods. Rats continuously infused via the portal system with glucose and vitamins C and P showed a greater resistance to damage. The increase in their transaminase level was significantly lower when compared with similarly intoxicated controls perfused with saline only. Applying the acinar concept to the study of the histopathology of the liver, Dr. Rappaport has prepared a critical review of the pertinent literature.

In Professor F. C. Monkhouse's section, Dr. Monkhouse has continued studies on plasma antithrombin. Antithrombin levels in rats and rabbits were decreased following the intravenous injection of thrombin, and the decrease was proportional to the amount of thrombin injected. The injection of thromboplastin did not give consistent results. With Dr. O. Sirek and Dr. Anna Sirek, plasma prothrombin levels have been measured in dogs receiving oral hypoglycaemic compounds. Mrs. Ruth

---

MacKneson has studied the production of clearing factor under a variety of conditions and in several animal species. With Dr. R. L. MacMillan, Department of Medicine, studies are being carried out on normal and atherosclerotic subjects. Miss Gunta Bammers has studied the relationship of histamine and heparin to mast cells. This work has been carried out in co-operation with Dr. W. G. B. Casselman.

In Professor D. W. Clarke's section, Dr. N. F. Forbath has been studying the effects of various enzyme poisons upon pentose transport in the isolated rat diaphragm. This work is being done in connection with a study of the actions of an oral hypoglycaemic agent, DBI. Experiments with a similar agent, carbutamide, have shown that under certain circumstances it can cause an increase in the amount of glycogen in the diaphragm. This is consistent with the hypothesis that the agent acts by stimulating insulin release by the pancreas. Mr. R. L. Evans has completed a series of experiments on the relationship between ethyl alcohol metabolism and carbohydrate metabolism, as studied in excised portions of liver, diaphragm and brain in alcohol-fed and control animals. Mr. J. E. Harris has been working on a comparison of the amounts of extractable glucagon from pancreases of diabetic and of non-diabetic patients. Preliminary studies suggest that in the former there is a reduced amount of extractable glucagon.

In Professor J. W. Scott's section, Mr. J. Blumenstein has continued his study of the histochemistry of the muscular dystrophy produced by feeding rabbits a choline-free diet. Mr. V. H. Chrom assayed the acetylcholine in the choline-deficient animals, and Mrs. J. M. Shephard undertook some electroencephalographic studies of the animals on the choline-free regimen. Mr. R. G. Black has continued the development and testing of equipment for determining strength-duration curves of muscles. Dr. H. J. Hoffman recorded simultaneous pressure changes in the urinary bladder and renal pelvis of normal and paraplegic dogs. Dr. S. Yamada and Dr. S. L. Vandewater (Department of Anaesthesia) undertook a study of hypotensive drugs at lowered body temperatures. The electroencephalographic study of dogs during hepatic coma, with Dr. Rappaport, was completed. In conjunction with Fl./Lt. H. P. Brent, R.C.A.F., a study was made of the cerebral effects of reduced blood sugar, hyperventilation, and positive acceleration.

In Professor O. Sirek's section, Dr. Sirek has continued the investigation of the cause of oedema observed in offspring of alloxan-diabetic rabbits. A normal serum electrolyte concentration having been found in litters of diabetic mothers, attention has now been focused on serum proteins and glycoproteins. In collaboration with Dr. Anna Sirek of the Banting and Best Department of Medical Research, a series of liver function tests in sera of dogs receiving tolbutamide has been performed.

Graduate students have completed the following theses:

*For the M.A. degree:*

BAMBERS, Miss G. The relationship of heparin to histamine in tissues under normal and experimental conditions.

BARROW, Mrs. P. C. Environmental and hormonal influences on the development of vascular lesions in the rat and rabbit.

KAJIOKA, R. Studies related to experimental shock concerned especially with elevations in blood potassium levels and carbohydrate changes.

*For the Ph.D. degree:*

ASHWORTH, M. A. Dietary and other factors influencing the islets of Langerhans.

RADDE, Miss I. C. Studies on thyroid function and on thyroid hormone metabolism.

## PSYCHIATRY

*Reported by Professor A. B. Stokes*

The research efforts of the Department of Psychiatry continue in the genetic, physical, psychological, and social areas. While in each area there are pointed ques-

---

tionings in respect to delimited problems, there is also a continuous review of the meaning of the particular findings to the general concern for a fuller, more penetrating understanding of mental disorder. A Committee on Research has been established under Dr. Lovett Doust to fulfil this integrative review function.

Dr. D. A. Sprott, biometrician in the Department, has developed the application of multivariant analysis to psychiatric problems. Analysis of the results of physiological observations on psychiatric patients has hitherto been confined to two reference terms. This procedure has been unsatisfactory because it assumes arbitrarily the absence of concomitant variables (and hence ignores the implications of homeostasis) and, at the same time, does less than justice to the data available. Now it has been shown that the newer methods of multivariant analysis yield more meaningful information in the ongoing kinetic researches. In this very important work Dr. Sprott has been associated with Dr. D. Naidoo, a visiting research fellow from London, England.

Dr. M. Sloane has progressed in his anatomical studies of the archipallium. This work has reference to an uncovering of feeling tone relationships in the cerebral integrate; it has been temporarily slowed down by defects in laboratory facilities.

Current interest in 5-hydroxytryptamine (Serotonin) in mediating neural transmission and in promoting metabolic exchange in nerve cells has stimulated Dr. H. Husdan to investigate the possible ways in which the function of this metabolite may be deranged in patients with mental illness. This study is being undertaken in the research laboratories of the Ontario Hospital, Toronto. Dr. J. B. Fotheringham is pursuing a parallel study involving the new psychopharmaceuticals under the direction of Professor Cullumbine in the Department of Pharmacology.

The kinetic metabolic investigation of patients with the psychoses of myxoedema and with unremitting catatonic stupor has been done with the object of ascertaining the possible hormonal and biochemical relationships between these conditions and periodic catatonia. The effect of progesterone in circular insanity (alternating hypomania depression) has been studied. The effect of tri-iodo-thyronine in patients with certain types of hallucinosis has been investigated. Patients with menstrual psychoses are being studied. In all these biochemical and hormonal investigations, collaboration with Dr. A. G. Gornall and the Department of Pathological Chemistry has been crucial.

Physiological studies under Dr. Lovett Doust have continued fruitfully. The autonomic responses of representative groups of psychiatric patients are being investigated by Dr. Charles Gregory in the research laboratories of the Ontario Hospital, Toronto (Dr. J. N. Hagan, Medical Superintendent, and Dr. A. Miller, Clinical Director). The relationship of perceiving to emotional upset and the disturbance of the relationship in different psychiatric illnesses are the ultimate objective of these researches. The influence of affect on homeostasis as revealed by autonomic oscillation is under review.

The part played by capillary anoxaemia in influencing the metabolism of the tissue cells of the skin continues to be of great interest. Water partition studies, varying osmosis, and plethysmographic observations of the changing hydration and dehydration of cells are continuing. The studies are concerned with man's relationship to changing environmental conditions and to his subjective feeling tone. Correlations between the findings and simultaneous molecular oxygen availability and capillary blood lactate are being sought.

The role of erythrocytic aggregation (sludging) in the capillary circulatory disturbances seen in psychiatric patients is being investigated in association with Dr. S. Bensley of the Department of Anatomy.

The electroencephalographic studies of patients with periodic catatonia, under Dr. A. Bonkalo, are continuing. Physiological tests of outcome, when electroconvulsive therapy is prescribed, are continuing under Dr. A. L. Jones.

Psychological researches continue. In particular, Dr. S. Neiger is comparing the Rorschach protocols of certain groups of psychiatric patients with those of normal subjects in various occupations. This work aims to make the Rorschach test a better

---

instrument in both normal and abnormal psychology by (a) extending its application to the relatively unexplored field of the personality psychology of occupations, and (b) increasing its discrimination value between psychiatric diagnostic groups as compared with each other and with normal subjects. Dr. B. Quarrington and Mr. Owen White have completed their study of substitutive learning in alcoholics, neurotics, and psychotics. Dr. Quarrington and Mr. E. Douglass continue their enquiry into disturbances of speech development.

Problems of psychodynamics and psychopathology are being investigated. Dr. D. Cappon, in association with Dr. C. Ezrin of the Department of Medicine, has looked at the psychological difficulties associated with hermaphrodites. With Dr. D. Coates, Dr. Cappon has studied the body image of the blind with the connotation of the effects of sensory deprivation on personality integration. Dr. A. Parkin has investigated the emergence of schizophrenic reactions from neurotic reactions over short periods of time.

Social areas of research are represented by Dr. T. Mallinson's continuing investigation of small group interplays, Mr. F. Toombs's enquiries into communication systems, and Mr. J. Seeley's studies with the Ontario Alcohol Foundation.

Clinical researches have been very active. Dr. D. Lewis (St. Michael's Hospital) has used lysergic acid Diethylamide as an aid to psychotherapy in the general hospital practice of psychiatry. With Dr. Bonkalo he has studied the clinical value of a new phenothiazine type of tranquilizing drug. Dr. D. J. McCulloch, with his associates at the Out-Patient Clinic of the Ontario Hospital, Toronto, has instituted a large-scale research into the effects of psychotherapy. Changes occurring in patients receiving psychotherapy will be related to a large number of variables derived from a life history questionnaire and from psychological test protocols. Dr. W. Mitchell has been appraising the value of progesterone in the puerperal psychoses. Dr. J. D. Armstrong has been testing new treatment techniques in the field of alcoholism and Dr. W. B. Spaulding has studied the onset of the dementing processes. A number of active enquiries have been initiated in the forensic clinic: (a) to isolate and identify those personality traits which distinguish certain classes of sex offenders, neurotics, and psychotics from other forensic patients; (b) to develop test indices for assessing the degree and type of personality disorder in forensic cases; and (c) to provide material for theory development in this difficult area of clinical practice. Dr. H. C. Hutchison and his associates are pioneers in a forgotten field of human disability.

As may be seen the over-all picture is one of creditable activity in first-hand experiencing and testing. What may be proven as true remains for future judgment.

## RADIOLOGY

*Reported by Professor A. C. Singleton*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Dr. J. D. Munn is investigating the cause and incidence of pneumoperitoneum in the newborn. He and Dr. C. A. F. Moes are conducting research into the correlation of clinical, radiological, and surgical findings in pyloric stenosis and neurenteric cysts. Dr. Munn anticipates that this year will conclude the study in the incidence of pneumothorax in hyaline membrane disease in infants.

Dr. K. E. Hodge, with Dr. G. A. Lawson and Dr. C. M. Godfrey of the Department of Physical Medicine, Sunnybrook Hospital, is conducting investigations in spinal traction for the treatment of painful back and neck conditions. Dr. Hodge is working on techniques for the improved demonstration of the small bowel by orally administered air and non-flocculating barium.

Dr. M. R. Hall, in conjunction with Dr. E. R. Yendt and Dr. W. K. Kerr, is making a study of renal hypertension.

Dr. D. E. Sanders, with Dr. N. Delarue of the Department of Surgery, is investigating the use of mediastinal and pulmonary angiographic findings in bronchogenic

---

carcinoma in assessing resectability. With Dr. J. M. Low, of the Department of Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Dr. Sanders is continuing research on stress incontinence.

Dr. L. R. Harnick and Dr. B. C. Prior have been conducting research in an attempt to assess relative bone density in radiographs in the study of osteoporosis.

## SURGERY

*Reported by Professor F. G. Kergin*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

### CARDIOVASCULAR SURGERY

Dr. W. G. Bigelow, with the assistance of Dr. A. S. Trimble who has been supported by a Defence Research Board fellowship, has continued the study of hibernation, and in the past year has been successful in extracting a substance from the hibernating gland of the ground hog which, when administered to ordinary laboratory animals, greatly increases their tolerance for cooling. In this work they have had the assistance of Dr. A. G. Gornall, Dr. E. Schönbaum, Mrs. Grajman, and Mrs. C. Mookerjea. Under Dr. Bigelow's direction Dr. R. H. Wensel, in co-operation with Dr. R. L. MacMillan of the Department of Medicine, has studied the problem of bleeding during hypothermia. The administration of heparin during the cooling phase has proved effective in preventing this complication.

Dr. R. O. Heimbecker, with the assistance of Dr. P. J. F. Kuyper, who has been supported by a National Research Council grant, has studied a number of problems related to cardiovascular surgery. With the help of Dr. H. B. Fairley, of the Department of Anaesthesia, they have tested and modified in the laboratory various types of pump-oxygenator and have adapted them for clinical use. An electronic device has been developed which automatically adjusts pumping rates in relationship to the patient's venous pressure. Dr. Barrie of the Department of Pathology has evolved a technique for demonstrating minimal air embolism and control studies have been done with experimental air embolism for comparison with the results of perfusion by bubble-oxygenators. Experiments with transplanted cardiac valves are being continued.

Dr. J. A. Key, with the assistance of Dr. R. H. Wensel, has continued a long-term study of the fate of a variety of types of arterial prostheses and this study is now almost complete. Attempts have been made, in the experimental animal, to produce revascularization of the myocardium by tapping the left ventricle, to date without success. In collaboration with Dr. S. D. Gordon and Dr. E. M. Tanz of the Plastic Surgery Service, sections of the aorta have been replaced experimentally by dermis grafts.

Dr. W. T. Mustard with Dr. W. Sapirstein has evolved a method for carrying out cardiac by-pass using an experimental animal's own lungs as the oxygenator instead of an artificial oxygenator and this work was reported at the Forum Section of the meeting of the American Association for Thoracic Surgery in April, 1958. With Dr. T. A. Wright, Dr. Mustard has studied the effects of extracorporeal circulation on blood components and has demonstrated that the antihaemophilic globulin is depleted directly in proportion to the length of time that an artificial heart lung machine is used. They have also demonstrated the presence of a precursor to prothrombin.

### GENERAL SURGERY

Dr. D. J. Currie is investigating the factors which can cause acute cholecystitis in the experimental animal.

Dr. N. C. Delarue has completed a final evaluation of the results in a large series of patients treated by adrenalectomy for advanced mammary cancer and has a report in preparation. Under his direction Dr. D. C. Steele has carried on the continuing study of the cases of bronchogenic carcinoma admitted to the Toronto General

---

Hospital by surveying the records for the past five years. Under a grant from the Ontario Cancer Treatment and Research Foundation, and with the help of Dr. D. E. Sanders of the Department of Radiology, Drs. Delarue and Steele have investigated the place of pulmonary angiography in assessing the extent of bronchogenic carcinoma and its operability; they have also, in co-operation with Dr. W. S. Anderson of the Department of Pathology, initiated a study of cancer cells in the blood under various conditions.

Dr. G. H. C. Joynt continues his efforts to cause pulmonary emphysema in the experimental animal by implanting a one-way valve in a bronchus.

Dr. L. J. Mahoney is attempting to prepare an antigen from cancers of the stomach which may influence the course of this disease in human patients.

Dr. J. D. Mills is continuing the follow-up study of over nine hundred patients who have been treated by partial gastrectomy for a variety of conditions at Sunnybrook Hospital.

Dr. H. F. Robertson continues his long-term follow-up of patients with peripheral vascular disease and is investigating these patients by various methods including plethysmography.

Dr. W. J. E. Spence, in association with Dr. J. M. Finlay of the Department of Medicine, using radioactive fat has studied fat digestion and absorption in a variety of gastro-intestinal diseases and after various kinds of gastric resection.

Dr. N. A. Watters is co-operating with Dr. T. C. Brown of the Department of Pathology of the Ontario Cancer Institute in a study of circulating cancer cells at various stages of treatment of the primary lesion.

#### NEUROSURGERY

Dr. W. J. Horsey has continued, with Dr. C. Ezrin of the Department of Medicine, an experimental investigation of hypothalamo-hypophyseal relationships and they have demonstrated the presence of a mechanism in the hypothalamus which inhibits the secretion of the thyrotropic hormone.

Dr. W. M. Lougheed has developed a technique for producing in the experimental animal a subarachnoid haemorrhage without damage to brain or cerebral blood vessels, and is proceeding to a study of the effects of this type of lesion.

Dr. T. P. Morley is continuing and extending his studies of cerebral tumours grown in tissue culture.

Dr. J. W. Scott has directed Dr. H. H. Hoffman in experiments designed to elucidate the cause of hydro-ureter in paraplegic patients. They have made simultaneous pressure recordings in the urinary bladder and renal pelvis in both normal and paraplegic dogs and have studied urinary secretion against elevated ureteral pressure. In association with Dr. S. L. Vandewater of the Department of Anaesthesia, Dr. W. M. Lougheed and Dr. S. Yamada, the inter-relationship of hypothermia and hypotensive drugs in modifying blood pressure has been studied.

#### ORTHOPAEDIC SURGERY

Dr. F. P. Dewar has continued his interest in fractures of the os calcis and with the Wallace and Mary Duncan Fellow, Dr. G. D. Kay, has made an experimental study of the forces required to fracture the human os calcis.

With the assistance of Dr. I. A. Todd, Dr. W. R. Harris has continued his studies of experimental fat embolism using quartz rod microscopy and is developing a method of estimating fat levels in the blood by paper chromatography. They have also investigated the nutrition of epiphyseal plates in an effort to develop a method of increasing limb growth.

Dr. D. L. MacIntosh has continued a clinical study of patients treated by hemiarthroplasty of the knee, using a plastic prosthesis.

Dr. Ian Macnab has studied 250 patients who have had fractures of the tibia with a view to relating rate of healing of the fractures to observations made on the experimental animal on the importance of blood supply in fracture healing. He is

---

also investigating the factors which affect the bridging of a bone defect with special reference to the effects of infection and the lytic action of pus on bone chips.

Dr. G. F. Pennal is investigating experimental fractures of the radius and ulna as well as completing a clinical study of fracture-dislocation of the pelvis and central dislocation of the hip.

Dr. R. B. Salter is conducting a clinical study of fractures about the elbow in children.

Dr. E. H. Simmons, collaborating with Dr. J. F. Murray, is investigating the experimental use of dermis grafts for arthroplasty.

#### PLASTIC SURGERY

Dr. S. D. Gordon is investigating the effect of polyethylene on the healing of tendons and the reaction of the peritendinous tissues to the plastic.

Dr. W. K. Lindsay continues his clinical studies in the end results of peripheral nerve injuries in children, tumours of the jaw, malignant soft tissue tumours in children, and factors influencing the end result of treatment of cleft palate. With Dr. H. G. Thomson he has made an extensive experimental study of factors influencing restoration of function in lesions of the digital flexor tendons.

#### UROLOGY

Dr. W. K. Kerr, assisted by Dr. A. Keresteci who has been supported by a grant from the Ontario Cancer Treatment and Research Foundation, has conducted several investigations. With Miss Helen Mayoh they have investigated the distribution of zinc in the human prostate gland. With the help of Dr. G. L. Dale of the staff of the Toronto Hospital for Tuberculosis, they have studied the blood electrolyte changes in patients treated by ileocystoplasty for tuberculous vesical contracture. Investigations are being conducted to develop a more precise method of determination of blood acid phosphatase. A method for producing local renal hypothermia during operations on the kidney has been developed with the help of Dr. C. A. Smythe.

#### THERAPEUTICS

*Reported by Professor K. J. R. Wightman*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Dr. J. R. Hilliard has carried out a study of the use of Megimide and Daptazole in severe barbiturate poisoning, as well as of the other measures of importance in this condition. He has also made a retrospective study of patients who have undergone splenectomy. Investigations of thrombocytosis and leukaemia have continued. A new antibiotic (Kanamycin) is under study. An increasing number of requests are being made by the pharmaceutical companies for clinical trials of new compounds. At times these can be fitted into existing research programmes, but so far no very well-organized method of dealing with the problem has been possible within the Department of Therapeutics.

#### COMMITTEE ON EXPERIMENTAL RESEARCH

*Chairman:* Professor J. D. Hamilton

*Supervisor:* Dr. R. C. Ritchie

During the past year the common research facilities available in the Banting Institute have again been used to the full.

No major changes have been made in the animal quarters, or in the other rooms, but it is now apparent that radical changes must be considered if the expansion of the research programme is to be permitted. The operating rooms in use on the fifth floor have proved adequate, and the associated preparation and X-ray rooms are also quite satisfactory. However, the recovery room is not satisfactory because of its poor venti-

---

lation and the fact that it is too large. Space for housing research teams on this floor is greatly in demand, and removal of the recovery room would satisfy the immediate requirements. Within five years, double the present space will be needed. In the animal quarters more space is needed for small animals and for monkeys. The sheep have left the quarters, but this only makes available one small room. The most pressing problem in the animal quarters is presented by the dogs. There are dogs in three categories, totalling approximately eighty at all times: first, those which are being prepared for operation; second, those which have been operated upon and are being retained for observation; and third, those which are being held for use in the near future by one or more of the research groups. The dogs are kept in individual cages, they are well fed, and the room is clean, but the animals should not be kept under such conditions for more than a short period of time, because no runways are available. The answer to the problem is a kennel outside the city. Preliminary explorations, however, have made it abundantly clear that a great deal of money will be required before we could maintain outside the city animals not immediately required. Efforts are continuing to try to find some way of keeping animals at a farm or a kennel because this would not only release space in our animal quarters, but also enable us to provide the research teams with animals in good condition. The cost of using animals in poor condition in research is very high, in terms of both time and poor results.

Again, I should like to pay tribute to Dr. R. C. Ritchie for the excellent way in which he has supervised the research facilities and the personnel, all of whom have continued to co-operate in assisting with the various research programmes.

### INSTITUTE OF AEROPHYSICS

*Reported by Professor G. N. Patterson*

A detailed description of the research at the Institute of Aerophysics for 1957-8 can be found in the UTIA Annual Progress Report. This document provides summaries of forty-two projects in six general branches of aerophysics research. These branches with their supervisors are: (1) mechanics of rarefied gases (G. N. Patterson); (2) unsteady flow-shock tubes (I. I. Glass, J. G. Hall); (3) steady flow-wind tunnels (B. Etkin, H. S. Ribner, G. K. Korbacher); (4) aerodynamic noise (B. Etkin, H. S. Ribner); (5) aerodynamics of propulsion (G. K. Korbacher, H. S. Ribner); and (6) airplane dynamics and aeroelasticity (B. Etkin, E. D. Poppleton). The research programme was supported financially by the Defence Research Board, United States Air Force Office of Scientific Research, United States Office of Naval Research, Avro Aircraft Ltd., Orenda Engines Ltd., and deHavilland Aircraft of Canada Ltd.

In addition to the above supervisory work, members of the staff have been engaged in the following research work:

ETKIN, B. Stability and control of airplanes and missiles. Response of airplanes to random atmospheric turbulence. Intensity of Aeolian tones. Air induction in a jet muffler.

GLASS, I. I. Hypersonic shock-tunnel drive. Spherical shock-wave studies. General survey of the theory and performance of shock tubes. Gasmagnetically driven

---

shock tubes and determination of temperature behind shock waves in air, oxygen, and nitrogen (while on leave of absence in England).

HALL, J. G. General survey of the design, instrumentation, and application of shock tubes. Collision of shock waves with wire-mesh screens.

KORBACHER, G. K. Study of the theory and application of the jet flap. Performance of perforated supersonic intakes. Gas turbine blade design and performance prediction.

PATTERSON, G. N. Molecular approach to problems of high-altitude, high-speed flight. Theory of free-molecule, orifice-type pressure probes. Theory of the long-tube pressure probe in free-molecule flow. Aerodynamics of flight at extreme altitudes. Application of the electron gun to low density flows.

POPPLETON, E. D. Pressure distribution on finite wings oscillating in incompressible flow. Effect of edge separations on low aspect ratio wings.

RIBNER, H. S. Skin stresses and fatigue associated with boundary-layer noise. Sound generated by the interaction of a single vortex with a shock wave. Theory of wings in slipstreams.

Research assistants and their projects are listed as follows:

BOYER, A. G. Design of a shock tube for the study of dissociated hypersonic flows.

BOYER, D. W. Spherical explosions and implosions.

BREMNER, G. F. Design and calibration of a hypersonic shock tube.

CLARK, J. Study of pressure recovery in a two-dimensional, perforated wall intake for supersonic flow.

DATAR, S. G. Measurement of flow quantities in spherical shock waves.

FRENCH, J. B. Design and development of a tunnel for the study of magnetogas-dynamics.

FRIEND, W. H. The interaction of a plane shock wave with an inclined perforated plate.

GADAMER, E. O. Development of an electron gun as a density probe in supersonic flows at large Knudsen numbers.

GORDON, S. A. Study of equilibrium temperature probes for use with shock waves at high Mach number.

HARRIS, E. L. Pressure measurements in the neighbourhood of a flat plate in a low density wind tunnel.

KEEFE, R. T. Measurement of the oscillating forces on a circular cylinder in an airstream.

DELEEUW, J. H. The interaction of a plane strong shock wave with a steady magnetic field.

LUDWIG, G. R. Sound radiated by a flexible wall adjacent to a turbulent boundary layer.

MADILL, R. Damping in pitch of two-dimensional wings with leading edge separations.

MAESTRELLO, L. Development of a quiet air duct for flow noise research.

MARTIN, W. A. An experimental study of the boundary layer behind a moving plane shock wave.

MUNTZ, E. P. Design and development of a wind tunnel for the study of aerodynamics at very high Mach numbers and extreme altitudes.

PRENDERGAST, V. Measurement of two-point pressure correlations on a circular cylinder.

RAM, G. S. Pressure distribution on finite wings oscillating in incompressible flow.

SREEKANTH, A. K. Interference drag and lift in free-molecule flow.

SRIDHAR, K. Jet flap performance in supersonic flow.

TAYLOR, B. W. Design and calibration of a thin film heat-transfer gauge for high temperature flows.

WALDRON, H. F. An experimental investigation of the flow properties behind strong shock waves in nitrogen.

WILSON, L. N. Sound radiated from a turbulent boundary layer.

## Faculty of Household Science

### FOOD CHEMISTRY

*Reported by Professor M. Doreen Smith*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Professor J. R. Butterfield has continued studies on the inhibitory action of aromatic acids and related compounds on the respiration of common food moulds.

Mr. Bruce Brown, under the direction of Professor Butterfield and Dr. E. M. Meade, is engaged in further studies of the chemical and physical changes in selected varieties of soybeans during germination and storage.

Carbohydrate studies have continued under Professor M. D. Smith with Dr. W. E. Parker and Dr. J. J. Kagan. These have included improvements in specificity of sugar analyses. One of the investigations has been on the sugars in soybeans which have been separated by paper chromatography. Experiments are in progress on the identification of a naturally occurring pentasaccharide found in soybean extracts. To this end, enzymic hydrolysis is being carried out on the paper chromatograms and the end products isolated for analyses by a right angle chromatographic development from the original chromatogram. Also, aging phenomena in starch and starch fractions are being examined by several approaches including factorial experiments to take care of the many variables which may be involved.

Dr. J. J. Kagan has continued with a study on the estimation of riboflavin in cereal products as a member of the committee on vitamins and minerals in cereal products of the American Association of Cereal Chemists.

Dr. E. M. Meade is continuing work on fatty acid separation by crystallization of soaps, together with the use of keto fatty acids as synthetic intermediates.

Graduate students have completed the following theses:

*For the M.A. degree:*

CZAJA, Miss C. A. A study of vanillic acid and propyl vanillate as mold inhibitors.

EISENBERG, A. The inhibition of *Aspergillus niger* by vanillates.

ELLIOTT, C. G. A study of the fluoride content of Newfoundland teeth related to fluoride-containing foods of the diet.

MORRISON, J. A. The application of a microbiological technique to the measurement of glucose in blood.

*For the Ph.D. degree:*

KAGAN, J. J. Studies on the acid hydrolysis of selected starches and starch products.

### HOUSEHOLD SCIENCE

*Reported by Professor Barbara A. McLaren*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Professor J. M. Wardlaw and Professor B. A. McLaren are co-operating with Dr. Dorothy Daley of the Women's College Hospital and Dr. M. F. Grapko of the Institute of Child Study in a long-term project with overweight women.

Professor E. W. Park and Professor McLaren are continuing the study of senior citizens in co-operation with Miss Margaret Wilkie, Chairman of the Nutrition Committee of the Ontario Dietetic Association.

Professor McLaren is co-operating with Miss Gladys Martin, Director of Food Services, Dr. Elizabeth Chant Robertson, and Dr. Andrew Sass-Kortsak of the Hospital for Sick Children in the development of suitable food products to be used for balance studies in coeliac disease.

Graduate students have completed the following theses:

*For the M.A. degree:*

FALBY, C. A study of methods of measuring enzymatic degradation in wool fibres.

LYNN, Y. The development of a stable milk emulsion.

## Ontario College of Education

*Reported by Professor R. W. B. Jackson*

The year under review witnessed a steady expansion of the activities of the Department of Educational Research. Many studies have been completed, substantial progress has been made on others, and new and significant studies—some of major, others of minor importance—have been undertaken. In part, this development reflects a much greater and more widespread interest in research and in the fruits of research, which for many people arose as a direct result of the advent of the Sputniks and Explorers orbiting in space. In the main, however, the increase in interest and activity in educational research reflects a growing awareness of the urgent need to evaluate our educational efforts and to secure through study and experimentation the basic information needed to enable us to improve our educational system and the education of our youth. There is a growing recognition that evaluation and research are essential in education. The major danger, now, is that too much may be expected of research; the demand may be made for some gadget or like intricate electronic device that will magically cure all our educational ills overnight and painlessly remove all evidences of our past and present mistakes in educational affairs. This would indeed be a vain hope; research in education, as in the physical sciences, does not operate in this happy fashion. Progress is achieved slowly, through careful, systematic study and experiment.

A complete list of the studies actively pursued during the academic year is given below, by study number and title. The numbers refer to the cumulative key list of studies undertaken by our Department from its beginning in 1931 to date (missing numbers represent either completed or inactive studies, mainly the former). Of the studies included, the vast majority either were completed during the year or form part of major continuing studies. The policy of our Department is to complete each study as expeditiously as possible and to publish the results. In the case of studies undertaken at the request of the provincial government or of local education authorities, of course, publication of the findings is the exception, not the rule, for reasons which are obvious and need not be elaborated upon here; it means, though, that much of our time and effort does not receive the usual academic recognition through publication.

Considerations of space restrict discussion of progress and findings to only a few of the major studies. Selection of those for mention has been arbitrary and does not necessarily indicate relative significance and worth. As so frequently happens in research, some of the studies dismissed as being relatively minor in scope and significance may in the long run prove to be the most important and lead to unexpected but far-reaching implications for educational theory and practice.

The study of school grants referred to in last year's report has continued, and the third stage has been reached. The new school grant plan, announced by the provincial government in the Legislative Assembly this year, involves the distribution of more than \$130,000,000 to approximately 4,000 school boards throughout the province. Most of the statistical work entailed in the derivation of the formulae and schedules was performed in our Department. Although the major part of this work will be completed during the coming academic year, it seems likely that we will be asked to retain partial responsibility for a continuing evaluation of the school grant plans and for the preparation of the data required for any adjustments that prove necessary and desirable. The load promises to be lighter in the future, but during the past year a very substantial part of our time and effort was devoted to this study alone.

It was with considerable pride and satisfaction that we decided to launch the publication of the *Ontario Journal of Educational Research*, a long-cherished dream of ours. Research in education in Ontario has been severely handicapped by the complete lack of any avenue for publication in this province or in Canada, except indirectly through the journals of education and of research in the United States and

---

England. Our plans call for a modest beginning of not more than two issues per year, and the initial issue (October, 1958) is devoted entirely to a survey of educational research activities in Canada for the period 1953 to 1956. Our hope is that this venture will be successful and help to meet a long-felt need.

The Atkinson Study of Utilization of Student Resources has progressed with marked success and has now reached the completion of the follow-up stage as originally planned. Naturally, not all the data have been collected and processed, but the final information will be secured once the records of universities and other institutions are completed for the past year and made available to us. To date, four reports of the Study have been published, in addition to several articles and lectures. Without exception, interest in the Study has been keen and widespread, and expressions of appreciation and support have been received from other provinces and countries, as far afield as our sister dominions of Australia and New Zealand. The crucial stage of the Study—prediction of success beyond the high school—has now been reached, and the investigations at present being pressed concern prediction of success in university, nursing schools, teachers' colleges, and in employment. It is confidently anticipated that reports of the major findings of these particular studies will be issued during the coming year.

The major extension of the Atkinson Study mentioned briefly last year has been undertaken, supported by a further generous grant of \$50,000 from the Atkinson Charitable Foundation. (Their grant of \$35,000 launched the original study.) The extension is concerned mainly with a longer and more thorough follow-up of the original group of students, in particular through all forms of further education and several years of employment in a profession or in industry and commerce. It was indeed gratifying to secure further support for the Study, sufficient in amount and purpose to enable us to extract the maximum possible amount of information and knowledge from the data and experience so laboriously secured and gained. When both the original and the extended Atkinson Studies have been completed, few if any, investigations in any part of the world will be able to match them in scope, thoroughness, or implications for the future of education in this and other countries.

Plans have now been prepared for beginning the much larger study of utilization of talent in high school and college which was, in part, the broader issue underlying both the Atkinson Study and the Headmasters' Association Study referred to in last year's report. The information we have secured very definitely indicates that the greatest loss of human resources in Ontario occurs during the secondary school course. A tragically high proportion of our ablest youth seem to be leaving school long before they reach a level of attainment commensurate with their innate and developed ability. The loss to themselves and to our country is grievous, and one that cannot long be tolerated with safety in these days of crisis, international competition, and shortage of trained and disciplined minds. Briefly, the new study proposes to test every Grade 9 student in the province of Ontario (possibly 90,000 altogether) and to follow them through their educational careers and out into the world of employment. That this is a tremendous undertaking is clearly realized, but the urgency of the situation demands that an all-out effort be made. As stated, our plans have been completed, and an outline of them together with a request for a very substantial measure of financial support has been submitted to a philanthropic foundation in the United States. We hope that the response will be favourable and generous, and that organizations within this province will, as they have already promised, support our endeavours when the proposed study is finally launched.

Construction and standardization of mental tests for use in schools have been continued and extended. Preparation of quick-scoring editions of three Dominion tests has been completed. The device we have developed for scoring utilizes one of the newer plastics, with answers printed on a transparent stencil for hand-scoring. Experiments have indicated that hand-scoring using this plastic key—which permits scanning and scoring to be done as a single operation—may be almost as satisfactory, in terms of both speed and cost, as the usual IBM machine-scoring using separate answer-sheets. Our investigations along these lines are continuing, and we hope

---

eventually to mechanize almost wholly the scoring of the objective-type tests published by our Department. The most promising lead is in the direction of complete mechanization, with responses recorded directly on special IBM cards by the students tested, through either "mark-sense" IBM cards or a form of portable punch using partially perforated IBM cards. In fact, the only reasonable solution to the problem posed by the volume of tests to be scored and the level of accuracy desired in scoring and reporting is some form of completely mechanized scoring, checking, and recording of scores.

Much of our attention in test construction was focused during the year on the preparation and use of experimental forms of tests for the Department of Education at the Grade 13 and the Grade 12 level. Final results are now being secured and interpreted for the Grade 13 examination experiment conducted last year, and consideration is being given to the introduction on an experimental basis of some form of objective tests as a part of the departmental examination system at that level. During the year under review, while awaiting the final results of the earlier experiment, the experimental forms of three objective-type achievement tests at the Grade 12 level were prepared and administered to a sample of approximately 3,000 Grade 12 students throughout the province. The tests were scored and the results forwarded to the schools, together with specially prepared norms, within two or three weeks of the date of receipt of the test papers. The experimental tests are now being thoroughly studied, item by item and part by part, in order to determine how useful, valid, and reliable such tests are under the circumstances prevailing in this province. Tests of developed scholastic aptitude, mechanics of expression in English, and knowledge of chemistry were tried last year; the hope is that similar tests for other subject areas may be gradually developed, a few at a time each year, and made available for use generally. There seems to be fairly general agreement in regard to the need for such tests at the Grade 12 level in Ontario and for the establishment of provincial standards of performance in the key subjects (at least in those aspects which can be measured adequately by objective-type tests).

A list of the current activities of the Department of Educational Research follows:

Graduate students completed the following theses for the degrees indicated:

*For the M.Ed. degree:*

LAMB, R. L. Legal liability of school boards and teachers for school accidents.

SAVAGE, H. W. A study of the factors affecting Ontario secondary school mathematics specialist teachers' knowledge of educational research.

## *Faculty of Forestry*

*Reported by Dean J. W. B. Sisam*

The research activities of the Faculty of Forestry are being carried on partly at the Glendon Hall Laboratory and partly at the University Forest.

At Glendon Hall Professor K. A. Armson continued his work on the mineral nutrition of trees. His material is obtained from tree nurseries and plantations throughout Ontario. Under his direction graduate students were engaged in studies on root development of red pine in relation to various soil factors and the nature of root grafts in red pine plantations.

Dr. J. L. Farrar continued his investigation of patterns in the height growth of Norway spruce. Work is also continuing on the thermodynamic potential of water in tree roots, the response of white pine to release from shade, and the response of a number of species to photoperiod. A start has been made on the establishment of an arboretum on the land of the David Dunlap Observatory at Richmond Hill. Under Dr. Farrar's direction, graduate students have carried out a study of root pruning in connection with the transplanting of tree nursery stock and a study of exposure damage to Norway spruce.

Professor A. D. Hall is making a histological study of the barks of commercially important trees of Ontario. Identification features will be determined and a key prepared to assist in microscopic and macroscopic separation of these species on the basis of bark characteristics.

Under the direction of Professor D. V. Love, graduate students are conducting

---

studies in the field of forest economics. One such investigation has to do with financial considerations associated with tree and timber-stand growth and the time of cutting with special reference to hard maple and yellow birch on the University Forest. Another investigation deals with the economic aspects of growing, harvesting, and marketing of red and white pine in the Pembroke area of Ontario. Both these studies are being carried on with the support and co-operation of the Ontario Department of Lands and Forests.

At the University Forest, investigations are under way by Professor A. D. Hall on the effect of spacing on the development of red pine plantations and by Professor A. S. Michell on methods of utilizing sawmill residues for the manufacture of wood pulp and the use of small gasoline-powered tractors in skidding pulpwood.

## *Faculty of Music*

*Reported by Dean Boyd Neel*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Professor A. R. Johnston was commissioned by the National Museum of Human History, the Canada Foundation and the Saskatchewan Arts Board to conduct preliminary researches in Saskatchewan on folk music and folklore of that province.

Graduate students have completed the following theses:

*For the Master of Music degree:*

GARLICK, ANTONY. Costanzo Antegnati Insigne.

GLICK, SRUL IRVING. Sinfonietta.

*For the Doctor of Music degree:*

DOLIN, SAMUEL JOSEPH. Symphony No. 2.

## *Faculty of Dentistry*

*Reported by Dean R. G. Ellis*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Dr. D. L. Anderson is conducting a histochemical investigation of the changes with age in intercellular connective tissue in rats.

Dr. R. C. Burgess (with Miss G. Schacter, Drs. Gordon Nikiforuk and F. Compton) has started a study designed to evaluate the effectiveness of topical application of stannous fluoride in the prevention of dental caries in pre-school children. The study is being conducted at Riverdale School in conjunction with the Department of Health, City of Toronto.

Dr. R. M. Grainger (with Dr. M. E. Jarrett) has commenced a study to develop a mass field caries-activity test. With Drs. K. J. Paynter and J. H. Shaw, he is investigating morphological differences in the teeth between a caries-susceptible and a caries-resistant strain of rats.

Dr. H. A. Hunter (with Dr. E. M. Madlener) has completed a histological investigation of micro-organisms in experimental "fusospirochetal" lesions in guinea pigs.

Dr. A. M. Hunt is initiating a programme of research utilizing radioactive isotopes. The immediate work employs autoradiographic techniques to determine the presence of thorium in autopsy material from humans who had been injected with this radioactive element.

Dr. W. J. Linghorne is engaged in a long-term investigation of the mechanism of repair of the supporting structures of teeth.

Dr. Gordon Nikiforuk (with Dr. R. C. Burgess and Mrs. C. M. Maclaren) is studying the carbohydrate and amino sugar components of dental tissues using chro-

---

matography techniques. With Dr. Donald Fraser he has studied dental manifestations of hypophosphatasia and vitamin D refractory rickets in children at the Hospital for Sick Children.

Dr. K. J. Paynter (with Mr. G. Pudy) is studying the structure, chemical nature and development of cementum in the rat. With Dr. M. Roberts, he has investigated the reattachment of periodontal fibres to cementum following surgical trauma.

The Burlington Orthodontic Research Study is continuing its sixth year of collecting and analysing data relating to malocclusions in children. A second in a series of progress reports on this study has been completed during the past year by the research staff at Burlington.

Graduate students have completed the following theses:

*For the M.Sc.D. degree:*

ANDERSON, D. L. Histochemistry of connective tissue interfibrillar substances.

## Faculty of Pharmacy

*Reported by Dean F. N. Hughes*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Professor R. M. Baxter and Dr. S. Kandel have continued the phytochemical investigation of *Acorus calamus* L., attempting to isolate the sedative-potentiating principle. They have also initiated studies on the biosynthesis of lysergic acid and the ergot alkaloids.

Professor H. J. Fuller conducted a statistical Canadian prescription survey, including some 43,000 prescriptions, in addition to the annual survey of retail pharmacy operations.

Dr. Kandel has continued his studies on the chemical structure of glutamyl bonds in proteins.

Professor D. R. Kennedy has been investigating sustained release of medication from granulations.

Professor G. R. Paterson has continued studies on the cardiotonic principle of *Ornithogalum umbellatum*. He has also initiated investigations of: (i) the Liliaceae in Canada for medicinal activity, (ii) stability of ophthalmic solutions of physostigmine, (iii) complexes of procaine with pharmacologically inert barbiturates, (iv) extraction of glycoside from *Verbena stricta* by ultrasonics. He has continued his historical studies, compiling biographical sketches of presidents of the Ontario College of Pharmacy and the Canadian Pharmaceutical Association.

Professor G. C. Walker continued the study of pigments formed by *Tricophyton rubrum*, and the stability of ophthalmic solutions of pilocarpine hydrochloride.

Graduate students have completed the following theses:

*For the degree of M.Sc.PhM.:*

BROWN, L. R. Pilocarpine hydrochloride ophthalmic solutions.

DANDIYA, P. C. Phytochemical study of *Acorus calamus* L.

LUCAS, D. M. Isolation and characterization of the sterols of *C. purpurea* grown in artificial culture.

## School of Architecture

*Reported by Professor Milton S. Osborne*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Professor E. R. Arthur has continued his research into the architecture of nineteenth-century Toronto, and has commenced research into the architecture of nineteenth-century Hamilton.

---

Professor Gerald A. P. Carrothers continued with research on methods of forecasting the population of regions and local areas.

Professor G. Stephenson acted as a consultant for the Educational Survey of the University of Pennsylvania. He prepared a report on the Department of City Planning and the Institute for Urban Studies.

### School of Nursing

*Reported by Professor N. D. Fidler*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Professor H. M. Carpenter, in co-operation with the Director and Public Health Nursing staff of the East York Leaside Health Unit, is conducting a study of home visits to newborn infants.

Miss M. Allemang is making an analysis of the experiences of hospitalized cardiac patients with a view to assessing their needs for nursing care.

### School of Hygiene

*Reported by Dr. A. J. Rhodes*

#### DEPARTMENT OF EPIDEMIOLOGY AND BIOMETRICS

Dr. N. E. McKinnon reports that members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Investigation has been limited by the lack of staff, both academic and technical. However, necessarily incomplete analyses suggest that the recorded increase in pulmonary cancer in Canada, in the past two or three decades, is, for the most part, real and not generally attributable to a shift in diagnosis. Further analyses still fail to substantiate the view, widely held, that cancer mortality can be greatly reduced by early treatment. A "cohort" survey, similarly incomplete, shows an interesting departure from the heretofore established pattern of mortality; it reveals that in the females in England and Wales the lowest mortality rate, which used to occur between the ages of ten and fifteen, now occurs later. This change in pattern is not yet apparent in the males in England and Wales, or in either sex in the United States or Canada. It calls for explanation.

#### DEPARTMENT OF HOSPITAL ADMINISTRATION

Dr. G. Harvey Agnew reports that members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

The Case Study Project sponsored by the W. K. Kellogg Foundation under the direction of Mr. Harold Dillon was continued during 1957. The first edition of the manual *Practical Studies in Education for Hospital Administration* was published, and these studies have been used for teaching. Copies of the manual have been distributed for use to directors of all accredited courses in Hospital Administration in Canada and the United States. This project provides for evaluation and revision of the manual, and will continue over a two-year period.

---

DEPARTMENT OF MICROBIOLOGY

Dr. A. J. Rhodes reports that members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Dr. Rhodes, in collaboration with Dr. C. R. Robinson and Miss Frances W. Doane, has continued investigations of the causal agents of outbreaks of presumed virus infections. Particular attention was given to a short-term febrile illness (apparently hitherto undescribed), with exanthem and herpangina-like ulceration of the throat, that occurred in a Toronto suburb. The causal agent appears to be a member of the Coxsackie Group A family not before isolated in North America. This study is supported by the National Health Grants programme of the Ontario and Canadian governments.

The continuation of this grant, the award of another grant from the same source, and the receipt of funds from the Advisory Committee on Scientific Research of the University have enabled very substantial progress to be made in providing facilities for virus research in the School of Hygiene. These facilities will shortly justify the Department in enrolling graduate students for course work and research in this branch of microbiology.

Dr. F. O. Wishart and Mr. P. Jacobs have investigated certain aspects of the antigenic structure of *Pasteurella tularensis*.

DEPARTMENT OF PARASITOLOGY

Dr. A. M. Fallis reports that members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Dr. Fallis and Dr. R. S. Freeman, at the Ontario Research Foundation, continued their investigations of parasitic blood protozoa in various birds and of tapeworms occurring in carnivorous animals. New intermediate hosts were discovered for some of the parasitic protozoa, adding to our understanding of parasites related to those causing malaria in man. Data on the life histories and relationships among the tapeworms were obtained. Mr. N. Kingston continued his researches on the life histories of two species of trematodes.

DEPARTMENT OF PHYSIOLOGICAL HYGIENE

Dr. C. G. Hunter reports that members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Dr. Hunter and Dr. D. G. Baker continued their research into the effects of acute radiation injury on gastro-intestinal function, and the external metabolism of body constituents of mammals. The effects of changes in the radiation factors have also been studied. Financial support for this project has been received from the Defence Research Board.

Miss Helen Christie and Dr. A. M. Fisher have continued studies relating to pulmonary changes in Shaver's Disease. This condition has been observed in workmen employed in industries in which metallic fumes are discharged into the work area. Rats and hamsters have been exposed to various metallic fumes and dusts in an endeavour to determine the causative agents, the concentrations which cause pathological changes, and the effect of exposure of animals to fresh air following prolonged exposure to polluted atmosphere. Dr. R. J. MacKay has conducted pathological examinations. The project has had financial assistance from the National Health Grants programme of the Ontario and Canadian governments.

Mr. L. Hodges and Dr. Fisher are investigating the effect of exposure of laboratory animals and birds to metallic dusts, with the object of contributing to information on the incidence of sinus and lung cancer in some industrial operations. Dr. J. P. W. Gilman of the Ontario Veterinary College has co-operated by investigating the effect of topical application of the dusts. The project has received financial support from industry.

The pollution of urban atmosphere is a matter of public concern. Measurement of dustfall in the Toronto area has been continued by Dr. Fisher with the technical assistance of Mrs. H. Rahlenbeck. Dustfall varies from about five tons per square

---

mile per month in the suburban areas to about fifty tons per month in other areas of the city. An investigation of various types of dustfall equipment has been conducted in an endeavour to facilitate comparison between cities, and to estimate the effect of control measures. The work has been aided by a grant from Metropolitan Toronto.

Dr. J. E. Goodwin, with the assistance of Miss Norda Cohen, Mr. Robert Dewar and Mr. Harry Silverman, has continued his researches on simulated underwater listening, studying various physiological factors involved in the listening tasks of Naval Sonar operators. The work is supported by the Defence Research Board.

#### DEPARTMENT OF PUBLIC HEALTH

Dr. M. H. Brown reports that members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Dr. Kenneth F. Clute and Dr. John B. Firstbrook are continuing their study of general medical practice in Canada. This is a joint undertaking of the University of Toronto and the College of General Practice of Canada.

Dr. William Mosley is conducting an analysis of home visits made by public health nurses to families served by the East York-Leaside Health Unit. This study is a co-operative effort with the School of Nursing.

Dr. John E. F. Hastings has completed a socio-medical study of a hundred paraplegic patients under the treatment and rehabilitation programme of the Ontario Workmen's Compensation Board. At the present time he is involved in a socio-medical study of disability and its effects on a hundred arm-amputees under the Ontario Workmen's Compensation Board.

Dr. W. Harding le Riche has made an investigation into changing trends in the teaching of preventive medicine, extensive studies on the epidemiology of diseases and costs in a large prepaid medical care plan, an investigation on job analysis in specialist and general practitioner practice, and a small clinical study on serum cholesterol in adult males. Consultative work was provided for Physicians' Services Inc., and for the Home Care Project of the Toronto Department of Health.

A graduate student has completed the following thesis for the M.A. degree:

BELCOURT, R. J.-P. Antigenicity tests as applied to inactivated poliomyelitis viruses.

#### DEPARTMENT OF PUBLIC HEALTH NUTRITION

Professor E. W. McHenry reports that he and Miss M. J. Veen, in co-operation with Dr. Haslett of the Department of Medicine, have begun a study on cholesterol metabolism in diabetic patients. Miss Veen, with the assistance of Miss Ruth Barnes, has continued an investigation of a substance secreted from tumour cells which alters liver metabolism.

Professor McHenry, Miss Veen, and Miss Barnes are engaged, in co-operation with Dr. Charles Farquharson of the Scarborough Health Unit, in a study of food habits of industrial workers and of their heights and weights.

Professor G. H. Beaton and Miss Doris Curry have continued investigations on metabolic adaptation in pregnancy and on the metabolic effects of growth hormone.

### *Institute of Business Administration*

*Reported by Professor T. C. Graham*

Members of the staff have undertaken the following research work:

Professor W. A. Beckett has undertaken research in three areas: problems of seasonality in Canadian business; problems relating to business forecasting in Canada; and the application of electronic computers to economic analysis.

Professor W. B. Coutts is continuing his studies of the application of federal income tax to the petroleum industry.

Dr. O. W. Main is continuing his research on consumer credit in Canada.

Professor J. V. Poapst is preparing a study of the residential mortgage market

---

for the Central Mortgage and Housing Corporation. He has continued his study of the financing of corporations in Canada.

Dr. J. C. Sawatsky has completed his research on the role of governmental and private agencies in the employment process of immigrants in Metropolitan Toronto.

### *Institute of Child Study*

*Reported by Professor W. E. Blatz*

Students have completed the following theses for the Diploma in Child Study:

BARKER, F. Factors relating to over-achievement and under-achievement in a grade six classroom.

BOCKNEK, G. and GOLDEN, R. A comparison of manual co-ordination ability and sociometric patterns of a group of "normal" nursery school, kindergarten, and grade one children.

DOTSCH, D. Parental attitudes and their change.

FRIEDBERG, P. and MEREDITH, W. Manual co-ordination and personality adjustment in young children.

GOAD, M. and SISSONS, H. A sociometric study using the draw-a-group test.

HU, M. A study relating children's adjustment on the entrance records to their acceptance and refusal of dependence and independence on the pre-school mental health form in October of the entrance year.

KENDALL, C. Studies of the mental health of the pre-school child: A comparison of acceptance and refusal of dependence and independence at home and at school.

MCLEAN, M. A comparison of accelerated and non-accelerated pupils in grade six according to age, I.Q., academic achievement, sociometric status, personal and social adjustment.

SUTHERLAND, J. A study of the mental health of infants in a period of prolonged stress.

### *Connaught Medical Research Laboratories*

*Reported by Professor J. K. W. Ferguson*

Research in the Connaught Medical Research Laboratories has been financed to the extent of about \$350,000 from the proceeds of the sale of products and to the extent of about \$120,000 from grants, gifts, and bequests. In the list that follows the outside granting agency, if any, is shown in brackets.

Members of the Connaught Medical Research Laboratories have undertaken the following researches:

Dr. C. W. J. Armstrong and Mr. F. C. Potter: Measurement of potency of poliomyelitis vaccine.

Drs. A. F. Charles and R. H. Painter: Derivatives of plasma proteins.

## REPORT OF THE REGISTRAR

I beg to submit the following statistics for the year ended June 30, 1958:

1. I. Distribution of the staff of the University and University College (furnished by the Chief Accountant).
2. II. Distribution of the staffs of the federated Arts Colleges (furnished by the registrars of the Colleges).
3. III. Registration of students by faculties and years as at December 1, 1957.
4. IV. Enrolment in the Arts Colleges (furnished by the registrars of the Colleges).
5. V. Enrolment in the University departments in Arts (furnished by the departments).
6. VI. Registration in courses in the Faculty of Arts.
7. VII. Registration in courses in the School of Graduate Studies (furnished by the Secretary of the School).
8. VIII. Results of annual examinations (furnished by the secretaries of the faculties and schools).
9. IX. Admission to degrees.
10. X. Admission to diplomas and certificates.
11. XI. Geographical distribution of students (furnished by the secretaries of the faculties and schools).

J. C. EVANS

---
